O(1)
ARCHITECTURE: The PN-Vector is part of SuperInstance's CRDT collection alongside G-Counter, G-Set, LWW-Register, and OR-Set. It powers bidirectional counters in distributed voting, inventory tracking, and engagement metrics across edge nodes. The conservation invariant γ + η = C connects here: γ (increments) and η (decrements) are both monotonically non-decreasing, and their difference C = γ − η is the observable counter value. The CRDT merge preserves this invariant because it takes the max of each component independently. MATH: $$\text{fifteens} = 2 \times |\{ S \subseteq \text{cards} : \sum_{c \in S} v(c) = 15 \}|$$ | **Complexity:** O(2ⁿ) where n = 5 (number of cards). With n fixed at 5, this is a constant 31 iterations. For a general n-card hand: O(2ⁿ). | $$\text{pairs} = 2 \times |\{ \{c_i, c_j\} : i < j,\ \text{rank}(c_i) = \text{rank}(c_j) \}|$$ | **Complexity:** O(n²) pairwise comparisons. | **Complexity:** O(n log n) for sorting, then O(n) for sequence scan.
ARCHITECTURE: The scorer implements the **γ + η = C** principle: - **γ (gamma)**: The scoring rules of cribbage — a 400-year-old specification defined by the American Cribbage Congress rulebook. Each scoring category (fifteens, pairs, runs, flush, knobs) is a mathematical function over card sets. - **η (eta)**: The Rust implementation — bitmask enumeration for fifteens, pairwise comparison for pairs, sort-and-scan for runs, suit comparison for flush/knobs. Each algorithm is a concrete realization of the corresponding rule.

ARCHITECTURE: CudaClaw provides the **GPU-accelerated agent dispatch layer** for the SuperInstance fleet. Within γ + η = C, it parallelizes the γ-layer computation: thousands of ternary agents ({-1, 0, +1}) are dispatched across GPU warps, each evaluating conservation-law constraints simultaneously. The persistent kernel model ensures that the GPU is always ready to process conservation violations in real-time. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

ARCHITECTURE: Deckboss Net provides the **reliable transport layer** for γ + η = C conservation in maritime deployments. When fleet nodes lose connectivity, conservation-law observations are queued with TTL guarantees — high-priority conservation violations propagate immediately when connectivity returns, while routine telemetry uses gentler backoff to avoid network flooding during the recovery burst. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

Myers' algorithm runs in O((N+M)×D) time where D is the edit distance — so when the texts are similar  | - **Fast for similar texts** — O(ND) where D = edit distance (small for typical code changes) | **The k-diagonal trick:** Myers observes that all vertices reachable with cost D on diagonal k (where k = x − y) can be computed from the cost D−1 diagonals. DEFINITION: **Edge Benchmark** is a Rust library providing benchmarking primitives designed for edge runtimes (Cloudflare Workers compatible) — supporting `no_std` environments with sub-millisecond timing, statistical analysis, Welch's t-test for significance testing, and Markdown/JSON report generation.

MATH: | Mean | Σxᵢ/n | O(n) | | | Median | Middle element(s) of sorted | O(1) | | | P95, P99 | Percentile index = (pct/100)×(n−1) | O(1) from sorted | | | StdDev | √(Σ(xᵢ−μ)²/n) | O(n) | | | Min, Max | First/last of sorted | O(1) |
ARCHITECTURE: Edge Benchmark provides the **edge-runtime performance monitoring** for γ + η = C. When conservation-law verification runs on Cloudflare Workers (η-layer), Edge Benchmark ensures it meets its latency budget. A regression that pushes conservation verification over the CPU time limit (50ms on Workers) would violate the real-time conservation contract. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md). **Warm-up rationale in edge contexts:** On Cloudflare Workers, the V8 isolate's JIT compiler reaches peak performance after ~100 iterations of a function. Running fewer than 100 warm-up iterations measures JIT-warmup overhead rather than steady-state performance. The `run_with_warmup` function addresses this by executing the closure 10–100 times before measurement, allowing the JIT to optimize the hot paths.

MATH: Edge Conservation Worker is the **edge-verification node** for γ + η = C. It tests that conservation invariants hold in the η-layer's most resource-constrained environment — the Cloudflare edge. By ru
ARCHITECTURE: Edge Conservation Worker is the **edge-verification node** for γ + η = C. It tests that conservation invariants hold in the η-layer's most resource-constrained environment — the Cloudflare edge. By running identical computations at 300+ locations and comparing results, it ensures that conservation-law violations are not artifacts of specific hardware or runtime environments. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md). **Edge colo as a variable:** Cloudflare's 300+ edge locations run diverse hardware: some colos use Intel Xeon, others use ARM-based Ampere CPUs. Floating-point results can differ slightly across architectures due to FPU implementation differences (IEEE 754 permits variant results for transcendental operations). The conservation Worker's delta measurements capture this hardware variance — a non-zero delta on `/entropy` or `/matrix` at a specific colo indicates architecture-dependent FP deviation, which must be accounted for when setting conservation violation thresholds.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: entropy-flow-rs
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, fleet, compute, protocol
DEFINITION: **Entropy Flow (Rust)** is a Rust library implementing information-theoretic measures — Shannon entropy, KL divergence, Jensen-Shannon divergence, and mutual information — for real-time conservation-law monitoring in the SuperInstance fleet.
SECTIONS: Entropy Flow (Rust) | Why It Matters | How It Works | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References | License
MATH: Measures the reduction in uncertainty about X when Y is known. In the fleet, this quantifies how much the γ-layer (observed actions) tells us about the η-layer (internal model state). | | Shannon entropy | O(n) | O(k) for k bins | | | KL divergence | O(n) | O(k) | | | JS divergence | O(n) | O(k) | | | Mutual information | O(n × bins²) | O(bins²) |
ARCHITECTURE: Entropy Flow (Rust) provides the **real-time entropy monitoring** for γ + η = C. It runs in the η-layer, continuously computing entropy of the γ-layer's action stream. When entropy deviates beyond conservation tolerance, the fleet's conservation-law verification triggers. The Rust implementation ensures sub-millisecond computation for fleet-scale monitoring. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md). **Numerical stability:** Computing KL divergence requires evaluating pᵢ × log(pᵢ/qᵢ). When qᵢ is very small (near zero) but pᵢ is non-zero, the ratio pᵢ/qᵢ explodes, causing floating-point overflow. This implementation uses the numerically stable identity: p × log(p/q) = p × log(p) − p × log(q), with the convention 0 × log(0) = 0 (by L'Hôpital's rule, lim_{x→0+} x log x = 0). This avoids division and handles zero-probability events gracefully.

DEFINITION: **epoll** is Linux's scalable I/O event notification mechanism — a system call API that lets a single thread monitor thousands of file descriptors for readiness events (readable, writable, error, hangup). SECTIONS: Epoll Wrapper — Safe Rust Bindings for Linux I/O Multiplexing | Why It Matters | How It Works | Epoll Lifecycle | Event Types | Level-Triggered vs. Edge-Triggered | Performance Model | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References | License
MATH: Every high-performance network server on Linux uses epoll (or a higher-level abstraction built on it). nginx, Redis, HAProxy, Envoy, and Tokio's reactor all rely on epoll for their event loop. Without | - `epoll_ctl(ADD/MOD/DEL)`: `O(1)` — hash table insert/modify/delete in kernel. | - `epoll_wait`: `O(ready_events)` — only returns descriptors with events, not all registered FDs. | - Memory: `O(registered_fds)` kernel-side interest list. | | `Epoll::new()` | Create epoll instance (`epoll_create1`). `O(1)`. |
ARCHITECTURE: Epoll is the I/O substrate for the γ (generation/I/O) side of γ + η = C in SuperInstance. It powers the event loops that handle fleet instance networking, enabling high-concurrency I/O without thread-per-connection overhead. See [SuperInstance Architecture](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

ARCHITECTURE: In the SuperInstance ecosystem, this crate provides the service discovery plane. Nodes register with Eureka on boot, discover peers through the registry, and use heartbeats as a liveness signal feeding into the γ + η = C consensus layer. Eureka's eventual-consistency model complements etcd's strong consistency: discovery uses Eureka for speed, while coordination falls back to etcd for correctness. documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: event-bus
CONCEPTS: conservation, fleet, crypto, math, systems, search
DEFINITION: **event-bus** is a lightweight, in-process publish/subscribe messaging system for Rust. It allows decoupled components to communicate through named topics without direct references to each other, using thread-safe handler registration and synchronous dispatch.
SECTIONS: Event Bus | Why It Matters | How It Works | Quick Start | API | `EventBus` | Handler Requirements | Architecture Notes | References | License
MATH: **Subscription** (`subscribe`) acquires a mutex lock on the map, inserts the handler into the topic's vector, and releases the lock. This is O(1) amortized. | **Publication** (`publish`) acquires the mutex lock, iterates over the topic's handlers, and calls each one synchronously. Dispatch is O(H) where H = number of handlers for that topic. | | `new()` | `→ EventBus` | O(1) | | | `subscribe(topic, handler)` | where `handler: Fn(&str) + Send + Sync + 'static` | O(1) amortized | | | `publish(topic, message)` | Calls all handlers for topic | O(H) |
ARCHITECTURE: This crate provides the in-process event routing for SuperInstance's component communication. The bus serves as the η (eta) layer's notification mechanism: when the γ (gamma) layer commits a state change, the bus distributes the update to all subscribed components, maintaining the γ + η = C invariant. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md) for system-wide design.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: expr-bytecode
CONCEPTS: fleet, graph, crypto, systems, search
DEFINITION: **expr-bytecode** is a stack-based bytecode compiler and virtual machine for arithmetic expressions. It compiles expression ASTs into a compact opcode sequence and executes them on a stack machine, demonstrating the same compilation pipeline used by languages like Lua, Python (CPython), and the JVM.
SECTIONS: Expression Bytecode Compiler & VM | Why It Matters | How It Works | Compilation: AST → Bytecode | Execution: Stack VM | Instruction Set | Comparison to Alternatives | Quick Start | API | AST Types | Compilation & Execution | Architecture Notes
MATH: Every programming language, configuration engine, and query DSL needs an expression evaluator. The stack-based bytecode approach — used by the JVM, CPython, and WebAssembly — offers two key advantages | **Complexity:** Compilation is O(N) where N = AST nodes. Each node emits exactly 1 instruction (literals) or 1 + children instructions (operators). | - `Push(v)` — Stack push: O(1) | - `Add/Sub/Mul/Div` — Pop 2, push result: O(1) | - `Neg` — Pop 1, push negation: O(1)
ARCHITECTURE: This crate demonstrates the compilation pipeline used within SuperInstance's rule engine. Expressions compile to bytecode that can be cached, serialized, and executed repeatedly without AST interpretation overhead. The VM's deterministic dispatch feeds into the γ + η = C model: compiled rules execute predictably within the gamma layer's decision engine. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md) for the full system design.

documents.name: fastloop-guard
documents.description:
CRATE: fastloop-guard
CONCEPTS: conservation, fleet, crypto, storage, search
DEFINITION: `fastloop-guard` is a high-performance semantic cache daemon for LLM query responses. It uses a **three-gate lookup** — exact BLAKE2b fingerprint → fuzzy MinHash similarity → miss — to dramatically reduce redundant LLM API calls. Built as a Unix socket server with async Tokio runtime, it provides sub-millisecond cache lookups with configurable TTL and LRU eviction.
SECTIONS: FastLoop Guard — Semantic Cache G

documents.description:
CRATE: federated-ternary
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, fleet, graph, compute, math, search
DEFINITION: **Federated learning over ternary weight spaces** — multiple nodes train {-1, 0, +1} weight vectors locally, then merge via element-wise majority vote. The merge operation is commutative, associative, and idempotent (CRDT properties), making it Byzantine-tolerant without coordination.
SECTIONS: federated-ternary | Why It Matters | How It Works | Local Training | Majority Merge (CRDT) | CRDT

DEFINITION: **Federated learning over ternary weight spaces** — multiple nodes train {-1, 0, +1} weight vectors locally, then merge via element-wise majority vote. The merge operation is commutative, associative, and idempotent (CRDT properties), making it Byzantine-tolerant without coordination.

MATH: FishingLog AI operates in the **γ-layer practical application** space. Within γ + η = C, it demonstrates conservation principles in resource management: the quota tracking system is a direct instance 
ARCHITECTURE: FishingLog AI operates in the **γ-layer practical application** space. Within γ + η = C, it demonstrates conservation principles in resource management: the quota tracking system is a direct instance of conservation-law enforcement (catch cannot exceed allocation), mirroring how the fleet's conservation matrix tracks avoidance-ratio invariants. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md). **Offline-first architecture:** The entire app is served as a single HTML payload with inline JavaScript and CSS — no external dependencies, no CDN calls, no font loading. State persists via `localStorage` with a schema version tag for migrations. When connectivity returns, a `navigator.onLine` event listener triggers a sync queue that uploads catch entries as JSON batches to a KV namespace. Conflict resolution is last-write-wins keyed by timestamp + vessel ID, which is safe because each vessel is the sole writer of its own data.

ARCHITECTURE: Fleet Bottle provides the **inter-agent communication protocol** for γ + η = C. Every conservation-law observation flows through bottles: γ-layer agents send their action distributions as bottles to η-layer analysis agents, which respond with conservation verdicts. The priority system ensures that conservation violations (Critical priority) preempt all other fleet communication. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: fleet-conductor
CONCEPTS: conservation, fleet, graph, compute, protocol, systems
DEFINITION: **fleet-conductor** is a Rust library that orchestrates distributed agent fleets — coordinating the lifecycle (spawn, health-check, scale, terminate) of agents running across heterogeneous nodes. It provides the conductor primitive that sits above `construct` (topology definition) and below the fleet management UI, issuing commands like "deploy 10 inference agents to node-A" or "drain agent-7 for maintenance" while respecting conservation constraints.
SECTIONS: fleet-conductor — Distributed Agent Fleet Orchestration | Why It Matters | How It Works | Desired-State Reconciliation | Agent Lifecycle Management | Conservation-Aware Scheduling | Load Balancing & Circuit Breaking | Complexity | Quick Start | Build | Test | API
MATH: Running a fleet of AI agents across multiple machines is an operations problem with real consequences: agents hold conversational state, have active sessions with users, and must be migrated without d | State transitions are guarded by predicates — e.g., Healthy → Draining requires the conservation check (does removing this agent destabilize γ + η = C?). | $$C_{\text{current}} = \gamma + \eta$$ | | Reconciliation cycle | $O(N + E)$ where $N$ = agents, $E$ = edges | | | Agent scheduling decision | $O(k)$ where $k$ = candidate nodes |
ARCHITECTURE: fleet-conductor is the **control plane** of the SuperInstance fleet. It consumes topology definitions from `construct`, runs coordination experiments from `construct-coordination` to validate configuration changes before deployment, and enforces the γ + η = C conservation invariant at the fleet level. The conductor is the only component authorized to spawn or terminate agents — this single-writer design prevents race conditions where multiple controllers create conflicting agent populations. In the conservation framework, the conductor's job is to keep $C$ stable while the underlying agent population evolves continuously. See: [SuperInstance Architecture](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md)
CROSS_REFS: relates-to:conservation relates-to:fleet relates-to:graph relates-to:compute relates-to:protocol relates-to:systems

CONCEPTS: fleet, graph, crypto, compute, storage, search
DEFINITION: **Centralized fleet node coordination** — registration, health monitoring, tag-based task assignment, and least-loaded load balancing. Manages a pool of nodes with capacity-based scheduling, where each task consumes resources from its assigned node until completion.
SECTIONS: fleet-coordinator | Why It Matters | How It Works | Node Registration | Task Assignment Algorithm | Load Release | Utilization Metric | Status Summary | Complexity Analysis | Quick Start | API | `FleetCoordinator`
MATH: Internally, nodes are stored in a `HashMap<String, FleetNode>` — O(1) lookup by ID. | **Complexity**: O(n) where n = number of nodes. Each node is checked against three filters. | This is a greedy online algorithm. It achieves a competitive ratio of O(1) against the optimal offline algorithm for identical machines (by the Graham's list scheduling bound): | $$\frac{C_{\text{greedy}}}{C_{\text{opt}}} \leq 2 - \frac{1}{n}$$ | $$U = \frac{\sum_{i} \text{load}_i}{\sum_{i} \text{capacity}_i} \times 100\%$$
ARCHITECTURE: The fleet coordinator implements the γ + η = C conservation link for fleet resources: - **γ** (gamma) = sum of active task loads across all nodes (consumed capacity) - **η** (eta) = sum of remaining capacity across all nodes (available capacity)

MATH: The test sends an identical prompt to three models and compares results side-by-side. The prompt is deliberately domain-specific — it asks the model to explain the conservation law **γ + η = C** as it | - **Time complexity:** O(N × T) where T is per-model latency (typically 1–10s) | - **Space complexity:** O(N × R) where R is max response size (capped at `MAX_TOKENS=300`) | The quality heuristic is O(W) per response where W is word count — negligible. | This test probes the external inference layer of the SuperInstance fleet. In the **γ + η = C** conservation law, the inference provider supplies γ (generation energy) — the raw reasoning power that dr
ARCHITECTURE: This test probes the external inference layer of the SuperInstance fleet. In the **γ + η = C** conservation law, the inference provider supplies γ (generation energy) — the raw reasoning power that drives agent responses. The test ensures that γ is available, fast, and coherent before the fleet depends on it. When integrated with fleet monitoring, this test runs as a periodic health check. A latency regression on `hermes-3-llama-3.1-405b` from 5s → 30s would trigger fleet routing changes, falling back to the cheaper `seed-2.0-mini` for non-critical paths while preserving 405B for high-stakes reasoning. See the [SuperInstance Architecture](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md) for how inference routing fits into the full fleet topology.

MATH: **Semantic search** (`fleet_search`): The query string is sent to the vector API, which embeds it using `@cf/baai/bge-small-en-v1.5` and performs cosine similarity against the 384-dimensional index. R | **Recommendation** (`fleet_recommend`): Given a known crate name, the API retrieves that crate's embedding and finds nearest neighbors — crates whose descriptions are semantically close. This is k-nea | This server is a stateless adapter in the SuperInstance fleet stack. It sits between MCP-compatible AI agents and the fleet-vector-api Cloudflare Worker. It contributes to the **γ + η = C** conservati
ARCHITECTURE: This server is a stateless adapter in the SuperInstance fleet stack. It sits between MCP-compatible AI agents and the fleet-vector-api Cloudflare Worker. It contributes to the **γ + η = C** conservation law by reducing coordination overhead (γ): agents don't need custom API integration code, shrinking the glue layer. The fleet-vector-api itself uses Cloudflare Vectorize for ANN search. See the [Architecture document](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md) for fleet topology. **Transport**: The server uses `StdioServerTransport`, reading JSON-RPC messages from stdin and writing responses to stdout. This is the standard MCP transport for local integrations — no network socket needed, no port conflicts. The server process is spawned by the client (e.g., Claude Desktop) and communicates via pipes.

Results are published to KV with a 10-minute TTL for downstream dashboards and
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: fleet-metrics-cron
CONCEPTS: fleet, crypto, compute, storage, math, search
DEFINITION: A Cloudflare Worker cron trigger that aggregates observability metrics for the SuperInstance fleet every 5 minutes. It queries D1 trace spans and event tables, computes latency percentiles (P50/P75/P90/P95/P99), error rates, throughput, budget consumption, and runs multi-rule anomaly detection against 7-day rolling baselines. Results are published to KV with a 10-minute TTL for downstream dashboards and alerting.
SECTIONS: fleet-metrics-cron | Why It Matters | How It Works | Percentile Approximation via ROW_NUMBER | Health Scoring Model | Anomaly Detection Rules | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Quick Start | Install dependencies | Local dev (requires wrangler) | Deploy to Cloudflare
MATH: where `rn = ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY duration_ms)`, `N = COUNT(*)`, and `δ` is a small band (±0.02) that averages rows near the target quantile. This is O(N log N) due to the sort inside the window | - **Per-worker metrics**: O(N log N) for percentile queries where N = span count in window | - **Anomaly detection**: O(W) where W = number of workers (15), with O(1) KV lookups for baselines | - **Total per tick**: O(W · N log N), dominated by D1 SQL execution | - KV entries: O(W) per window key (3 per worker: latency, errors, throughput) + 1 overview + anomalies
ARCHITECTURE: This worker embodies the **γ + η = C** principle: it is the **feedback controller (γ)** that closes the observability loop for the fleet. The fleet's executors (edge workers, APIs, orchestrators) are the **plant (η)**. Without this controller, the fleet operates open-loop — no detection of degradation, no early warning on budget exhaustion, no cascading failure prevention. The 5-minute cadence is the sampling period `T_s` of the discrete-time control system; the anomaly rules are the transfer function `C(z)` that maps observed metrics to corrective signals.

documents.description:
CRATE: fleet-midi

DEFINITION: **MIDI** (Musical Instrument Digital Interface, pronounced "middy") is a 40-year-old serial protocol that encodes musical events — note-ons, note-offs, control changes, pitch bends — as compact 1–3 byte messages. **Fleet MIDI** is a Rust library that parses these binary messages and broadcasts them across a constellation of agents, treating the MIDI wire format as a real-time control plane for distributed systems.

It does not execute GPU work itself; it observes the γ-layer (actual GPU streams running Flux bytecode) and 
ARCHITECTURE: This crate is a **η (eta)** module — an orchestration component in the γ + η = C framework. It does not execute GPU work itself; it observes the γ-layer (actual GPU streams running Flux bytecode) and makes control decisions. The `ScaleEvent` log provides an audit trail for understanding why scaling decisions were made, enabling post-hoc analysis of the control loop's behavior. The autoscaler implements a simple **bang-bang controller** with hysteresis (the Hold band between 0.2 and 0.8). For more sophisticated control, the thresholds can be tuned or replaced with PID-style logic.

The VM and bytecode ISA are the **γ (gamma)** — fixed, deterministic, mathematical. The vocabulary layer and A2A swarm are the **η (eta)** — the orchestratio
ARCHITECTURE: FLUX embodies the **γ + η = C** principle. The VM and bytecode ISA are the **γ (gamma)** — fixed, deterministic, mathematical. The vocabulary layer and A2A swarm are the **η (eta)** — the orchestration and coordination layer that adapts at runtime. Together they form **C** — a complete agent execution system where behavior is auditable (γ) and coordination is flexible (η). The design mirrors real CPU architectures: the register file and fetch-execute loop would be familiar to anyone who has implemented a CHIP-8 or RISC-V emulator, but extended with agent-specific opcodes (`TELL`, `ASK`, `DELEGATE`, `BROADCAST`).

ARCHITECTURE: Font rasterization is a **γ** (transform) that converts mathematical curve data (**η**, the font outline) into a visual representation (**C**, the rendered bitmap). The quality of this transform determines text legibility. The `Rasterizer` trait abstracts the transform so different backends (FreeType, harfbuzz, stb_truetype) can plug in without changing consumers. This is the γ + η = C principle applied to typography: the outline is the ground truth, the rasterizer is the lens, the bitmap is what you see.

ARCHITECTURE: The Forgemaster operates at the **silicon layer** of the SuperInstance constellation. In the conservation law **γ + η = C**, it manages γ (generation energy) at the hardware level — ensuring that every CUDA core in the fleet is doing useful work. A DGX sitting idle at 5% utilization is wasted γ; the Forgemaster's job is to drive every GPU toward its optimal operating point. The Forgemaster dispatches kernels for `ternary-cell` simulations, `fleet-vector-api` embedding generation, and any other GPU-intensive fleet workload. See the [SuperInstance Architecture](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md) for how GPU dispatch integrates with the room-as-codespace model.

DEFINITION: A **packet fragment reassembly library** that reconstructs original messages from ordered fragments arriving over unreliable channels, handling gaps, duplicates, and out-of-order delivery using offset-indexed buffering.
SECTIONS: Fragment Reassembler | Why It Matters | How It Works | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References | License
MATH: **Naive approach** — sort fragments by offset and concatenate. This fails when fragments are missing or overlapping. Time: O(N log N) for the sort. | **Complexity**: O(F + L) where F is total fragment bytes and L is reassembled length. Space: O(L) for the receive buffer. | This library provides the reliability layer for the SuperInstance message bus. When agents communicate over UDP or unreliable transports, messages exceeding the MTU are fragmented; this library ensure
ARCHITECTURE: This library provides the reliability layer for the SuperInstance message bus. When agents communicate over UDP or unreliable transports, messages exceeding the MTU are fragmented; this library ensures transparent reconstruction. It contributes to **η** (reflex) in **γ + η = C** — reassembly is automatic, requiring zero coordination overhead from the application layer. See [Architecture](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md). **Timeout and cleanup**: Fragments that never arrive (permanent gaps) must be garbage-collected. A common strategy is a timer per partial reassembly: if the timer expires before completion, the partial buffer is discarded to prevent memory leaks from malformed or malicious senders. **Security**: Fragment reassembly is the target of several well-known attacks (teardrop, overlap attacks). A robust reassembler validates that fragment offsets don't overlap maliciously and rejects fragments claiming to extend beyond the expected total length.

CONCEPTS: conservation, crypto, math, systems
DEFINITION: A Rust library for simulating and analyzing the Gauss-Markov process (also known as the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process). SECTIONS: gauss-markov | Why It Matters | How It Works | Stochastic Differential Equation | Euler-Maruyama Discretization | Stationary Distribution | Autocorrelation Function | Parameter Estimation | Stability Condition | Quick Start | API | `GaussMarkovParams`
MATH: **Time complexity**: O(N) for N = ⌈(t_end - t_start) / dt⌉ steps. | **Space complexity**: O(N) for storing the trajectory. | The OU process embodies **γ + η = C** in estimation theory. The process noise (**η**) represents unmodeled dynamics perturbing the system. The Kalman filter (**γ**) uses the OU process model to predic
ARCHITECTURE: The OU process embodies **γ + η = C** in estimation theory. The process noise (**η**) represents unmodeled dynamics perturbing the system. The Kalman filter (**γ**) uses the OU process model to predict how uncertainty evolves, producing optimal state estimates (**C**). The autocorrelation function ρ(τ) = e^{−θτ} is the transfer function: it tells the filter how much past information to retain. Longer memory (smaller θ) means more smoothing; shorter memory (larger θ) means more responsiveness. This tradeoff between noise rejection and responsiveness is the fundamental tension in feedback control.

MATH: Part of the [SuperInstance](https://github.com/SuperInstance) ecosystem. Ghost Track implements the lifecycle dimension of the conservation law γ + η = C — a ghost process consumes γ (resources) while
ARCHITECTURE: Part of the [SuperInstance](https://github.com/SuperInstance) ecosystem. Ghost Track implements the lifecycle dimension of the conservation law γ + η = C — a ghost process consumes γ (resources) while producing zero η (innovation). By reaping ghosts quickly, the fleet minimizes wasted γ and redirects resources to productive agents. The 90-second detection window (3 × 30s heartbeat) balances false-positive rate (killing healthy agents that happened to be slow) against resource waste (letting ghosts linger). See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

CRATE: gibbs-phenomenon

DEFINITION: Demonstrates and measures the **Gibbs phenomenon** — the persistent ~8.95% overshoot that Fourier series exhibit near jump discontinuities, regardless of the number of terms. Includes the **Lanczos sigma factor** mitigation.
SECTIONS: gibbs-phenomenon: Fourier Series and the Gibbs Overshoot | Why It Matters | How It Works | Square Wave Fourier Series | The Gibbs Overshoot
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: gibbs-phenomenon
CONCEPTS: conservation, wavelet, graph, crypto, compute, storage, protocol, math, systems
DEFINITION: Demonstrates and measures the **Gibbs phenomenon** — the persistent ~8.95% overshoot that Fourier series exhibit near jump discontinuities, regardless of the number of terms. Includes the **Lanczos sigma factor** mitigation.
SECTIONS: gibbs-phenomenon: Fourier Series and the Gibbs Overshoot | Why It Matters | How It Works | Square Wave Fourier Series | The Gibbs Overshoot | Measurement | Lanczos Sigma Factor | Complexity | Quick Start | API | `FourierSeries` | Free Function
MATH: $$f(x) = \frac{4A}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,...}^{N} \frac{\sin(n\pi x / L)}{n}$$ | $$\text{overshoot} = \frac{2}{\pi} \int_0^{\pi} \frac{\sin(t)}{t} dt - 1 \approx 0.08949\ldots$$ | $$\text{Si}(\pi) = \int_0^{\pi} \frac{\sin t}{t} dt \approx 1.85194$$ | **Key insight**: The overshoot does NOT decrease with more terms — only its width decreases as O(1/N). | $$\sigma_n = \frac{\sin(n\pi/N)}{n\pi/N} = \text{sinc}(n/N)$$
ARCHITECTURE: This is a **γ (gamma)** module — pure mathematics demonstrating a fundamental result in approximation theory. The Gibbs phenomenon illustrates a deep principle: **truncation in frequency space causes spatial artifacts** (Heisenberg uncertainty in signal processing). In the γ + η = C framework, this crate provides the analysis tool; an **η** layer would apply filtering strategies (Lanczos, Fejér, Jackson) and measure their effectiveness.

ARCHITECTURE: Git-Agent Codespace is the **onboarding vessel** of the Git-Agent Standard v2.0. New agents (greenhorns) fork this template to bootstrap their environment. It implements the I2I (iron-to-iron) communication protocol and integrates with fleet discovery. The template itself is a static artifact — it doesn't execute, it *contains*. In the **γ + η = C** conservation law, this template compresses onboarding overhead (γ) into a reproducible artifact (η). See [Architecture](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md). **Fleet neighbors**: The Codespace template connects to other vessels in the fleet taxonomy — `tminus-dispatcher` (temporal heartbeat), `fleet-bridge` (A2A transport), `symphony-runtime` (grammar conductor), `composite-headspace` (dual-shell mediator), and `i2i-bottle-agent` (bottle postmaster). Each vessel has a designated role in the Git-Agent Standard v2.0. **The template IS the dojo**: New agents (greenhorns) fork this template as their first action, inheriting a working environment, the fleet discovery protocol, and I2I communication tools. The Codespace itself serves as the agent's training ground.

CRATE: gossip-protocol
CONCEPTS: fleet, graph, crypto, protocol, systems
DEFINITION: A **epidemic-style information dissemination protocol** that spreads membership updates, application data, and control messages across a distributed cluster with O(log N) convergence — no central coordinator, no broadcast storms, gracefully handles partitions and node churn.
SECTIONS: Gossip Protocol | Why It Matters | How It Works | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References | License
MATH: A **epidemic-style information dissemination protocol** that spreads membership updates, application data, and control messages across a distributed cluster with O(log N) convergence — no central coor | where β is the fanout (peers per round), N is cluster size, and I is informed nodes. This logistic growth model gives convergence time of O(log N / β) rounds with probability 1 - 1/N. | - Per gossip round: O(fanout × message_size) bandwidth per node | - Convergence: O(log N) rounds with fanout=1, O(log N / fanout) with higher fanout | - Memory: O(N) per node (full member table)
ARCHITECTURE: Gossip Protocol is the communication backbone of the SuperInstance distributed layer. It orchestrates gossip-member (state), gossip-ping (liveness), gossip-suspicion (failure handling), and gossip-seed (bootstrapping). The protocol's O(log N) convergence is what enables the fleet to scale — adding nodes doesn't increase per-node coordination cost. In **γ + η = C**, gossip converts coordination from explicit (γ) to reflexive (η): information spreads automatically. See [Architecture](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

CRATE: gossip-seed

DEFINITION: A **bootstrapping library** for gossip protocols that solves the cold-start problem — how does a new node discover existing cluster members when it has no prior contact information? This library provides seed node lists, DNS-based discovery, and persistent contact caches.

O(K) attempts where K is the seed l | 1. Read peer cache (O(1) file read) | 2. If cache empty, query DNS SRV records (O(DNS_latency)) | 3. If DNS empty, fall back to static seed list (O(K)) | - Peer cache read: O(N) where N is cached peer count (typically <100)
ARCHITECTURE: Gossip Seed is the bootstrap layer of the SuperInstance gossip stack. It solves the initial coordination problem — finding the cluster. In **γ + η = C**, the seed discovery is a one-time γ cost at join time; after that, gossip protocol membership is pure η. The persistent cache minimizes even that γ cost. See [Architecture](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: gpu-annealing
CONCEPTS: conservation, fleet, graph, compute, math, systems, search
DEFINITION: A pure-Rust orchestration layer for GPU-accelerated simulated annealing. Provides cooling schedules, parallel trial management, topology-aware annealing over DAGs, and conservation-constrained optimization. The crate contains **no GPU code** — it manages the state, scheduling, and decision logic that drives a GPU kernel executing thousands of annealing trials in parallel.
SECTIONS: gpu-annealing: GPU-Accelerated Simulated Annealing Orchestration | Why It Matters | How It Works | The Metropolis Criterion | Cooling Schedules | Parallel Annealing | Topology-Aware Annealing | Conservation-Constrained Annealing | Reheating | Complexity Summary | Quick Start | API
MATH: $$P(\text{accept}) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \Delta E < 0 \\ e^{-\Delta E / T} & \text{if } \Delta E \geq 0 \end{cases}$$ | **Complexity**: O(N) per cooling step for N parallel trials. | Extends SA with a **conservation constraint** (γ + η = C): the total "mass" of the solution must remain constant. Any proposed move that violates conservation is rejected. This is analogous to: | | `try_accept(ΔE)` | O(1) | Metropolis criterion | | | `cool_all(iteration)` | O(N) | N parallel trials |
ARCHITECTURE: This crate is the **η (eta)** layer — orchestration and decision logic — in the γ + η = C framework. The **γ** is the GPU kernel that evaluates the cost function and generates random perturbations. This separation is essential: the GPU handles the compute-intensive inner loop (millions of Metropolis evaluations per second), while this crate handles the outer loop (when to cool, reheat, checkpoint, and terminate).

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: graph-astar
CONCEPTS: graph, crypto, compute, storage, search
DEFINITION: A Rust implementation of the A\* (A-star) shortest-path algorithm for weighted graphs. Given a graph with non-negative edge weights and an admissible heuristic, A\* finds the optimal (minimum-cost) path from a start node to a goal node. This implementation uses a binary heap priority queue with lazy deletion for efficiency.
SECTIONS: graph-astar | Why It Matters | How It Works | Algorithm | Admissibility and Optimality | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Implementation: Lazy Deletion | Quick Start | API | `astar(graph, start, goal, heuristic)` | Architecture Notes
MATH: | Priority queue operations | O(E log V) total (each edge may push) | | | Relaxation checks | O(E) | | | **Total** | **O((V + E) log V)** | | where V = number of vertices, E = number of edges. With a perfect heuristic (h = h\*), A\* explores only nodes on the optimal path: O(L) where L = path length. With h = 0, it degenerates to Dijkstra's | O(V) for the g-score array and priority queue.
ARCHITECTURE: A\* is a **γ** (search strategy) applied to a **graph** (**η**) to produce a **path** (**C**). The heuristic h is what makes A\* more than Dijkstra: it injects domain knowledge into the search, biasing exploration toward the goal. The quality of C depends on the quality of h — a perfect heuristic gives O(L) performance, a zero heuristic gives O(E log V). This is the γ + η = C principle in action: the algorithm (γ) and the problem structure (η) together determine the cost of the solution (C). Better heuristics → less search → faster answers.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: graph-bellman-ford
CONCEPTS: graph, compute, storage, protocol, search
DEFINITION: A **single-source shortest-path algorithm** for weighted directed graphs that handles negative edge weights and detects negative cycles — the only common shortest-path algorithm that can do both.
SECTIONS: Graph Bellman-Ford | Why It Matters | How It Works | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References | License
MATH: Dijkstra's algorithm is faster (O((V+E) log V) vs O(VE)) but fails catastrophically on negative edges — it greedily commits to the first path it finds, which may be suboptimal when a later negative ed | | Time | O(V × E) | O((V + E) log V) | | | Space | O(V) | O(V + E) | | **Comparison**: On a dense graph (E ≈ V²), Bellman-Ford is O(V³). On sparse graphs (E ≈ V), it's O(V²), competitive with Dijkstra for small graphs. The SPFA (Shortest Path Faster Algorithm) is a queue | This crate is part of SuperInstance's graph algorithm suite. Bellman-Ford handles the general case (negative weights, cycle detection) while graph-dijkstra handles the common case (non-negative weight
ARCHITECTURE: This crate is part of SuperInstance's graph algorithm suite. Bellman-Ford handles the general case (negative weights, cycle detection) while graph-dijkstra handles the common case (non-negative weights, faster). The choice between them is a **γ + η = C** trade-off: Bellman-Ford's generality (low γ from fewer constraints on input) costs more computation time (higher η). See [Architecture](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md). **Currency arbitrage example**: Given exchange rates as a graph, negative cycles represent arbitrage opportunities. If `USD → EUR → JPY → USD` yields more than you started with, that's a negative-weight cycle when edge weights are `-log(rate)`. Bellman-Ford detects this in O(VE). **Difference constraints**: Systems of inequalities `x_j - x_i ≤ c` can be solved by constructing a constraint graph where edge `(i, j)` has weight `c`. Shortest paths from a virtual source give a feasible solution. This is used in hardware timing analysis and scheduling.

The adjacency list representation (`Vec<Vec<usize>>`) enables O(degree(v)) iteration over neigh | - Time: O(V + E) — each vertex enqueued once, each edge examined once (directed) or twice (undirected) | - Space: O(V) for the visited set and queue | | Memory (worst case) | O(V) — width of graph | O(V) — depth of graph |
ARCHITECTURE: This crate is the BFS primitive in SuperInstance's graph algorithm suite. It provides the traversal engine that graph-walker-go and higher-level algorithms (shortest path, connected components, bipartite check) build upon. In **γ + η = C**, BFS represents the optimal η for unweighted shortest paths — O(V+E) is a lower bound for graph traversal. See [Architecture](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md). **Bipartite testing**: A graph is bipartite if and only if BFS finds no edge connecting two nodes at the same depth level. Color nodes alternating by depth; if any edge connects same-color nodes, the graph is odd-cycle (non-bipartite). This runs in O(V + E). **Connected components**: Run BFS from every unvisited node. Each BFS tree is one connected component. O(V + E) for the full decomposition.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: graph-dijkstra
CONCEPTS: graph, crypto, compute, protocol, math
DEFINITION: A **single-source shortest-path algorithm** for graphs with non-negative edge weights, using a binary min-heap (priority queue) to achieve O((V + E) log V) time — the asymptotically optimal approach for this problem class.
SECTIONS: Graph Dijkstra | Why It Matters | How It Works | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References | License
MATH: A **single-source shortest-path algorithm** for graphs with non-negative edge weights, using a binary min-heap (priority queue) to achieve O((V + E) log V) time — the asymptotically optimal approach f | Dijkstra's algorithm is the backbone of real-world routing: GPS navigation (Google Maps), network routing protocols (OSPF, IS-IS), telephone network least-cost routing, and any system that computes "c | **Why the min-heap matters**: Each `pop` extracts the minimum-distance unsettled node in O(log V). Without the heap (naive linear scan), each extraction is O(V), giving O(V²) total — fine for dense gr | **Lazy deletion**: The algorithm pushes duplicate entries into the heap when a shorter path is found, without removing the old entry. The stale check `if d > dist[u]: continue` skips outdated entries  | | Array (linear scan) | O(V²) | Dense graphs (E ≈ V²) |
ARCHITECTURE: Dijkstra is the workhorse shortest-path algorithm in SuperInstance's graph suite. It handles the common case (non-negative weights) optimally, deferring to Bellman-Ford only when negative weights are possible. The choice is a **γ + η = C** trade: Dijkstra's constraint (no negative weights, low γ) buys lower computational cost (efficient η). See [Architecture](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

documents.name: hausdorff-dimension
documents.description:
CRATE: hausdorff-dimension
CONCEPTS: graph, protocol
DEFINITION: The **Hausdorff dimension** is a fractal dimension measure that generalizes the notion of dimension to sets that are not smooth manifolds — quantifying how the "mass" of a set scales as the measurement unit shrinks. For self-similar fractals, it equals `D = log(N) / log(1/r)` where N is the number of self-similar copies and r is the scaling ratio.
SECTIONS: Hausdorff Dimension | Why It Matters | How It Works | Self-Similar Fractals
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: hausdorff-dimension
CONCEPTS: graph, protocol
DEFINITION: The **Hausdorff dimension** is a fractal dimension measure that generalizes the notion of dimension to sets that are not smooth manifolds — quantifying how the "mass" of a set scales as the measurement unit shrinks. For self-similar fractals, it equals `D = log(N) / log(1/r)` where N is the number of self-similar copies and r is the scaling ratio.
SECTIONS: Hausdorff Dimension | Why It Matters | How It Works | Self-Similar Fractals | Box-Counting Dimension | Relationship to Topological Dimension | Complexity | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References | License
MATH: $$D = \frac{\log N}{\log(1/r)}$$ | $$D = \lim_{\epsilon \to 0} \frac{\log N(\epsilon)}{\log(1/\epsilon)}$$ | $$D = \frac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}$$ | - Analytic formula: **O(1)** | - Box-counting regression: **O(m)** where m is the number of measurements
ARCHITECTURE: Part of the **SuperInstance** fractal geometry toolkit. Hausdorff dimension computation supports the topological analysis and complexity quantification modules. This contributes to **γ + η = C**: γ (mathematical rigor in dimension theory) and η (efficient computation) combine for correct fractal analysis. documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: hermite-polynomial
CONCEPTS: wavelet, graph, compute, math, systems, search
DEFINITION: **Hermite polynomials** are a classical family of orthogonal polynomials that arise in probability (Edgeworth expansions), physics (quantum harmonic oscillator wavefunctions), and numerical analysis (Gauss-Hermite quadrature). This crate implements both the **probabilist's** Hₙ(x) and **physicist's** Heₙ(x) variants with evaluation, derivatives, coefficient generation, and quadrature node/weight computation.
SECTIONS: Hermite Polynomials | Why It Matters | How It Works | Two Conventions | Three-Term Recurrence (Evaluation) | Derivatives | Coefficient Generation | Gauss-Hermite Quadrature | Complexity | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes
MATH: $$H_{n+1}(x) = x \, H_n(x) - n \, H_{n-1}(x), \quad H_0 = 1, \; H_1 = x$$ | $$\text{He}_{n+1}(x) = 2x \, \text{He}_n(x) - 2n \, \text{He}_{n-1}(x), \quad \text{He}_0 = 1, \; \text{He}_1 = 2x$$ | Both variants use a **climbing recurrence** — evaluate from the bottom up, requiring only O(n) time and O(1) space: | $$H_n'(x) = n \cdot H_{n-1}(x) \qquad \text{He}_n'(x) = 2n \cdot \text{He}_{n-1}(x)$$ | $$w_i = \frac{2^{n-1} n! \sqrt{\pi}}{n \cdot [\text{He}_{n-1}(x_i)]^2}$$
ARCHITECTURE: Part of the **SuperInstance** numerical analysis library. Hermite polynomials support probability modeling, quadrature integration, and physics simulations across the Fleet compute layer. This contributes to **γ + η = C**: γ (mathematical correctness of orthogonal polynomial theory) and η (O(n) evaluation via recurrence) combine for efficient special function computation. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md) for the full system design.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: import-resolver
CONCEPTS: crypto, compute, storage, systems, search
DEFINITION: A Rust library for parsing and resolving PE (Portable Executable) import tables. It maintains a structured index of all external function dependencies declared by a binary, organized by DLL for efficient lookup. Supports both named imports and ordinal-based imports.
SECTIONS: import-resolver | Why It Matters | How It Works | PE Import Table Structure | Data Model | Lookup Complexity | Ordinal vs Named Imports | Memory Layout | Quick Start | API | `ImportTable` | `ImportEntry`
MATH: The resolver uses a two-level index for O(1) lookup by either global position or per-DLL: | ├── entries: Vec<ImportEntry>          ← flat storage, O(1) by index | └── by_dll: HashMap<String, Vec<usize>> ← DLL → entry indices, O(1) per DLL | | Add import | O(1) amortized | Push to entries + HashMap insert | | | Look up by DLL | O(K) | HashMap lookup + K indices |
ARCHITECTURE: The import table is the **dependency manifest (η)** of a PE binary. The resolver (**γ**) transforms raw PE bytes into a queryable structure (**C**). This is the γ + η = C principle applied to binary analysis: the raw import data is the ground truth, the resolver makes it accessible. Security tools consume the resolved table to answer questions like "does this binary call `CreateRemoteThread`?" without re-parsing PE headers.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: impulse-response
CONCEPTS: conservation, wavelet, graph, compute, storage, math, systems, search
DEFINITION: A Rust library for **discrete-time impulse response analysis** of Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) systems. It provides convolution, energy computation, and delta function generation for digital signal processing, audio filter design, and system identification.
SECTIONS: impulse-response | Why It Matters | How It Works | Discrete Convolution | Delta (Dirac) Function | Energy | Complexity Analysis | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References | License
MATH: $$y[n] = (x * h)[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \cdot h[n-k]$$ | Without efficient convolution, real-time DSP is impossible. This crate provides the baseline $O(nm)$ direct form for correctness validation before optimizing with FFT-based overlap-add. | $$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} h[k] \cdot x[n-k]$$ | $$E = \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} |h[n]|^2$$ | By Parseval's theorem, this equals $\frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} |H(e^{j\omega})|^2 d\omega$ — the total power of the frequency response.
ARCHITECTURE: This crate is the **computational kernel (η)** of a DSP pipeline. The impulse response coefficients define the filter characteristics (γ, the system model), and the convolution operation applies that model to input signals. Together, γ (system identification) + η (convolution engine) = C (complete filtering system). For production audio, this crate provides the reference implementation against which FFT-accelerated versions are validated.

CRATE: intent-flux-bridge

DEFINITION: An experimental Rust crate that maps **natural-language GPU computation intent** to **Flux bytecode** — a portable IR for heterogeneous compute. The γ + η = C model: Flux bytecode definitions (γ) + this compiler (η) = a complete intent-to-execution pipeline.

SECTIONS: Intersection Form | Why It Matters | How It Wo
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: intersection-form
CONCEPTS: conservation, graph, crypto, compute, storage, protocol, math, search
DEFINITION: **A Rust library for computing intersection forms on 4-manifolds — the fundamental bilinear form that encodes how 2-dimensional cycles intersect inside a 4-dimensional topological space.** It computes signatures, determinants, direct sums, and checks for unimodularity, providing the algebraic toolkit for 4-manifold topology.
SECTIONS: Intersection Form | Why It Matters | How It Works | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | License
MATH: - **`evaluate(x, y)`** — Computes `x^T · Q · y` in O(n²) by direct matrix-vector multiplication. This is the algebraic intersection number of two homology classes. | - **`signature()`** — Computes `b⁺ - b⁻` (positive minus negative eigenvalue count) via LDLᵀ decomposition with partial pivoting. This is O(n³) via Gaussian elimination. The signature is a homotopy in | - **`direct_sum()`** — Block-diagonal concatenation of two forms. This corresponds to the connected sum of the underlying manifolds: `Q(M#N) = Q(M) ⊕ Q(N)`. O((m+n)²). | - **`det()`** — Recursive cofactor expansion along the first row. O(n!) worst case but fine for the small ranks typical in topology (rarely > 24). A form is **unimodular** iff `|det| = 1`, which is ne | - `evaluate(x, y) -> i64` — Bilinear form evaluation. O(n²).
ARCHITECTURE: Part of the SuperInstance topology toolkit alongside [knot-invariant](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md), [linking-number](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md), [mapping-class](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md), and [L²-cohomology](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md). Together these provide a computational framework for low-dimensional topology research.

CRATE: interval-tree

DEFINITION: A Rust implementation of a **centered interval tree** — a data structure for efficient stabbing queries on a static set of intervals. Supports both point queries ("which intervals contain point p?") and range queries ("which intervals overlap [a, b]?").

MATH: Without an interval tree, answering "which of N intervals contain point p?" requires O(N) linear scan. For large datasets queried repeatedly, the interval tree reduces this to O(log N + k) where k is  | 1. At center node: scan intervals_by_start until start > p   → O(k_center) | | Construction | O(N log N) | Median-finding + recursive partitioning | | | Point query | O(log N + k) | k = number of results | | | Range query | O(log N + k) | k = number of results |
ARCHITECTURE: This crate is the **data structure layer (γ)** — it defines the spatial index structure. The **query layer (η)** performs the lookups and is embedded in the same crate. Together, γ (interval storage) + η (query algorithms) = C (complete range search capability). The static-build design means the tree is optimized for read-heavy workloads where the interval set changes infrequently.

CRATE: ir-optimizer
CONCEPTS: conservation, graph, compute, math
DEFINITION: A Rust library for optimizing intermediate representation (IR) code using Static Single Assignment (SSA)-style passes. It implements three classical compiler optimizations — constant folding, strength reduction, and dead code elimination — operating on a statement-level IR with support for binary operations, comparisons, phi nodes, and function calls.
SECTIONS: ir-optimizer | Why It Matters | How It Works | Constant Folding | Strength Reduction | Dead Code Elimination (DCE) | Convergence | Quick Start | API | `IRModule` | Expression Types | Architecture Notes
MATH: **Time complexity**: O(N) per pass where N = number of expression nodes in the module. | **Time complexity**: O(N) per pass. | **Time complexity**: O(N · D) where N = statements, D = average expression depth for `used_vars` traversal. | The IR optimizer is a **γ** (transform pipeline) applied to a **program** (**η**) to produce **optimized code** (**C**). Each pass is a local rewrite rule: fold replaces constant subtrees with leaves,
ARCHITECTURE: The IR optimizer is a **γ** (transform pipeline) applied to a **program** (**η**) to produce **optimized code** (**C**). Each pass is a local rewrite rule: fold replaces constant subtrees with leaves, reduce replaces expensive ops with cheaper ones, DCE removes dead assignments. The composition of these γ passes produces a cumulative improvement that no single pass achieves alone — this is the power of pipeline composition in the γ + η = C framework. The correctness invariant: each pass preserves program semantics (the observable behavior of the output C is identical to the input η).

documents.description:
CRATE: ising-model
CONCEPTS: conservation, graph, compute
DEFINITION: A Rust implementation of the **2D Ising model** using Metropolis-Hastings Monte Carlo simulation. The Ising model is the canonical model of phase transitions in statistical mechanics — it exhibits a spontaneous symmetry breaking at the critical temperature $T_c = 2/\ln(1+\sqrt{2}) \approx 2.269$ (Onsager, 1944).
SECTIONS: ising-model | Why It Matters | How It Works | Hamiltonian | Metropolis-Hastings Algorithm | Observables |

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: jacobian-system
CONCEPTS: conservation, crypto, compute, math, search
DEFINITION: A Rust library for **numerical Jacobian matrix computation** and related linear algebra operations. It computes Jacobians via central differences, calculates determinants via Laplace expansion, and provides matrix-vector multiplication — the core operations needed for Newton's method, optimization, and dynamical system analysis.
SECTIONS: jacobian-system | Why It Matters | How It Works | Numerical Jacobian via Central Differences | Determinant via Laplace Expansion | Complexity Analysis | Central Difference vs. Forward Difference | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References | License
MATH: $$J_F(x) = \begin{pmatrix} \frac{\partial F_1}{\partial x_1} & \cdots & \frac{\partial F_1}{\partial x_n} \\ \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \frac{\partial F_m}{\partial x_1} & \cdots & \frac{\partial F_m | $$\frac{\partial F_i}{\partial x_j} \approx \frac{F_i(x + h \cdot e_j) - F_i(x - h \cdot e_j)}{2h}$$ | **Error analysis:** The truncation error is $O(h^2)$ (from the Taylor series), while roundoff error is $O(\varepsilon_{\text{mach}}/h)$. The optimal step size balances these: | $$h_{\text{opt}} \approx \sqrt[3]{\varepsilon_{\text{mach}}} \approx 6 \times 10^{-6}$$ | $$\det(A) = \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} (-1)^j a_{0j} \det(M_{0j})$$
ARCHITECTURE: This crate is the **numerical analysis kernel (η)**: it provides the computational primitives that operate on mathematical functions (γ, the system being analyzed). Together, γ (the nonlinear system F) + η (Jacobian + linear algebra) = C (a complete Newton-type solver or stability analyzer). For production optimization, pair with LU decomposition from a linear algebra crate for O(n³) determinants and matrix inversion.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: jwt-claims
CONCEPTS: crypto, storage, protocol, search
DEFINITION: A Rust library for parsing, serializing, and validating JSON Web Token (JWT) claims as defined by RFC 7519. Provides strongly-typed access to standard registered claims (`sub`, `exp`, `iat`, `iss`, `aud`) with serde-based (de)serialization and expiration checking.
SECTIONS: jwt-claims | Why It Matters | How It Works | JWT Structure | Expiration Validation | Serialization | Time Complexity | Security Considerations | Quick Start | API | `Claims` | Methods
MATH: The check is a simple integer comparison: O(1). The `now` parameter should be the current Unix timestamp (seconds since epoch). Clock skew between issuer and verifier should be handled by the caller ( | All operations are O(1): | - Deserialization: O(|JSON|) where |JSON| is token payload length | - Expiration check: O(1) integer comparison | - Serialization: O(1) for fixed-size fields
ARCHITECTURE: JWT claims are the **state payload (η)** of a stateless authentication system. The verifier (**γ**) parses claims, checks expiration, and validates issuer/audience to produce an authorization decision (**C**). The γ + η = C principle is literal here: the claims are the entire state — there is no server-side session lookup. This makes JWT verification O(1) per request (after signature check), enabling horizontal scaling without shared session storage. The tradeoff: tokens cannot be revoked before expiration without additional infrastructure (deny-lists, short TTLs).

It's used in GPS navigation, aerospace guidance  | All operations are O(1) for the scalar filter and O(1) (fixed 2×2 matrices) for the 2D filter. | - `predict()` — Advance state via dynamics model. documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: key-value-store
CONCEPTS: conservation, crypto, compute, storage, math, systems, search
DEFINITION: A persistent key-value store with Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) and log compaction, implemented in Rust. The store provides `O(1)` amortized point lookups via hash indexing while ensuring crash recovery through an append-only write-ahead log.
SECTIONS: Key-Value Store | Why It Matters | How It Works | Hash Index Lookup | Write-Ahead Log (WAL) | Tombstones | Compaction | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References | License
MATH: A persistent key-value store with Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) and log compaction, implemented in Rust. The store provides `O(1)` amortized point lookups via hash indexing while ensuring crash recovery t | - **Reads** are served from an in-memory `HashMap`, giving `O(1)` average-case lookup with no disk I/O on the hot path. | $$h(k) \mod N_\text{buckets}$$ | and follows the chain to find the matching key. Average case is `O(1)`; worst case (all keys collide) is `O(n)`. | | `get(k)`  | `O(1)` avg     | `O(1)` |
ARCHITECTURE: The store implements the **γ + η = C** contract at the storage layer: - **γ (gamma): correctness** — the WAL guarantees that every committed write survives a crash. The tombstone mechanism ensures deletes are also durable. - **η (eta): efficiency** — the hash index provides `O(1)` reads, and compaction bounds the WAL to `O(active_keys)` rather than `O(total_writes)`.

MATH: $$\text{Writhe} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \epsilon_i$$ | $$V_K(t) = (-A^3)^{-w(K)} \langle K \rangle \quad \text{where } t = A^{-4}$$ | $$\langle \crossing \rangle = A\langle \smooth \rangle + A^{-1}\langle \hsmooth \rangle$$ | | `writhe()` | `O(n)` — single pass over crossings | | | `crossing_number()` | `O(1)` — stored as `len()` |
ARCHITECTURE: The library embodies the **γ + η = C** principle in computational mathematics: - **γ (correctness)**: invariants are computed from the mathematical definitions directly. The writhe is exactly $\sum \epsilon_i$, not an approximation. - **η (efficiency)**: the simplified Jones polynomial is computed in `O(n)` from the writhe and crossing count, avoiding the full `O(2^n)` state-sum when only the normalized form is needed.

documents.name: kolmogorov-complexity

CRATE: kolmogorov-complexity
CONCEPTS: crypto, compute
DEFINITION: **A Rust library for estimating Kolmogorov complexity — the length of the shortest program that produces a given output — through practical compression-based and entropy-based estimators.** While true Kolmogorov complexity K(x) is uncomputable (a consequence of the halting problem), this crate provides tight upper and lower bounds using Lempel-Ziv factorization, block entropy, and Shannon entropy.
SECTIONS: Kolmogorov Complexity

SECTIONS: Kolmogorov Equation | Why It Matters | How It Works | Forward Equation (Fokker–Planck) | Backward Equation | Finite-Difference Discretization | Stability: The CFL Condition | Boundary Conditio
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: kolmogorov-equation
CONCEPTS: wavelet, graph, search
DEFINITION: Numerical solvers for the Kolmogorov forward (Fokker–Planck) and backward PDEs governing probability evolution in stochastic dynamical systems. The library uses explicit finite-difference discretization on a 1-D spatial grid.
SECTIONS: Kolmogorov Equation | Why It Matters | How It Works | Forward Equation (Fokker–Planck) | Backward Equation | Finite-Difference Discretization | Stability: The CFL Condition | Boundary Conditions | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References
MATH: $$\frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\bigl[\mu(x)\,p\bigr] + \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial^2}{\partial x^2}\bigl[\sigma^2(x)\,p\bigr]$$ | $$-\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \mu(x)\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{1}{2}\sigma^2(x)\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$$ | $$\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \approx \frac{p_{i+1} - p_{i-1}}{2\Delta x} \quad \text{(central, `O(\Delta x^2)` accuracy)}$$ | $$\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x^2} \approx \frac{p_{i+1} - 2p_i + p_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2} \quad \text{(3-point stencil)}$$ | $$\alpha = \frac{\sigma^2 \Delta t}{2 \Delta x^2} \leq \frac{1}{2}$$
ARCHITECTURE: The solvers respect **γ + η = C** in scientific computing: - **γ (correctness)**: central differences guarantee `O(Δx²)` spatial accuracy. Reflecting boundaries conserve probability mass. The CFL condition is documented and must be respected by the caller. - **η (efficiency)**: the explicit scheme is `O(n · steps)` with minimal memory (`O(n)` — two buffers swapped in-place). No matrix factorization is required.

Unlike the legacy `select(2)` and `poll(2)` system calls, `kqueue` operates in ` | - **Scalable**: `O(active_events)` return, not `O(total_fds)`. | | `Kqueue::new()` | `O(1)` | Single syscall (`kqueue()`) | | | `add(fd, filter, flags)` | `O(1)` | One `kevent` call with 1 change | | | `wait(events, timeout)` | `O(k)` | Where `k` = number of ready events |

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: l2-cohomology
CONCEPTS: conservation, wavelet, graph, crypto, compute, storage, math, systems
DEFINITION: A Rust library for computing $L^2$-cohomology and reduced $L^2$-Betti numbers of covering spaces. Provides cochain representations, harmonic form detection, and $L^2$-Betti number computation for surfaces of arbitrary genus.
SECTIONS: L² Cohomology | Why It Matters | How It Works | L² Cohomology Definition | L² Betti Numbers | Key Theorems | Surface Computation | Complexity | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References
MATH: $$L^2\Omega^k(\tilde{M}) = \bigl\{ \omega \in \Omega^k(\tilde{M}) : \|\omega\|_{L^2}^2 = \int_{\tilde{M}} \omega \wedge *\omega < \infty \bigr\}$$ | $$H^k_{(2)}(\tilde{M}) = \frac{\ker(d_k) \cap L^2\Omega^k}{\overline{\mathrm{im}(d_{k-1})} \cap L^2\Omega^k}$$ | $$b^{(2)}_k(\tilde{M}) = \dim_{\Gamma} H^k_{(2)}(\tilde{M})$$ | $$\chi(X) = \sum_{k=0}^{\dim X} (-1)^k b^{(2)}_k(\tilde{X})$$ | **Singer Conjecture** (open): For closed aspherical manifolds, $b^{(2)}_k(\tilde{M}) = 0$ for all $k \neq \frac{\dim M}{2}$.
ARCHITECTURE: The library instantiates **γ + η = C** in computational topology: - **γ (correctness)**: $L^2$-Betti numbers for surfaces use proven closed-form results from the spectral theory of the hyperbolic Laplacian (Dodziuk 1982). The Euler characteristic identity is verified against Atiyah's theorem. - **η (efficiency)**: closed-form formulas give `O(1)` computation for surfaces. General manifolds would require numerical spectral estimation, which is `O(n^3)` for sparse eigensolvers on $n$-element meshes.

documents.description:
CRATE: laplacian-operator
CONCEPTS: conservation, wavelet, graph, crypto, math, search
DEFINITION: A Rust library for computing the combinatorial and normalized graph Laplacian matrices. These matrices are foundational to spectral graph theory, graph partitioning, spectral clustering, and graph neural networks.
SECTIONS: Laplacian Operator | Why It Matters | How It Works | Combinatorial Laplacian | Normalized Laplacian | Quadratic Form Interpretation | Complexity | Quick Start | API | Architectu
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: laplacian-operator
CONCEPTS: conservation, wavelet, graph, crypto, math, search
DEFINITION: A Rust library for computing the combinatorial and normalized graph Laplacian matrices. These matrices are foundational to spectral graph theory, graph partitioning, spectral clustering, and graph neural networks.
SECTIONS: Laplacian Operator | Why It Matters | How It Works | Combinatorial Laplacian | Normalized Laplacian | Quadratic Form Interpretation | Complexity | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References | License
MATH: $$L = D - A$$ | $$L_{ij} = \begin{cases} d_i & \text{if } i = j \\ -1 & \text{if } (i,j) \in E \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ | $$L_{\text{norm}} = D^{-1/2} L D^{-1/2} = I - D^{-1/2} A D^{-1/2}$$ | $$L_{\text{norm},ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } i = j,\ d_i > 0 \\ -\frac{1}{\sqrt{d_i \cdot d_j}} & \text{if } (i,j) \in E \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ | $$f^\top L f = \sum_{(i,j) \in E} (f_i - f_j)^2$$
ARCHITECTURE: The library implements **γ + η = C** in numerical linear algebra: - **γ (correctness)**: the Laplacian is constructed directly from the definition $L = D - A$. Row sums are guaranteed zero (verified in tests), ensuring the constant vector is in the kernel. The normalized form uses exact $1/\sqrt{d_i d_j}$ weighting per Chung's definition. - **η (efficiency)**: construction is `O(|V| + |E|)` — optimal for the dense output format. Isolated vertices (degree 0) are handled correctly in the normalized form by setting the diagonal to 0 rather than dividing by zero.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: lattice-cipher
CONCEPTS: graph, crypto, compute, math, search
DEFINITION: A Rust library implementing placeholder lattice-based cryptographic primitives based on the Learning With Errors (LWE) problem. Provides key generation, single-bit encryption, and decryption for educational and prototyping purposes. The LWE problem is the cryptographic foundation of post-quantum cryptography.
SECTIONS: lattice-cipher | Why It Matters | How It Works | The LWE Problem | Encryption Scheme (Single Bit) | Security Parameters | Complexity | Post-Quantum Context | Quick Start | API | `LweKeyPair` | Free Functions
MATH: | Key generation | O(n²) | O(n²) for public key | | | Encryption | O(n) | O(n) for ciphertext | | | Decryption | O(n) | O(1) | | | Best known attack (BKZ) | 2^(O(n/log n)) | 2^(O(√n)) | | LWE is the **trapdoor function (η)** — without the secret key, the ciphertext is indistinguishable from random. The encryption algorithm (**γ**) uses the public key to encode a message, and decryption
ARCHITECTURE: LWE is the **trapdoor function (η)** — without the secret key, the ciphertext is indistinguishable from random. The encryption algorithm (**γ**) uses the public key to encode a message, and decryption (**γ⁻¹**) uses the secret key to recover it. The composition γ + η = C holds: the security of C (the plaintext) depends entirely on the hardness of η (the LWE problem). If a quantum computer can solve LWE efficiently, the composition breaks. This is why the entire post-quantum migration exists — to find η that resists both classical and quantum adversaries.
CROSS_REFS: relates-to:graph relates-to:crypto relates-to:compute relates-to:math relates-to:search

documents.description:
CRATE: lattice-reduction

DEFINITION: A Rust library implementing the LLL (Lenstra–Lenstra–Lovász) lattice basis reduction algorithm with Gram–Schmidt orthogonalization. Lattice reduction is the cornerstone of computational number theory, lattice-based cryptography, and integer programming.
SECTIONS: Lattice Reduction | Why It Matters | How It Works | Lattices and Bases | The Lovász Condition | The LLL Algorithm | Quality Guarantee | Complexity | Quick Start
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: lattice-reduction
CONCEPTS: graph, crypto, math, search
DEFINITION: A Rust library implementing the LLL (Lenstra–Lenstra–Lovász) lattice basis reduction algorithm with Gram–Schmidt orthogonalization. Lattice reduction is the cornerstone of computational number theory, lattice-based cryptography, and integer programming.
SECTIONS: Lattice Reduction | Why It Matters | How It Works | Lattices and Bases | The Lovász Condition | The LLL Algorithm | Quality Guarantee | Complexity | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References
MATH: $$L = \left\{ \sum_{i=1}^{n} z_i \mathbf{b}_i : z_i \in \mathbb{Z} \right\}$$ | $$\mathbf{b}_i^* = \mathbf{b}_i - \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \mu_{ij} \mathbf{b}_j^*, \qquad \mu_{ij} = \frac{\langle \mathbf{b}_i, \mathbf{b}_j^* \rangle}{\langle \mathbf{b}_j^*, \mathbf{b}_j^* \rangle}$$ | A basis is **LLL-reduced** with parameter $\delta \in (\frac{1}{4}, 1]$ (typically $\delta = \frac{3}{4}$) if: | 1. **Size-reduced**: $|\mu_{ij}| \leq \frac{1}{2}$ for all $j < i$. | $$\|\mathbf{b}_1\| \leq 2^{(n-1)/2} \cdot \lambda_1(L)$$
ARCHITECTURE: The library instantiates **γ + η = C** in computational number theory: - **γ (correctness)**: the GSO is computed with exact dot products over integers, projected to `f64`. The Lovász condition and size-reduction are applied iteratively, matching the original LLL paper's algorithm exactly. - **η (efficiency)**: the algorithm runs in polynomial time. Each iteration recomputes the GSO incrementally (a known optimization point — a full implementation would cache GSO between swaps). The `f64` arithmetic suffices for moderate-dimensional inputs; arbitrary precision would be needed for cryptographic-scale problems.

documents.description:
CRATE: learning-rate
CONCEPTS: graph
DEFINITION: A Rust library implementing learning-rate schedules for gradient-descent optimizers. Provides four fundamental schedules — constant, step decay, exponential decay, and cosine annealing — each returning a closure `Fn(usize) -> f64` that maps epoch number to learning rate.
SECTIONS: learning-rate | Why It Matters | How It Works | 1. Constant Schedule | 2. Step Decay | 3. Exponential Decay | 4. Cosine Annealing | Comparison Table | Quick Start | API
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: learning-rate
CONCEPTS: graph
DEFINITION: A Rust library implementing learning-rate schedules for gradient-descent optimizers. Provides four fundamental schedules — constant, step decay, exponential decay, and cosine annealing — each returning a closure `Fn(usize) -> f64` that maps epoch number to learning rate.
SECTIONS: learning-rate | Why It Matters | How It Works | 1. Constant Schedule | 2. Step Decay | 3. Exponential Decay | 4. Cosine Annealing | Comparison Table | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References
MATH: **Convergence**: Does not satisfy Robbins-Monro. SGD with constant rate oscillates within a ball of radius O(η) around the optimum. | η(t) = η₀ · γ^⌊t / S⌋ | Drops the learning rate by factor γ every S epochs. Common values: γ = 0.5, S = 10 (halve every 10 epochs) or γ = 0.1, S = 30 (drop 10× every 30 epochs, the "step LR" from ImageNet training). | **Convergence**: Satisfies Robbins-Monro if γ < 1 and S is finite. The exponential decay ensures Ση_t² < ∞. | **Time complexity**: O(1) per evaluation (integer division + `powi`).
ARCHITECTURE: The learning rate schedule is the **γ** (control law) that governs how the optimizer (**η**, the update rule) converges. The schedule maps epoch → step size, and this mapping determines convergence quality (**C**). In the γ + η = C framework: a good schedule makes even a simple optimizer (SGD) competitive with Adam — this is why ResNet training on ImageNet uses step decay with plain SGD and still achieves state-of-the-art. The schedule is the cheapest performance lever available: changing it costs zero runtime, only retuning the hyperparameters.
CROSS_REFS: relates-to:graph

documents.name: lebesgue-integral
documents.description:
CRATE: lebesgue-integral
CONCEPTS: compute
DEFINITION: **A Rust library for computing Lebesgue integrals via simple-function approximation and Lebesgue-Stieltjes integration.** Unlike Riemann integration (which partitions the domain), Lebesgue integration partitions the *range* and sums up the measure of the preimage of each level set — a fundamentally different approach that generalizes to more abstract measure spaces.
SECTIONS: Lebesgue Integral | Why It Matters | How It Works | Quick Start |
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: lebesgue-integral
CONCEPTS: compute
DEFINITION: **A Rust library for computing Lebesgue integrals via simple-function approximation and Lebesgue-Stieltjes integration.** Unlike Riemann integration (which partitions the domain), Lebesgue integration partitions the *range* and sums up the measure of the preimage of each level set — a fundamentally different approach that generalizes to more abstract measure spaces.
SECTIONS: Lebesgue Integral | Why It Matters | How It Works | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | License
MATH: 1. **Sample** f at `n_sample` points over `[a, b]` to find `f_min` and `f_max`. O(n_sample). | 3. **Measure preimage**: For each band `[lo, hi)`, count how many sample points satisfy `f(x) ∈ [lo, hi)` and multiply by `dx` (approximating the Lebesgue measure of the preimage). O(n_sample) per lev | Total cost: O(n_levels · n_sample). For constant functions, this is detected early and returned immediately. | **Lebesgue-Stieltjes integral** (`lebesgue_stieltjes`) — Computes `∫ f dg` where `g` is a monotone function. The measure `dg` replaces `dx`: instead of uniform weighting, each interval `[x_i, x_i+1)`  | - `lebesgue_integral(f, a, b, n_levels, n_sample) -> f64` — Simple-function approximation. O(n_levels · n_sample).

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: ledger-journal
CONCEPTS: conservation, fleet, graph, crypto, storage, search
DEFINITION: A Rust library implementing an append-only ledger journal — the foundational data structure for double-entry accounting systems, audit logs, and event-sourced state machines. Entries are timestamped, tagged, and maintained in chronological order via merge-on-insert sorting.
SECTIONS: ledger-journal | Why It Matters | How It Works | Data Model | Merge-on-Insert | Ordering Guarantee | Tag-Based Querying | Time Complexity | Quick Start | API | `LogEntry` | `collect_logs`
MATH: entries.extend(batch)                  // O(B) where B = batch size | entries.sort_by_key(|e| e.timestamp)   // O(N log N) where N = total entries | This is simple but O(N log N) per batch. For high-throughput systems, a production implementation would use: | | Re-sort (this impl) | O(N log N) | Simple, fine for small N | | | Binary search insertion | O(B · log N + B) | Better for large journals |
ARCHITECTURE: The ledger journal is the **immutable event log (η)** — the ground truth of what happened. Any consumer (**γ**) that reads and interprets the journal produces a derived view (**C**): a balance sheet, a graph state, a search index. The γ + η = C principle is the essence of event sourcing: the journal is the single source of truth, and all application state is a materialized view computed from it. This separation enables time-travel queries (replay to any point), audit compliance (the log is immutable), and fault tolerance (rebuild state from the log after a crash).
