MATH: **Gate 1 — BLAKE2b hashing**: Each registered intent pattern is hashed with BLAKE2b-256, producing a deterministic 32-byte digest. Query intents are hashed the same way; comparison is O(1) memcmp. BLA | **Cosine search**: Brute-force O(N × D) where N = corpus size, D = 64. For 1000 intents: 64,000 multiply-adds per query. The dot product is unrolled in 4-element chunks for auto-vectorization: | Lever Runner Carapace is the native performance layer replacing Python hot paths in the lever-runner system. Every component is pure Rust with no external ML dependencies. The three-gate pipeline embo
ARCHITECTURE: Lever Runner Carapace is the native performance layer replacing Python hot paths in the lever-runner system. Every component is pure Rust with no external ML dependencies. The three-gate pipeline embodies **γ + η = C**: Gate 1 (exact match) is pure η (reflex, zero coordination cost); Gate 2 (vector similarity) has moderate cost; Gate 3 (unresolved) falls back to γ (requiring external resolution). See [Architecture](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: lever-runner-wasm
CONCEPTS: wavelet, graph, crypto, compute, storage, math, search
DEFINITION: A WebAssembly build of the lever-runner intent-to-command carapace, providing near-native performance for intent hashing, embedding, vector similarity search, and a three-gate routing pipeline — all running entirely in the browser with zero server round-trips.
SECTIONS: Lever Runner WASM | Why It Matters | How It Works | Intent Hashing (BLAKE2b-128) | Position-Aware Character Embedding | Vector Search: Cosine Similarity | Three-Gate Pipeline | Complexity | Quick Start | API | Exported WASM Functions | Architecture Notes
MATH: Lever-runner WASM offers a **third path**: a complete intent-resolution engine compiled to WebAssembly, running at near-native speed inside the browser. The three-gate pipeline (exact hash → vector si | $$h = \text{BLAKE2b}_{128}(\text{intent})$$ | BLAKE2b is a cryptographic hash function faster than MD5 and SHA-1, with a proven security margin. The hash provides `O(1)` exact-match lookup via hash-table comparison. | $$w_{\text{pos}}(i) = 1 + \frac{2}{1 + i}$$ | $$\text{bucket}(c_i, c_{i+1}) = (c_i \cdot 31 + c_{i+1}) \mod 16$$
ARCHITECTURE: The library satisfies **γ + η = C** in edge computing: - **γ (correctness)**: the three-gate cascade provides a correctness floor. Gate 1 never produces false positives (exact hash match). Gate 2 uses a calibrated cosine threshold (0.75) balancing precision and recall. Gate 3 provides a deterministic keyword fallback — never returning empty. - **η (efficiency)**: the entire pipeline runs in WASM with no I/O. The `opt-level = "z"` release profile and LTO minimize binary size for fast initial page load. Vector search is `O(n)` with $d=64$ — fast enough for thousands of commands.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: lexer-gen
CONCEPTS: crypto, protocol, math, search
DEFINITION: A lightweight, composable lexer generator for Rust. Builds tokenizers from rule definitions using character predicates, producing typed tokens with source spans. No build step, no proc macros — just fluent Rust.
SECTIONS: Lexer Gen | Why It Matters | How It Works | Tokenization Model | Token Types | Character Predicates | Single-Char vs. Multi-Char Rules | Complexity | Rule Priority | Quick Start | API | Lexer Builder
MATH: - **`O(n)` throughput** — single-pass tokenization with no backtracking. | | `add_rule` / `add_multi_rule` | `O(1)` | Append to rule vector | | | `tokenize(input)` | `O(n \cdot r)` | $n$ = input length, $r$ = number of rules | | | Space per token | `O(\|text\|)` | String storage + 3 usizes | | With a fixed rule set, tokenization is `O(n)` — optimal for any scanner.
ARCHITECTURE: The library embodies **γ + η = C** in compiler tooling: - **γ (correctness)**: every character is consumed exactly once — no characters are skipped or double-counted. Unrecognized characters produce `Error` tokens rather than silently dropping. The `Eof` sentinel ensures parsers can detect end-of-input without length checks. - **η (efficiency)**: `O(n)` single-pass tokenization with no backtracking. Rules are stored in a `Vec` and evaluated linearly — for small rule sets (< 20), the branch predictor makes this faster than a hash-table-based dispatch. Builder pattern enables rule definition without temporary variables.

DEFINITION: A Rust library implementing Unicode line breaking utilities based on UAX #14 (Unicode Standard Annex #14: Unicode Line Breaking Algorithm). O(N/B) for the lines vector. | Line breaking is a **γ** (rendering transform) applied to **text** (**η**) to produce **wrapped output** (**C**). The quality of the transform determines readability: poorly broken text is hard to par
ARCHITECTURE: Line breaking is a **γ** (rendering transform) applied to **text** (**η**) to produce **wrapped output** (**C**). The quality of the transform determines readability: poorly broken text is hard to parse visually. In the γ + η = C framework, the break algorithm's job is to preserve the author's meaning while fitting physical constraints (screen width). The full UAX #14 algorithm handles the tension between these constraints across all human languages — a problem far harder than it appears.

MATH: - Routing: O(1) lookup table | - Generation prompt: O(P) where P = problem string length | - Debate protocol construction: O(R) where R = rounds | - Linguistic experiment generation: O(L · P) where L = number of languages | Polyformalism is a **meta-γ**: it generates multiple cognitive transforms (**γ₁, γ₂, ..., γₙ**) and applies each to the same problem (**η**), producing a diverse solution set (**C₁, C₂, ..., Cₙ**). Th
ARCHITECTURE: Polyformalism is a **meta-γ**: it generates multiple cognitive transforms (**γ₁, γ₂, ..., γₙ**) and applies each to the same problem (**η**), producing a diverse solution set (**C₁, C₂, ..., Cₙ**). The synthesis step finds insights that emerge only from the intersection — the whole is greater than any part. The γ + η = C principle scales recursively: each linguistic frame is itself a γ that restructures η into a different C. The debate protocol is a γ-oscillator: it alternates between generative and evaluative transforms to explore the solution space systematically rather than greedily.

CRATE: linking-number
CONCEPTS: conservation, graph
DEFINITION: A Rust library for computing the linking number and writhe — topological invariants of knot theory that describe how closed curves interlock and twist in three-dimensional space. The linking number counts how many times one closed curve wraps around another; the writhe measures the self-twisting of a single closed curve.
SECTIONS: linking-number | Why It Matters | How It Works | Crossing-Sign Convention | Linking Number Formula | Writhe | Gauss Linking Integral | Properties | Time Complexity | Quick Start | API | `linking_number(crossings: &[i32]) -> i32`
MATH: | Sum crossing signs | O(N) where N = crossings | | | Gauss integral (discretized) | O(M²) where M = segments per curve | | | **Space** | O(N) for crossing list | | The linking number is a **topological invariant (η)** — it is a property of the curves themselves, independent of projection or measurement. The crossing-sign computation (**γ**) extracts this invaria
ARCHITECTURE: The linking number is a **topological invariant (η)** — it is a property of the curves themselves, independent of projection or measurement. The crossing-sign computation (**γ**) extracts this invariant from a diagrammatic representation. The composition γ + η = C holds: the computed Lk (C) is the same regardless of which projection you use, making it a reliable discriminator. This invariance under transformation is what makes topological invariants powerful — they are immune to noise in the representation, unlike geometric features (distances, angles) that change under deformation.

ARCHITECTURE: The library implements **γ + η = C** in compiler infrastructure: - **γ (correctness)**: the Rust type system enforces the IR type hierarchy at compile time. `IrType::Ptr(Box<IrType>)` ensures pointer types always carry their pointee type. The instruction enum guarantees that only well-formed instructions can be constructed. - **η (efficiency)**: all structures are plain enums and structs with no allocation beyond `String` and `Vec` — the minimum needed for variable-length names and instruction lists. Serialization to textual IR is `O(n)`.

ARCHITECTURE: The framework satisfies **γ + η = C** in compiler optimization: - **γ (correctness)**: each pass preserves IR validity — transformations produce well-formed IR that could be consumed by any LLVM-compatible backend. The `PassResult` honestly reports whether the module was modified, enabling pipeline convergence detection. - **η (efficiency)**: each pass runs in `O(n)` where $n$ is the instruction count. The `PassManager` avoids redundant work by checking `modified` flags — if a pass didn't change anything, dependent passes can short-circuit.

Data layout captures the ABI constraints that affect codegen correctness. - **η (efficiency)**: all types are `Clone + Debug` with no heap allocation beyond `String` fields. MATH: **RoundRobin** — An `AtomicUsize` counter, incremented with `Relaxed` ordering (no lock needed). On each request, filter to healthy targets and pick `counter % healthy_count`. O(n) for the health filt | **WeightedRandom** — Uses a deficit-based algorithm (similar to smooth weighted round-robin). Maintains a `RwLock<Vec<f64>>` tracking how much each target is "owed." On each selection, picks the targe | **LeastConnections** — Stateless. Picks the healthy target with the lowest `active_connections` count. O(n) scan. Best for long-lived connections (WebSocket, gRPC streaming) where round-robin would pi | **TernaryAffinity** — A novel strategy that computes the **trit sum** of the source string: for each byte, compute `(byte % 3) - 1` (producing -1, 0, or +1) and sum. This creates a ternary hash of the | - **`RoundRobin`** — Lock-free counter. O(n) health filter + O(1) selection.

Sits in front of backend pools, routing requests authenticated by [JWT claims](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md), with health status updated by the [liveness-check](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md) system and traces propagated by the [Jaeger client](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md). CONCEPTS: conservation, fleet, graph, crypto, storage, math, search
DEFINITION: A Rust library for collecting, merging, and ordering structured log entries from multiple sources. Implements a merge-on-insert pattern where incoming log batches are appended and re-sorted by timestamp, maintaining a globally consistent chronological order suitable for audit trails, observability pipelines, and event-sourced systems.
SECTIONS: log-collector | Why It Matters | How It Works | Data Model | Merge Algorithm | Sorting Analysis | Alternative: Priority Queue Merge | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Quick Start | API | `LogEntry`
MATH: entries.extend(batch);             // Append: O(B) | entries.sort_by_key(|e| e.timestamp);  // Sort: O(N log N) | Timsort is O(N) in the best case (already sorted or nearly sorted) and O(N log N) in the worst case. For log collection, incoming batches are often nearly sorted (each batch is internally ordered by t | | Batch already sorted | O(B) for merge | Normal operation (source emits in order) | | | Batch fully reversed | O(N log N) | Clock skew correction after NTP sync |
ARCHITECTURE: The log collector is the **temporal aggregator (γ)** that imposes order on the chaos of distributed log emission (**η**). Without this transform, logs from different services arrive in arbitrary order, making causal analysis impossible. The sorted log (**C**) is the foundation of observability: dashboards, alerts, and audit reports all depend on chronological consistency. In the γ + η = C framework, the collector is a one-way valve: entries are append-only, and the sort is idempotent (re-sorting an already-sorted log is O(N) with Timsort). This idempotency makes the collector safe for at-least-once delivery semantics in distributed pipelines.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: loss-function
CONCEPTS: math
DEFINITION: **A Rust library implementing the four most common loss functions for machine-learning model training: Mean Squared Error, Binary Cross-Entropy, Categorical Cross-Entropy, and Huber Loss.** Each function is the differentiable objective that gradient descent minimizes to learn model parameters.
SECTIONS: Loss Function | Why It Matters | How It Works | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | License
MATH: **Mean Squared Error** (`mse`) — `(1/n) Σ (ŷ_i - y_i)²`. Convex, differentiable everywhere, gradient `∇ = 2(ŷ - y)/n`. Heavily penalizes large errors (quadratic), making it outlier-sensitive. O(n). | **Binary Cross-Entropy** (`binary_cross_entropy`) — `-(1/n) Σ [y·log(ŷ) + (1-y)·log(1-ŷ)]`. Convex for fixed y. Gradient w.r.t. ŷ: `(ŷ - y) / (ŷ(1-ŷ))`. This is the negative log-likelihood of a Bernou | **Categorical Cross-Entropy** (`categorical_cross_entropy`) — `-Σ y_i · log(ŷ_i)`. Only the true class contributes (since `y_i = 0` for all other classes). Gradient w.r.t. ŷ_k: `-y_k/ŷ_k`. Combined wi | The parameter δ controls the transition point. The gradient is continuous at the transition (unlike L1). O(n). Huber is also called "smooth L1" — it's the default loss for object detection (Fast R-CNN | All implementations are O(n) single-pass with assertions that predicted and target arrays have equal length.
ARCHITECTURE: Provides the training objectives for SuperInstance ML pipelines. Pairs with [learning-rate](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md) schedules (which control the optimizer step size) and the [Mahalanobis distance](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md) (for outlier-aware loss weighting). See the [architecture overview](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: lotka-volterra-agents-c
CONCEPTS: conservation, fleet, compute, math, search
DEFINITION: The **Lotka-Volterra (LV) equations** are a system of coupled ordinary differential equations that model how populations of interacting species change over time. Originally formulated by Alfred Lotka (1925) and Vito Volterra (1926) for predator-prey pairs, the generalized N-species form describes competition, mutualism, and parasitism across an entire ecosystem. This library implements the generalized LV system in portable C with **Euler** and **RK4** numerical integration, analytical equilibrium computation, perturbation testing, and ecological diversity metrics.
SECTIONS: Lotka-Volterra Agents — Multi-Species Ecological Dynamics in C | Why It Matters | How It Works | The Generalized Lotka-Volterra Equation | Numerical Integration: Euler vs RK4 | Equilibrium Analysis | Perturbation Testing | Shannon Diversity Index | Quick Start | Build | Run all 15 tests | include "lv_agents.h"
MATH: Local truncation error: **O(dt²)**. Global error: **O(dt)**. Stable for dt < 1/rₘₐₓ. | Local truncation error: **O(dt⁵)**. Global error: **O(dt⁴)**. The library's tests confirm RK4 converges to equilibrium at least as fast as Euler for the same step count. | | Euler | 1 | O(N²) | | | RK4 | 4 | O(N²) | | Each evaluation of f requires computing the competition sum Σⱼ αᵢⱼ · Nⱼ for each species — an O(N²) matrix-vector product. For N = 32 (the library maximum), this is 1,024 multiply-adds per evaluation,
ARCHITECTURE: This C library is the **on-device ecological engine** for the SuperInstance constellation. In the conservation law **γ + η = C**, Lotka-Volterra dynamics describe how γ (generation energy) distributes across agent species. When γ is partitioned among competing agent types, the LV equations predict which types will thrive, which will go extinct, and how the system responds to shocks. The library runs on edge devices alongside `strategy-ecology-c`, which adds game-theoretic strategy interactions on top of the LV dynamics. Together they form the **ecological prediction layer** that lets a room anticipate which agent configurations will be stable before deploying them. See the [SuperInstance Architecture](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

CRATE: lru-cache-atomic
CONCEPTS: conservation, graph, crypto, storage, systems, search
DEFINITION: A thread-safe, lock-based LRU (Least Recently Used) cache for Rust. Uses a mutex-protected doubly-linked list backed by a `HashMap` for `O(1)` access, insertion, and eviction — safe for concurrent shared use across threads.
SECTIONS: LRU Cache Atomic | Why It Matters | How It Works | Data Structure: Hash + Linked List | Operations | Get (Touch) | Put (Insert) | Eviction | Complexity | Thread Safety | Quick Start | API
MATH: A thread-safe, lock-based LRU (Least Recently Used) cache for Rust. Uses a mutex-protected doubly-linked list backed by a `HashMap` for `O(1)` access, insertion, and eviction — safe for concurrent sha | 1. **`HashMap<K, usize>`**: maps keys to indices in the entry array — provides `O(1)` lookup. | 2. **Doubly-linked list** (via `Vec<Option<Entry>>`): orders entries by recency — provides `O(1)` reordering. | 1. Look up key in HashMap → entry index. `O(1)` | 2. Detach entry from its current position. `O(1)`
ARCHITECTURE: The cache implements **γ + η = C** in concurrent data structures: - **γ (correctness)**: the `Mutex` ensures all operations are atomic — no torn reads, no lost updates, no ABA problems. The doubly-linked list invariants (head.prev == None, tail.next == None, ring is consistent) are maintained under all operation sequences. The free-list prevents use-after-free of entry slots. - **η (efficiency)**: all operations are `O(1)` amortized. Slot recycling via the free-list avoids `Vec` growth after warmup. The closure-based `get` minimizes lock hold time — the caller copies what it needs inside the critical section.

ARCHITECTURE: Lucineer is the companion application to LucidDreamer AI — same deployment pattern (Cloudflare Worker, single HTML response), different aesthetic and focus. Lucineer emphasizes practice tracking; LucidDreamer emphasizes dream journaling. Both serve the SuperInstance web portfolio. In **γ + η = C**, serverless deployment eliminates infrastructure γ entirely. See [Architecture](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md). **Dream sign methodology**: Dream signs are recurring elements in dreams — themes, locations, people, or situations that appear repeatedly. Lucineer tracks these with occurrence counts, enabling data-driven trigger selection. The technique: when you encounter a dream sign while awake (e.g., a house with unusual architecture), perform a reality check. This conditional habit is far more effective than random checks because it targets your personal dream patterns. **Progress tracking**: The monthly lucid rate chart provides long-term feedback. Rather than focusing on individual dream success/failure, the trend line reveals whether overall practice is working. A 2.3× improvement over four months (12% → 28%) represents realistic, sustainable progress.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: memory-mapper
CONCEPTS: crypto, compute, storage, protocol, systems, search
DEFINITION: A virtual-to-physical memory mapping library with page table abstraction. Provides address translation, permission flags, and TLB simulation for operating system kernels, hypervisors, and memory management units (MMUs).
SECTIONS: Memory Mapper | Why It Matters | How It Works | Page Table Model | Page Size | Permission Flags | Address Translation | TLB Simulation | Complexity | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes
MATH: $$\text{VAddr} = \underbrace{\text{VPN}}_{\text{bits 31–12}} \cdot 2^{12} + \underbrace{\text{offset}}_{\text{bits 11–0}}$$ | $$\text{PAddr} = \text{PFN} \cdot 2^{12} + \text{offset}$$ | $$\text{PAGE\_SIZE} = 4096 = 2^{12}$$ | This is `O(1)` — a single hash lookup. Real hardware does a multi-level page table walk (4 levels on x86-64), which is `O(4)` but still constant time. | | `map_page` | `O(1)` | HashMap insert |
ARCHITECTURE: The library implements **γ + η = C** in memory management: - **γ (correctness)**: the VPN/PFN masking ensures offsets are always preserved during translation. The `bitflags` crate provides type-safe flag composition — invalid flag combinations are caught at compile time. The global `Mutex<HashMap>` ensures concurrent map/translate operations don't race. - **η (efficiency)**: all operations are `O(1)` hash lookups. The page table grows only with the number of mapped pages, not the size of the address space. The `lazy_static` global avoids repeated initialization.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: mempool-arena
CONCEPTS: conservation, graph, crypto, protocol, math, systems
DEFINITION: An arena (bump allocator) memory pool for Rust. Provides `O(1)` allocation with bulk deallocation via reset — ideal for short-lived scopes such as request handlers, render passes, and compilation phases.
SECTIONS: Mempool Arena | Why It Matters | How It Works | Bump Allocation | Alignment | Reset | Memory Safety | Complexity | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References
MATH: An arena (bump allocator) memory pool for Rust. Provides `O(1)` allocation with bulk deallocation via reset — ideal for short-lived scopes such as request handlers, render passes, and compilation phas | The standard allocator (`malloc`/`free`, `jemalloc`) is a general-purpose beast. It must handle arbitrary-size allocations and frees in any order, requiring `O(log n)` or worse per operation. But many | An arena exploits this by **bumping a pointer** for each allocation and **resetting the pointer** for bulk deallocation. Both are `O(1)` — no free list, no fragmentation, no metadata per allocation. | $$\text{aligned}(p) = (p + 7) \ \& \ \neg 7$$ | This satisfies the alignment requirement of `u64`, `f64`, and most Rust types (which are at most 8-byte aligned). The padding bytes between allocations are wasted (internal fragmentation), but the tot
ARCHITECTURE: The arena implements **γ + η = C** in memory management: - **γ (correctness)**: `Drop` guarantees the underlying allocation is freed — no leaks. `alloc` returns `None` when full rather than panicking or overrunning. Pointer arithmetic respects alignment. The `Cell<*mut u8>` interior mutability is sound because the arena outlives all borrowed pointers (enforced by Rust's borrow checker at the usage site). - **η (efficiency)**: allocation is 3 instructions (align, add, compare). No free list, no coalescing, no per-allocation metadata. Memory locality is optimal — all allocations are contiguous in a single cache-friendly block.

CRATE: mempool-buddy
CONCEPTS: crypto, compute, protocol, systems
DEFINITION: A buddy-system memory allocator implementing power-of-two block splitting and coalescing. The classic algorithm described by Knowlton (1965), used in the Linux kernel's `__alloc_pages()` and in FreeBSD's `uma` zone allocator.
SECTIONS: Mempool Buddy | Why It Matters | How It Works | Block Orders | Allocation | Deallocation and Coalescing | Internal Fragmentation | Complexity | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | R
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: mempool-buddy
CONCEPTS: crypto, compute, protocol, systems
DEFINITION: A buddy-system memory allocator implementing power-of-two block splitting and coalescing. The classic algorithm described by Knowlton (1965), used in the Linux kernel's `__alloc_pages()` and in FreeBSD's `uma` zone allocator.
SECTIONS: Mempool Buddy | Why It Matters | How It Works | Block Orders | Allocation | Deallocation and Coalescing | Internal Fragmentation | Complexity | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References
MATH: 1. **Fast coalescing**: when a block is freed, its "buddy" (address XOR block_size) is checked. If free, they merge — `O(\log n)` to potentially coalesce all the way back to the full block. | $$\text{order}(s) = \lceil \log_2(s) \rceil$$ | $$\text{buddy}(a, k) = a \oplus 2^k$$ | $$\text{waste ratio} = \frac{2^k - s}{2^k} < \frac{2^{k-1}}{2^k} = 50\%$$ | | `allocate(s)` | `O(\log(total/min))` | Split down from higher order |
ARCHITECTURE: The allocator implements **γ + η = C** in memory management: - **γ (correctness)**: the power-of-two invariant is enforced at construction (`assert!(total_size.is_power_of_two())`). Splitting and coalescing use the XOR buddy formula, which is provably correct for aligned blocks. The free-list-per-order structure ensures blocks of different sizes are never confused. - **η (efficiency)**: allocation and deallocation are `O(\log(total/min))` — typically 5–10 iterations. No external metadata is needed beyond the free lists themselves. The split bitmap enables `O(1)` checks for whether a block has been split.

CRATE: mempool-thread-local

DEFINITION: A thread-local memory pool with fast per-thread bump allocation and a central pool for buffer distribution. Eliminates synchronization overhead for single-threaded allocation patterns while maintaining a shared reserve for buffer lifecycle management.

MATH: - **Tier 1 (local)**: Each thread has private bump-allocator buffers — zero contention, zero synchronization, `O(1)` allocation. | This is the same `O(1)` bump allocation as `mempool-arena`, but scoped to a thread. | Access is `O(1)` — no atomics, no locks. The TLS (Thread-Local Storage) segment is set up by the OS at thread creation time. | | `alloc(size)` (local hit) | `O(b)` where $b$ = buffers per thread | Zero (no lock) | | | `alloc(size)` (central refill) | `O(1)` + mutex | One lock acquisition |
ARCHITECTURE: The library implements **γ + η = C** in concurrent memory allocation: - **γ (correctness)**: the `thread_local!` macro ensures each thread's buffers are isolated — no data races. The `RefCell` provides dynamic borrowing checks for the per-thread vector. The central pool's `Mutex` ensures atomic acquire/release. Buffer reset prevents use-after-free: once returned to central, old pointers are invalid. - **η (efficiency)**: the fast path (local buffer hit) is lock-free — just pointer arithmetic. The slow path (central refill) acquires the mutex once per buffer, amortizing the lock cost over thousands of allocations. The steady-state allocation rate approaches that of a single-threaded arena.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: mesh-simplification
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, graph, compute, storage, math
DEFINITION: A Rust implementation of **Quadric Error Metrics (QEM)** for triangle mesh simplification, following the landmark Garland–Heckbert algorithm. The library computes per-vertex quadric matrices from face plane equations, enabling edge-collapse decisions that minimize geometric error.
SECTIONS: Mesh Simplification | Why It Matters | How It Works | Plane Quadric | Per-Vertex Quadrics | Quadric Error | Edge Collapse | Complexity | Quick Start | API | `Mesh::plane_quadric(v0, v1, v2) -> Matrix4` | `Mesh::vertex_quadrics() -> Vec<Matrix4>`
MATH: The QEM approach is the industry standard because it produces high-quality results with O(n log n) complexity. | | Compute all plane quadrics | O(F) | O(F) | | | Accumulate vertex quadrics | O(F) | O(V) | | | Evaluate error at position | O(1) | O(1) — fixed 4×4 | | | Full edge-collapse sequence | O(E log E) | O(V + E) |
ARCHITECTURE: The implementation stores 4×4 symmetric matrices as full 16-element arrays (not exploiting symmetry). The quadric accumulation correctly sums contributions from all incident faces per vertex, and the error evaluation uses the full bilinear form v̄ᵀ Q v̄. The **γ + η = C** ternary classification: each vertex is either **(γ) on all its incident planes** (error = 0, interior to the surface) or **(η) at some distance** (error > 0, approximation introduced). The total surface approximation error C = Σ_v Δ(v̄_v) bounds the simplification quality.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: minkowski-metric
CONCEPTS: ternary, graph, crypto, math, search
DEFINITION: A Rust library for computing **Minkowski distances** (generalized L_p norms) between numerical vectors. The Minkowski distance of order p ≥ 1 is a proper metric that unifies Manhattan (L₁), Euclidean (L₂), and Chebyshev (L∞) distances under a single parameterized formula.
SECTIONS: Minkowski Metric | Why It Matters | How It Works | Definition | Special Cases | Metric Properties | Convergence as p → ∞ | Complexity | Quick Start | API | `minkowski_distance(a, b, p) -> f64` | `manhattan_distance(a, b) -> f64`
MATH: | L_p distance (general) | O(n) | O(1) | | | Manhattan (L₁) | O(n) | O(1) | | | Euclidean (L₂) | O(n) | O(1) | | | Chebyshev (L∞) | O(n) | O(1) | | The Chebyshev convenience function avoids the `powf(∞)` approach and instead uses `fold(f64::max)` for an exact O(n) computation with no floating-point precision loss from exponentiation.
ARCHITECTURE: The Chebyshev convenience function avoids the `powf(∞)` approach and instead uses `fold(f64::max)` for an exact O(n) computation with no floating-point precision loss from exponentiation. The **γ + η = C** ternary classification: the parameter p partitions the space of metrics into **(γ) p = 1** (sparse/robust regime, Manhattan), **(η) p = ∞** (uniform/worst-case regime, Chebyshev), and the **continuum C** = {p ∈ [1, ∞)} interpolating between them. The choice of p controls the bias-variance tradeoff in ML applications.

documents.knowledge_doc:

DEFINITION: A Rust library for **Morse theory** — the study of the relationship between the topology of a smooth manifold and the critical points of smooth functions defined on it. This library implements Morse functions, critical point enumeration, Morse polynomials, and the Morse inequalities.
SECTIONS: Morse Theory | Why It Matters | How It Works | Morse Functions | The Morse Polynomial | The Morse Inequalities | Perfect Morse Functions | Complexity | Quick Start | API | `MorseFunction::new(dimension) -> Self` | `MorseFunction::add_critical_point(index, value)`
MATH: | Add critical point | O(1) | | | Count by index | O(m) where m = # critical points | | | Compute Morse polynomial | O(m) | | | Check weak inequalities | O(min(m, dim)) | | The **γ + η = C** ternary classification: critical points are **(γ) ordinary** (non-degenerate, index well-defined) or **(η) degenerate** (Hessian singular, Catastrophe theory territory). Together the
ARCHITECTURE: The library stores critical points as a flat vector of `(index, value)` pairs. The Morse polynomial is computed by bucket-sorting by index in a single pass. The **γ + η = C** ternary classification: critical points are **(γ) ordinary** (non-degenerate, index well-defined) or **(η) degenerate** (Hessian singular, Catastrophe theory territory). Together they form all critical points C = {p : ∇f(p) = 0}. This library handles only γ-type critical points (true Morse functions).

CONCEPTS: conservation, crypto, systems
DEFINITION: **Navier-Stokes** is a Rust library implementing numerical solvers for the Navier-Stokes equations — the partial differential equations governing fluid dynamics — featuring finite-difference discretization, explicit time integration, and pressure projection via the Poisson equation.
SECTIONS: Navier-Stokes | Why It Matters | How It Works | The Navier-Stokes Equations | Finite-Difference Discretization | Chorin's Projection
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: navier-stokes
CONCEPTS: conservation, crypto, systems
DEFINITION: **Navier-Stokes** is a Rust library implementing numerical solvers for the Navier-Stokes equations — the partial differential equations governing fluid dynamics — featuring finite-difference discretization, explicit time integration, and pressure projection via the Poisson equation.
SECTIONS: Navier-Stokes | Why It Matters | How It Works | The Navier-Stokes Equations | Finite-Difference Discretization | Chorin's Projection Method | Stability (CFL Condition) | Complexity Summary | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References
MATH: ∂u/∂x ≈ (u[i+1,j] - u[i-1,j]) / (2Δx)       (central difference, O(Δx²)) | Grid size: N×M cells. Memory: **O(N × M)**. Each time step evaluates every grid point: **O(N × M)**. | Convergence: **O(k × N × M)** for k Jacobi iterations. Typically k = 20-50 for acceptable residual. | | Advection | O(N × M) | | | Diffusion | O(N × M) |
ARCHITECTURE: Navier-Stokes provides the physical simulation layer for fluid dynamics in SuperInstance. In γ + η = C, the momentum equation's convective term `(u·∇)u` represents γ (growth — energy transfer through motion) while the viscous term `ν∇²u` represents η (avoidance — energy dissipation through friction). The conservation constant C is the total kinetic energy, conserved in the inviscid limit (ν → 0). The incompressibility constraint `∇·u = 0` is the kinematic conservation law. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md) for physical simulation architecture.

ARCHITECTURE: Negative Space Core is a foundational theory crate in SuperInstance. The 294:1 conservation law mirrors the broader γ + η = C principle (growth + exploration = conservation): what is avoided (η) and what is explored (γ) are conserved across the system. The `TernaryState` values {-1 (avoid), 0 (explore), +1 (choose)} in `ternary-agent` directly map to this framework's decision types. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md) for the full conservation law hierarchy.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: netlink-socket
CONCEPTS: graph, compute, protocol, systems
DEFINITION: A low-level Rust abstraction over **Linux netlink sockets** — the kernel-to-userland communication mechanism used for network configuration, route table access, interface management, firewall rules (conntrack), and uevent monitoring. This crate provides a minimal, unsafe-free-at-the-API-boundary wrapper around the raw `AF_NETLINK` socket operations.
SECTIONS: Netlink Socket | Why It Matters | How It Works | Socket Creation | Address Binding | Message Format | Complexity | Quick Start | API | `NetlinkSocket::new(protocol) -> io::Result<Self>` | `NetlinkSocket::bind(groups) -> io::Result<()>` | `NetlinkSocket::send(buf, dst) -> io::Result<usize>`
MATH: | Socket creation | O(1) — one syscall | O(1) | | | Bind | O(1) — one syscall | O(1) | | | Send message | O(n) where n = message size | O(n) | | | Receive message | O(n) | O(n) | | The **γ + η = C** ternary classification: each netlink message is either **(γ) a request** (userspace → kernel, e.g. "give me the route table") or **(η) a notification** (kernel → userspace, e.g. "a n
ARCHITECTURE: The socket wrapper is intentionally thin — it handles `socket(2)`, `bind(2)`, `sendto(2)`, and `close(2)` but leaves message construction and parsing to higher layers. This follows the Unix philosophy: a building block, not a framework. The RAII pattern in `Drop` ensures that file descriptors are never leaked, even on panic. The `AsRawFd` impl enables integration with async runtimes via `tokio::io::unix::AsyncFd`. The **γ + η = C** ternary classification: each netlink message is either **(γ) a request** (userspace → kernel, e.g. "give me the route table") or **(η) a notification** (kernel → userspace, e.g. "a new interface appeared"). Together they form the complete netlink communication contract C between the kernel networking subsystem and user-space processes.

SECTIONS: Neuron Layer | Why It Matters | How It Works | Forward Pass | Complexity | No Activation | Weight Initialization | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References | License
MATH: In matrix notation: `y = Wx + b`. This is an affine transformation — a linear projection followed by a translation. The computational cost is O(m·n) multiply-adds per forward pass. | | Forward pass | O(m · n) | O(m) output | | | Weight storage | — | O(m · n) | | | Initialization | O(m · n) | — | | This crate provides the linear-combination primitive for SuperInstance's ternary neural stacks. When combined with ternary quantization (weights ∈ {-1, 0, +1}), the forward pass reduces from O(m·n) mu
ARCHITECTURE: This crate provides the linear-combination primitive for SuperInstance's ternary neural stacks. When combined with ternary quantization (weights ∈ {-1, 0, +1}), the forward pass reduces from O(m·n) multiply-adds to O(m·n) sign-and-add operations — a 4× memory reduction and elimination of floating-point multipliers. In the γ + η = C framework, the neuron layer represents γ (constructive projection): it transforms and combines signals, while η (negative-space inference) operates on what the layer's output does *not* activate. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

ARCHITECTURE: Object Pool provides the memory substrate for γ (gamma) — the constructive operations in SuperInstance that create and transform data. documents.name: octree-spatial
documents.description:
CRATE: octree-spatial
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, graph, compute, storage, search
DEFINITION: A Rust implementation of a **pointer-based octree** for spatial indexing of 3D points. Supports dynamic insertion, spherical range queries, and k-nearest-neighbor (k-NN) search using max-heap pruning. The octree subdivides 3D space into eight octants recursively, enabling O(log N) average-case queries for uniformly distributed points.
SECTIONS: Octree Spatial Index | Why It Matters | How It Works |
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: octree-spatial
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, graph, compute, storage, search
DEFINITION: A Rust implementation of a **pointer-based octree** for spatial indexing of 3D points. Supports dynamic insertion, spherical range queries, and k-nearest-neighbor (k-NN) search using max-heap pruning. The octree subdivides 3D space into eight octants recursively, enabling O(log N) average-case queries for uniformly distributed points.
SECTIONS: Octree Spatial Index | Why It Matters | How It Works | Structure | Insertion | Range Query | K-Nearest Neighbors | Complexity | Quick Start | API | `Octree::new(bounds: BBox) -> Self` | `Octree::insert(pt: Point3)`
MATH: A Rust implementation of a **pointer-based octree** for spatial indexing of 3D points. Supports dynamic insertion, spherical range queries, and k-nearest-neighbor (k-NN) search using max-heap pruning. | | Insert | O(log₈ N) | O(N) — all points in one octant | | | Range query (radius r) | O(N^(2/3) + k) | O(N) — r covers entire tree | | | k-NN | O(log₈ N + k) | O(N) | | | Space | O(N) | O(N) |
ARCHITECTURE: The tree uses an `enum Node` with `Leaf { points: Vec<Point3> }` and `Branch { children: Box<[Option<Node>; 8]> }` variants. The `Option<Node>` allows sparse octants to consume no memory. The `OrderedFloat` wrapper provides a total order on `f64` for use in `BinaryHeap`. Constants `MAX_LEAF = 16` and `MIN_HALF = 1e-6` control the split threshold and recursion depth limit. The **γ + η = C** ternary classification: each query result is either **(γ) a true positive** (point is genuinely within the query radius) or **(η) a false positive** (point is in a node that overlaps the query region but is outside the radius — currently minimized because the range query tests exact distances). The total C = γ + η = candidate set size, and the precision γ/C approaches 1 as the tree subdivision aligns with the query region.

CONCEPTS: conservation, crypto, protocol, search
DEFINITION: **OGG Decoder** is a Rust library for parsing the OGG container format — decoding page headers, segment tables, and granule positions from OGG bitstreams without decoding the encapsulated audio codec (Vorbis, Opus, FLAC).
SECTIONS: OGG Decoder | Why It Matters | How It Works | OGG Page Structure | Header Type Flags | Segment Table | Granule Position | Page Iteration | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References
MATH: Header parsing: **O(1)** fixed 27 bytes. Total page size: `27 + N + Σ segment_sizes`. | Helper methods: `is_beginning_of_stream()`, `is_end_of_stream()`, `is_continued()`. Each: **O(1)** bitmask check. | Segment table parsing: **O(N)** where N = number of segments. Segment data extraction: **O(D)** where D = total data bytes. | Full stream parsing: **O(P × (27 + N + D))** ≈ **O(S)** where S = total stream size. Each byte is read exactly once. | | `parse_page` | `fn(data: &[u8], offset: usize) -> Result<(OggPage, usize), String>` | O(27 + N + D) |
ARCHITECTURE: OGG Decoder provides the media container parsing layer for audio processing in SuperInstance. In γ + η = C, the granule position represents γ (growth — temporal progression through the media stream), the EOS flag signals η (avoidance — stream termination), and the segment table enforces C (conservation — every byte belongs to exactly one segment, no gaps or overlaps). Integrates with `op-codec` for binary encoding patterns and `octree-spatial` for multidimensional data structures. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md) for media processing architecture.

CONCEPTS: ternary, protocol, math, systems
DEFINITION: **Op Codec** is a binary opcode encoder/decoder for stack-machine bytecode — providing one-byte instruction prefixes, little-endian immediate values, varint-encoded jump targets, round-trip verification, and disassembly with address annotations.
SECTIONS: Op Codec | Why It Matters | How It Works | Instruction Encoding | Varint Encoding | Round-Trip Guarantee | Disassembly | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: op-codec
CONCEPTS: ternary, protocol, math, systems
DEFINITION: **Op Codec** is a binary opcode encoder/decoder for stack-machine bytecode — providing one-byte instruction prefixes, little-endian immediate values, varint-encoded jump targets, round-trip verification, and disassembly with address annotations.
SECTIONS: Op Codec | Why It Matters | How It Works | Instruction Encoding | Varint Encoding | Round-Trip Guarantee | Disassembly | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References | License
MATH: Encoding cost: **O(1)** for fixed-size opcodes, **O(log addr)** for varint-encoded jumps. | Encoding/decoding: **O(k)** where k = ⌈log₂(addr) / 7⌉. For addresses < 128: 1 byte. For addresses < 16,384: 2 bytes. | Linear scan: **O(N)** for N bytes. No ambiguity — the encoding is prefix-free (no opcode is a prefix of another except through explicit operand consumption). | Op Codec provides the bytecode encoding layer for the ternary VM in SuperInstance. In γ + η = C, encoding represents γ (growth — translating high-level intent to compact executable form) while decodin
ARCHITECTURE: Op Codec provides the bytecode encoding layer for the ternary VM in SuperInstance. In γ + η = C, encoding represents γ (growth — translating high-level intent to compact executable form) while decoding represents η (avoidance — rejecting malformed byte sequences with errors). The varint encoding conserves space: short programs stay short. Integrates with `ternary-compiler-optimizer` for optimization passes on encoded bytecode. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md) for VM bytecode architecture.

ARCHITECTURE: The ESP32 Bridge is the motor cortex of the OpenMind system in SuperInstance. documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: openmind-mirror
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, fleet, graph, crypto, storage, systems, search
DEFINITION: **Metacognition for AI agents** — self-reflection and coherence checking for agent muscle memory. OpenMind Mirror watches an agent's compiled reflexes (its "muscle memory"), detects when they go stale or inconsistent, and recommends calibration adjustments based on observed performance.
SECTIONS: OpenMind Mirror | Why It Matters | How It Works | Chord Shapes | The Trit Decision Model | Module: Coherence | Module: Mirror (Version Diff) | Module: Calibration | Module: Proprioceptor | Complexity | Quick Start | API
MATH: Each chord uses a **ternary decision** (γ + η = C): | The sum γ + η = C: the total invocation space C is partitioned into **(γ) hardcoded calls** and **(η) model-delegated calls**. The calibration system optimizes to maximize γ (speed) while maintaining  | | Coherence check | O(V + E) where V = chords, E = call edges | | | Mirror diff | O(V₁ + V₂) | | | Calibration analysis | O(V) |
ARCHITECTURE: The muscle memory is a **directed graph** where nodes are chord shapes and edges are call relationships. The coherence checker performs a single graph traversal, and the mirror diff uses hash-based set operations for O(V) comparison. The **γ + η = C** link is explicit and central: the Trit enum is the ternary decision model. HARDCODE (+1) is the γ term (deterministic, cached), MODEL (0) and HYBRID (−1) are the η terms (non-deterministic, model-invoking). The calibration system's goal is to maximize γ/C (the fraction of calls served by hardcode) subject to accuracy constraints.

Oracle2's contribution is making O tractable by producing a compact distribution rather than enumerating all possible future
ARCHITECTURE: Oracle2 provides the **predictive cognition layer** for the SuperInstance constellation. In the conservation law **γ + η = C**, prediction enables **proactive γ allocation**: if Oracle2 forecasts a spike in demand, the fleet pre-allocates γ (compute capacity) before the spike arrives, rather than reactively scaling after. This transforms the fleet from reactive to anticipatory. Oracle2 is the successor to Oracle1 (the first-generation forecasting prototype). Where Oracle1 ran as a PLATO room service, Oracle2 is an in-process library — forecasts happen in the agent's own memory space, eliminating the network hop. See the [SuperInstance Architecture](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

DEFINITION: **Outbox Pattern** is a Rust library implementing the transactional outbox pattern for reliable event publishing — storing events in the same transaction as data changes, then asynchronously publishing them with retry, exponential backoff, and dead-letter handling.

MATH: Save: **O(1)** amortized (VecDeque push_back). This occurs within the same database transaction as the data write — atomicity guaranteed. | Poll: **O(N)** scan. Mark published/failed: **O(N)** linear search by ID (or **O(1)** with HashMap index). | Retry: **O(N)** scan. The publisher calls `poll_pending()` → attempts to publish each → calls `mark_published()` or `mark_failed()` per result. Failed messages cycle back to Pending via `retry_failed( | Outbox Pattern provides reliable event publishing for fleet state changes in SuperInstance. In γ + η = C, the save operation represents γ (growth — recording new fleet events), the retry logic represe
ARCHITECTURE: Outbox Pattern provides reliable event publishing for fleet state changes in SuperInstance. In γ + η = C, the save operation represents γ (growth — recording new fleet events), the retry logic represents η (avoidance — preventing event loss through transient failures), and the dead-letter queue represents the conservation boundary C (messages that exceed retry capacity are quarantined rather than silently dropped). The at-least-once delivery guarantee means consumers must be idempotent — processing an event twice should not double-apply γ or η. Integrates with `orchestration-engine` for task event publishing and `ternary-command` for command event sourcing. CRATE: oxide-barrier
CONCEPTS: ternary, compute, storage, systems, search
DEFINITION: **Oxide Barrier** provides synchronization barriers for GPU kernel phases with ternary arrival states — `+1 (AllArrived)`, `0 (SomeWaiting)`, `-1 (Timeout)` — featuring counting barriers, cyclic barriers with maximum generations, and multi-phase barriers with per-phase party counts.

MATH: `arrive()`: **O(1)** (increment + compare). `timeout()`: **O(1)** (reset + increment). | Use case: limit GPU kernel iterations. After N barrier cycles, force termination to prevent infinite loops. Cost: **O(1)** per arrive. | Example: Phase 0 (4 threads compute), Phase 1 (2 threads reduce), Phase 2 (1 thread writeback). Per-phase party count varies. Cost: **O(1)** per arrive. | Oxide Barrier provides GPU kernel synchronization primitives for SuperInstance. In γ + η = C, AllArrived (+1) represents γ (growth — all threads completed their computation, enabling progress), Timeou
ARCHITECTURE: Oxide Barrier provides GPU kernel synchronization primitives for SuperInstance. In γ + η = C, AllArrived (+1) represents γ (growth — all threads completed their computation, enabling progress), Timeout (-1) represents η (avoidance — deadlock prevention by force-releasing stuck threads), and SomeWaiting (0) is the neutral state where the outcome is undecided. The generation counter enforces C: each generation is one complete cycle, and stale threads are detected by generation mismatch. Integrates with `oxide-ring` for event logging during synchronization and `oxide-epoch` for epoch-based memory management. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md) for GPU synchronization architecture.

CRATE: oxide-chunk

DEFINITION: **Oxide Chunk** provides GPU memory chunk management with ternary allocation status — `+1 (Allocated)`, `0 (Fragmented)`, `-1 (Free)` — featuring buddy-style splitting and merging, automatic coalescing on deallocation, defragmentation, and utilization statistics.

MATH: find smallest free chunk ≥ size     — O(N) scan | split chunk into two buddies    — O(1) | sort chunks by offset            — O(N log N) | scan for adjacent free buddies   — O(N) | merge until no more merges       — O(N) per pass
ARCHITECTURE: Oxide Chunk provides GPU memory management for SuperInstance. In γ + η = C, Allocated (+1) chunks represent γ (growth — memory actively used for computation), Free (-1) chunks represent η (avoidance — memory available but unused, potential for waste), and Fragmented (0) is the mixed state where the pool is neither fully utilized nor empty. The defragmentation operation directly conserves C: by compacting allocated chunks, it reduces wasted address space while preserving total allocated memory. Integrates with `page-allocator` for OS-level page management and `oxide-ring` for allocation event logging. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md) for GPU memory management architecture.

CRATE: oxide-epoch

DEFINITION: **Epoch-based memory reclamation** for concurrent GPU data structures, using a **ternary epoch state** model. This crate implements the epoch reclamation pattern (similar to `crossbeam-epoch`) with a ternary classification — Active, GracePeriod, Reclaimable — that maps to the numeric values +1, 0, −1.
SECTIONS: Oxide Epoch | Why It Matters | How It Works | The Epoch Model | Ternary Classification | RAII Gu
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: oxide-epoch
CONCEPTS: ternary, graph, crypto, compute, storage, systems
DEFINITION: **Epoch-based memory reclamation** for concurrent GPU data structures, using a **ternary epoch state** model. This crate implements the epoch reclamation pattern (similar to `crossbeam-epoch`) with a ternary classification — Active, GracePeriod, Reclaimable — that maps to the numeric values +1, 0, −1.
SECTIONS: Oxide Epoch | Why It Matters | How It Works | The Epoch Model | Ternary Classification | RAII Guard | Deferred Free | Complexity | Quick Start | API | `EpochManager` | `EpochGuard`
MATH: | Hazard pointers | O(1) per access | High (per-pointer tracking) | All | | | **Epoch-based reclamation (EBR)** | O(1) amortized | Medium | All | | | Reference counting | O(1) per access | Low | All (but costly) | | | `pin()` | O(1) — atomic load + mutex lock | Amortized very fast | | | `advance()` | O(1) — atomic fetch_add | |
ARCHITECTURE: The manager uses `Arc<EpochManagerInner>` for shared state, with a `Weak` reference held by each guard. This ensures the manager outlives all guards and prevents use-after-free if the manager is dropped first. The active guard counts are stored in a `HashMap<u64, usize>` — mapping epoch number to number of active guards. This allows O(1) increment/decrement on pin/drop. The **γ + η = C** ternary model is the central design principle: each epoch is **(γ) Active** (has live guards, not safe to reclaim), **(η) Reclaimable** (no guards and old enough, safe to free), or in the **GracePeriod** boundary state (η₀). The classification determines whether deferred frees can proceed.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: oxide-raft-log

DEFINITION: A **Raft-style replicated log** with ternary entry status — each log entry is in one of three states: `Committed (+1)`, `Appended (0)`, or `Conflicting (-1)`. This crate implements term-based conflict resolution, commit advancement via majority quorum, and log compaction for the SuperInstance GPU cluster.

MATH: This is O(1) per entry and preserves audit history via the status field. | Complexity: O(N) per commit check where N = cluster size. In practice, N ≤ 7, so this is negligible. | Oxide Raft Log provides the consensus substrate for SuperInstance's distributed state — the γ (gamma) of agreement. Agents *construct* shared state through log replication. The ternary entry status (C
ARCHITECTURE: Oxide Raft Log provides the consensus substrate for SuperInstance's distributed state — the γ (gamma) of agreement. Agents *construct* shared state through log replication. The ternary entry status (Committed/Appended/Conflicting) maps directly to the {-1, 0, +1} domain, making it native to the γ + η = C framework: committed entries are γ (+1), conflicting entries are η (-1), and appended entries are the neutral transition state (0). See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

documents.name: oxide-ring
documents.description:
CRATE: oxide-ring
CONCEPTS: ternary, compute, storage, systems, search
DEFINITION: **Oxide Ring** is a ring buffer for GPU event logging with ternary overflow state — `+1 (Normal)`, `0 (NearFull)`, `-1 (Overflowed)` — providing lossless event recording until capacity is exceeded, then graceful degradation with oldest-event dropping and overflow tracking.
SECTIONS: Oxide Ring | Why It Matters | How It Works | Ring Buffer Structure | Write Operation | Read Operation | Ternary State Computation | Q
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: oxide-ring
CONCEPTS: ternary, compute, storage, systems, search
DEFINITION: **Oxide Ring** is a ring buffer for GPU event logging with ternary overflow state — `+1 (Normal)`, `0 (NearFull)`, `-1 (Overflowed)` — providing lossless event recording until capacity is exceeded, then graceful degradation with oldest-event dropping and overflow tracking.
SECTIONS: Oxide Ring | Why It Matters | How It Works | Ring Buffer Structure | Write Operation | Read Operation | Ternary State Computation | Query Operations | Statistics | Memory Layout | Quick Start | API
MATH: GPU kernels generate thousands of events per millisecond: kernel launches, memory transfers, synchronization points, and errors. Logging all of them requires a buffer that can absorb burst writes with | Write: **O(1)** amortized (VecDeque push_back). When capacity is full, pop_front is also **O(1)** amortized. | Read: **O(1)** amortized. `peek()` (read without removing): **O(1)**. | State check: **O(1)** (length comparison). Note: once Overflowed, the state stays Overflowed even after reading — the `dropped` counter is cumulative. | Filter: **O(N)** where N = current buffer length. `drain()` (remove all): **O(N)**.
ARCHITECTURE: Oxide Ring provides event logging for GPU kernel monitoring in SuperInstance. In γ + η = C, the Normal (+1) state indicates γ (growth — the system is capturing complete event history), the Overflowed (-1) state indicates η (avoidance — oldest events sacrificed to prevent writer blocking), and the NearFull (0) state is the early warning enabling proactive drain before data loss. The `total_written` counter tracks γ (total growth), `dropped` tracks η (total avoidance), and their ratio approximates C (data conservation). Integrates with `oxide-barrier` for synchronization event logging and `opentelemetry-trace` for distributed tracing export. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md) for GPU event logging architecture.
CROSS_REFS: relates-to:ternary relates-to:compute relates-to:storage relates-to:systems relates-to:search

Should run periodically during low-load periods. | The tombstone_count and purge_count are tracked as **O(1)** running counters. | Oxide Tombstone provides safe deletion for GPU data structures in the oxide-* stack. ARCHITECTURE: Oxide Tombstone provides safe deletion for GPU data structures in the oxide-* stack. In γ + η = C, tombstoning is η (avoidance — marking data as deleted without risking concurrent readers) while compaction is γ (growth — reclaiming physical space for new allocations). Works with `oxide-epoch` for safe reclamation timing and `oxide-chunk` for memory pool management. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md) for GPU data management architecture.

Each step resolves to a ternary outcome: complete (+1), running (0), or failed (−1).
SECTIONS: Oxide Workflow | Why It Matters | How It Works | Ternary Step States | DAG Construction | Topological Sort (Kahn
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: oxide-workflow
CONCEPTS: ternary, fleet, graph, compute, systems
DEFINITION: **DAG-based workflow orchestration** for multi-step GPU kernel pipelines with ternary step states, automatic retry on failure, topological ordering (Kahn's algorithm), rollback support, and progress tracking. Each step resolves to a ternary outcome: complete (+1), running (0), or failed (−1).
SECTIONS: Oxide Workflow | Why It Matters | How It Works | Ternary Step States | DAG Construction | Topological Sort (Kahn's Algorithm) | Execution with Retry and Rollback | Progress Tracking | Complexity | Quick Start | API | `WorkflowDAG`
MATH: | Time | O(V + E) | | | Space | O(V + E) | | | Add step | O(1) | | | Topological sort | O(V + E) | | | Find ready steps | O(V) |
ARCHITECTURE: The DAG stores steps in a `HashMap<String, WorkflowStep>` for O(1) lookup. The topological sort allocates temporary adjacency lists and in-degree maps, then uses a `VecDeque` as the Kahn's algorithm worklist. The `execute()` callback is a generic `FnMut(&str) -> bool`, allowing any execution strategy (synchronous, async via blocking, shell-out to GPU binary). The **γ + η = C** ternary model: each step resolves to **(γ) Complete (+1)** (success — the step's contribution is locked in) or **(η) Failed (−1)** (permanent failure — triggers rollback). Together γ + η = C covers all terminal states, with Running (0) being the transient state. The Progress struct tracks this partition: `completed` counts γ, `failed` counts η.
CROSS_REFS: relates-to:ternary relates-to:fleet relates-to:graph relates-to:compute relates-to:systems

MATH: Complexity: O(1) for in-order delivery, O(log w) for insertion into the buffer (where w = window size). | The Packet Sequencer represents the γ (gamma) ordering primitive in SuperInstance — it *constructs* order from entropy. In the γ + η = C framework, γ builds the ordered stream while η tracks the gaps 
ARCHITECTURE: The Packet Sequencer represents the γ (gamma) ordering primitive in SuperInstance — it *constructs* order from entropy. In the γ + η = C framework, γ builds the ordered stream while η tracks the gaps (missing packets, lost data). The gap reports feed into negative-space analysis: systematic gaps reveal network topology or adversarial interference patterns. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

documents.name: page-allocator
documents.description:
CRATE: page-allocator
CONCEPTS: crypto, compute, storage, protocol, systems, search
DEFINITION: **Page Allocator** is a Rust library implementing the buddy-system page allocator for contiguous physical memory regions — the same algorithm used by the Linux kernel's zone allocator for managing 4KB page frames.
SECTIONS: Page Allocator | Why It Matters | How It Works | Buddy System Algorithm | Allocation | Deallocation | Internal Fragmentation | Page Alignment | Quick Start | API | Architecture Not
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: page-allocator
CONCEPTS: crypto, compute, storage, protocol, systems, search
DEFINITION: **Page Allocator** is a Rust library implementing the buddy-system page allocator for contiguous physical memory regions — the same algorithm used by the Linux kernel's zone allocator for managing 4KB page frames.
SECTIONS: Page Allocator | Why It Matters | How It Works | Buddy System Algorithm | Allocation | Deallocation | Internal Fragmentation | Page Alignment | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References
MATH: Every operating system and hypervisor needs to efficiently allocate contiguous memory regions of varying sizes. The buddy allocator is the gold standard: it guarantees **O(log N)** allocation and deal | 1. Compute `order = order_for_pages(K)` — the smallest order where `2^order ≥ K`. Cost: **O(1)** (bit scan reverse or loop). | 2. If a free block exists at `order`, remove it and return. Cost: **O(1)**. | 3. If not, search upward for a larger block, split it recursively. Cost: **O(log MAX_ORDER)**. | Total: **O(log N)** worst case where N = MAX_ORDER.
ARCHITECTURE: Page Allocator provides the foundational memory management for large-buffer allocation in SuperInstance. In γ + η = C, allocation is γ (growth — expanding memory usage for computation) while deallocation and coalescing are η (avoidance — returning memory to the free pool and preventing fragmentation). Integrates with `oxide-chunk` for GPU memory pools. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md) for memory management architecture.
CROSS_REFS: relates-to:crypto relates-to:compute relates-to:storage relates-to:protocol relates-to:systems relates-to:search

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: pairing-heap
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, graph, crypto, compute, storage, protocol, math, search
DEFINITION: **pairing-heap** is a Rust implementation of the pairing heap data structure — a simple, merge-based priority queue with excellent amortized performance and O(1) insert and find-min operations.
SECTIONS: Pairing Heap | Why It Matters | How It Works | Structure | Core Operation: Merge | Insert | Extract-Min | Meld | Complexity Summary | Comparison to Binary Heap | Quick Start | API
MATH: **pairing-heap** is a Rust implementation of the pairing heap data structure — a simple, merge-based priority queue with excellent amortized performance and O(1) insert and find-min operations. | Priority queues are the backbone of scheduling algorithms (OS process scheduling, Dijkstra's shortest path, A* search, Huffman coding), event simulation, and network packet routing. Pairing heaps are  | **Complexity: O(1)** — one comparison and one vector push. | **Complexity: O(1)** amortized. | **Complexity: O(log n) amortized**, though the worst case for a single extract-min is O(n). The amortized bound comes from the pairing structure: expensive extractions are balanced by many cheap ones.
ARCHITECTURE: This crate provides priority scheduling for SuperInstance's task dispatcher. The O(1) meld operation enables work-stealing: idle nodes meld their heaps with busy nodes' heaps in O(1) time, balancing load without blocking. This supports the γ + η = C framework's scheduling guarantees. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md) for the full design.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: pbkdf2-derive
CONCEPTS: graph, crypto, compute, storage, protocol, systems, search
DEFINITION: A from-scratch Rust implementation of **PBKDF2** (Password-Based Key Derivation Function 2) as specified in **RFC 2898 / PKCS #5 v2.0**, with a built-in hand-coded **SHA-256** and **HMAC-SHA-256** PRF. Zero external dependencies — the entire cryptographic stack is implemented in pure Rust.
SECTIONS: PBKDF2-Derive | Why It Matters | How It Works | Algorithm (RFC 2898 §5.2) | HMAC-SHA-256 PRF | SHA-256 | Security Considerations | Complexity | Quick Start | API | `pbkdf2(password, salt, iterations, dk_len, prf) -> Vec<u8>` | `pbkdf2_hmac_sha256(password, salt, iterations, dk_len) -> Vec<u8>`
MATH: | PRF (HMAC-SHA-256) | O(1) per call | O(64) bytes | | | PBKDF2 block T_i | O(c · |H|) | O(|H|) | | | Full derivation | O(⌈dkLen/hLen⌉ · c) | O(dkLen) | | The **γ + η = C** ternary classification: each output byte is either **(γ) derived from the first block T₁** (covering indices 0–31 for SHA-256) or **(η) from subsequent blocks** (T₂ at 32–63, etc.). 
ARCHITECTURE: The SHA-256 implementation uses pre-computed round constants (`SHA256_K`) and initial hash values (`SHA256_INIT`). The message schedule and compression loop are unrolled for clarity. The PBKDF2 block loop XORs successive U values **in place** on the result buffer, avoiding allocation per iteration. The **γ + η = C** ternary classification: each output byte is either **(γ) derived from the first block T₁** (covering indices 0–31 for SHA-256) or **(η) from subsequent blocks** (T₂ at 32–63, etc.). The total key C = dkLen bytes is partitioned by which block contributes each byte — relevant when dkLen is not a multiple of hLen.

ARCHITECTURE: Part of the [SuperInstance](https://github.com/SuperInstance) ecosystem. The fleet's build harness uses peephole patterns to optimize generated code — the self-improving loop extracts optimization patterns from successful builds and feeds them back as peephole rules. This is the conservation law in action: spend γ (build cost) to produce η (optimization patterns) that reduce future γ. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

DEFINITION: **Persona Engine** manages agent identities — the configuration that defines how an agent presents itself, what decisions it can make autonomously, and how it interacts with other agents and humans. MATH: Part of the [SuperInstance](https://github.com/SuperInstance) ecosystem. Personas implement the hermit crab analogy: the agent code is the crab (persistent, alive), the persona is the shell (swappable
ARCHITECTURE: Part of the [SuperInstance](https://github.com/SuperInstance) ecosystem. Personas implement the hermit crab analogy: the agent code is the crab (persistent, alive), the persona is the shell (swappable, environment-appropriate). A deep-sea crab needs a different shell than a tide-pool crab — same animal, different operating parameters. The conservation law γ + η = C is parameterized per persona: a "cautious" persona spends more γ (thorough testing) to ensure higher η (reliable output), while an "aggressive" persona accepts lower η per task but completes more tasks. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

The γ + η = C framework uses pipelines as the structured data path: gamma decisions feed into the pipeline head, and eta notifications emerge from the pipeline tail. DEFINITION: **Bootstrap a Plato room in 30 seconds** — sensor configurations, alarm rules, and fleet manifests for real-time physical-space monitoring.

MATH: $$\mathcal{R} = (\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, \mathcal{K})$$ | $$\text{fire}(k_j, t) = c_j(\mathcal{S}(t)) \wedge (t - t_{\text{last}}(k_j) \geq \delta_j)$$ | **Big-O complexity**: All commands are $O(1)$ — they write/read fixed-size JSON templates. The `validate` command is $O(n + m)$ where $n$ is the number of required fields and $m$ is the number of sens | This tool is the entry point to the Plato ecosystem. The configuration it generates follows the **γ + η = C** conservation pattern: γ (generation of sensor data) combined with η (evaluation of alarm r
ARCHITECTURE: This tool is the entry point to the Plato ecosystem. The configuration it generates follows the **γ + η = C** conservation pattern: γ (generation of sensor data) combined with η (evaluation of alarm rules) must equal C (consistent room state). Every tick must produce a complete snapshot — no partial updates, no dropped alarms. The fleet manifest supports polyrythmic ticking: different rooms at different frequencies, coordinated by a watchdog agent. This maps to the conservation law $\sum_i \gamma_i \cdot f_i = C_{\text{fleet}}$ — the sum of all room processing rates must match the fleet's aggregate throughput capacity.

ARCHITECTURE: PlayerLog applies γ + η = C to gaming performance: γ (gamma) is the constructive tracking and analysis that builds improvement insight, while η (eta) is the elimination of tilt-induced losses identified through session tagging. The weapon/loadout win-rate analysis reveals which strategies (γ) actually produce competence C — vs. which are wasted effort. The local-first privacy model ensures η extends to data sovereignty: your performance data isn't monetized by third parties. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

Plugins implement domain-specific logic (parsers, validators, transformers) that the core system loads at startup. The γ + η = C framework u
ARCHITECTURE: This crate provides the extension system for SuperInstance. Plugins implement domain-specific logic (parsers, validators, transformers) that the core system loads at startup. The γ + η = C framework uses plugins as the eta layer's pluggable notification handlers: the gamma layer makes decisions, and plugins determine how those decisions are communicated. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md) for the full design.

CONCEPTS: fleet, graph, protocol, search
DEFINITION: A Rust implementation of the **Poison Pill pattern** for graceful distributed shutdown. A sentinel message — the "poison pill" — is injected into a work queue, signaling consumer threads to terminate after processing remaining work. The library provides a multi-worker thread pool with clean shutdown semantics.
SECTIONS: Poison Pill | Why It Matters | How It Works | The Sentinel | Worker Lifecycle | Shutdown Protocol | Shared
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: poison-pill
CONCEPTS: fleet, graph, protocol, search
DEFINITION: A Rust implementation of the **Poison Pill pattern** for graceful distributed shutdown. A sentinel message — the "poison pill" — is injected into a work queue, signaling consumer threads to terminate after processing remaining work. The library provides a multi-worker thread pool with clean shutdown semantics.
SECTIONS: Poison Pill | Why It Matters | How It Works | The Sentinel | Worker Lifecycle | Shutdown Protocol | Shared Channel with Mutex | Complexity | Properties | Quick Start | API | `WorkerPool::new(num_workers) -> (Self, Receiver<String>)`
MATH: | Submit task | O(1) | Channel send | | | Worker lock + recv | O(1) amortized | Contention on mutex | | | Shutdown | O(N) | N = num_workers pills | | | Drain results | O(R) | R = remaining results | | | **Bounded termination** | Workers exit within O(max_task_duration) after pill |
ARCHITECTURE: The shared-receiver pattern uses `Arc<Mutex<Receiver>>`. This is simpler than per-worker channels (which require round-robin or load-balancing dispatch logic) but introduces mutex contention. For high-throughput scenarios, consider: - **Work-stealing** (crossbeam-deque, rayon) — each worker has its own deque, no mutex. - **Per-worker channels** — producer selects a worker via round-robin or least-loaded.

documents.name: poisson-process
documents.description:
CRATE: poisson-process
CONCEPTS: fleet, wavelet, graph, systems
DEFINITION: A **Poisson process** is a counting process where events occur continuously and independently at a constant average rate λ (lambda). It's the mathematical model for things that happen at random but with a predictable average frequency: radioactive decay, phone call arrivals, server request traffic, neuron firing, and insurance claims.
SECTIONS: Poisson Process — Modeling Rare Events | Why It Matters | How It Works | Quic

In γ + η = C, it demonstrates that γ (growth — problem-solving capability) is invariant across different constraint systems (languages), while η (avoidance — each language rejects problem framings that don't fit its ontology) is the mechanism that preserves this invariance. The findings inform the `negative-space-core` theory of avoidance-based intelligence. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md) for the theoretical framework.

documents.name: potential-field
documents.description:
CRATE: potential-field
CONCEPTS: conservation, fleet, compute, storage, math, search
DEFINITION: **Artificial potential fields** are a robot path-planning technique where the goal exerts an attractive force pulling the robot toward it, while obstacles exert repulsive forces pushing the robot away. The robot follows the negative gradient — rolling downhill through a potential landscape toward the target.
SECTIONS: Potential Field — Robot Navigation via Vector Fields | Why It Matters | How It Work
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: potential-field
CONCEPTS: conservation, fleet, compute, storage, math, search
DEFINITION: **Artificial potential fields** are a robot path-planning technique where the goal exerts an attractive force pulling the robot toward it, while obstacles exert repulsive forces pushing the robot away. The robot follows the negative gradient — rolling downhill through a potential landscape toward the target.
SECTIONS: Potential Field — Robot Navigation via Vector Fields | Why It Matters | How It Works | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References | License
MATH: Potential fields are elegant because they reduce path planning to vector addition: at each point, sum the attractive vector (pointing to goal) with all repulsive vectors (pointing away from obstacles) | **Complexity**: Building the field is O(W×H×(G+O)) where W×H is grid dimensions. Querying is O(1) — just look up the precomputed vector. This makes it ideal for scenarios where the environment changes | Part of the [SuperInstance](https://github.com/SuperInstance) ecosystem. Potential fields map directly to the fleet's conservation law: the attractive force is η (innovation pulling toward the goal), 
ARCHITECTURE: Part of the [SuperInstance](https://github.com/SuperInstance) ecosystem. Potential fields map directly to the fleet's conservation law: the attractive force is η (innovation pulling toward the goal), the repulsive force is γ (cost pushing away from expensive paths). The optimal trajectory minimizes total cost while reaching the goal — the physical analog of γ + η = C. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: procfs-scanner
CONCEPTS: conservation, fleet, graph, crypto, compute, storage, protocol, systems
DEFINITION: **Linux `/proc` filesystem scanner** for process enumeration, system metrics, and memory analysis.
SECTIONS: procfs-scanner | Why It Matters | How It Works | Process Enumeration | Process State Codes | RSS Sorting | Memory Parsing | Quick Start | API | `ProcessInfo` Struct | Architecture Notes | References
MATH: $$\text{RSS}_{\text{KB}} = \text{pages} \times P_{\text{KB}}$$ | $$\text{sort key} = \text{RSS}_{\text{KB}}(\text{pid})$$ | - Process enumeration: $O(n)$ where $n$ is the number of processes | - RSS sort: $O(n \log n)$ | - Total: $O(n \log n)$ per scan
ARCHITECTURE: The scanner follows the **γ + η = C** conservation link: γ (kernel-published proc data) combined with η (user-space parsing) must yield C (a consistent snapshot). Because `/proc` is a virtual filesystem, reads are atomic per-file but not across files. For higher consistency, a production version would use `pread` with `/proc/[pid]/status` which provides more fields in a single read. The tool deliberately avoids `procfs` crate dependencies to minimize attack surface in containerized environments. All parsing is done with `std::fs` and string splitting.

ARCHITECTURE: This crate provides the serialization layer for SuperInstance's inter-node communication. Protocol Buffers encode consensus messages (γ layer proposals and votes) and notification payloads (η layer events) in a compact binary format. The γ + η = C framework's network efficiency depends on protobuf's space efficiency: varint encoding reduces wire payload by 60–80% versus JSON. ARCHITECTURE: PTX Bench measures the raw computational capacity (γ — generation throughput) of the SuperInstance GPU stack. The ternary optimization opportunities (replacing multiply with sign-flip) are the η (elimination) side: by removing floating-point multiplies, we eliminate 75% of the arithmetic cost. The benchmark data feeds directly into the γ + η = C equation by quantifying how much C (competence) each implementation tier achieves per watt-second. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: quadtree-index
CONCEPTS: fleet, wavelet, graph, crypto, storage, systems, search
DEFINITION: A **quadtree** is a tree data structure where each internal node has exactly four children, partitioning 2D space into quadrants recursively. This crate implements a region quadtree for spatial indexing — fast point queries, range searches, and collision detection in 2D space.
SECTIONS: Quadtree Spatial Index | Why It Matters | How It Works | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References | License
MATH: Quadtrees solve a fundamental problem: "find all objects within this rectangle" or "find the nearest neighbor to this point." On N points, a brute-force scan is O(N) per query. A quadtree reduces this | **Range query** — Starting at root, if the query rectangle intersects a node's bounds, descend into that node. At leaves, check each point against the query rect. This prunes entire quadrants that can | **Point lookup** — O(log N): descend directly to the leaf containing the point. | The AABB intersection test is O(1): two rectangles overlap iff they overlap on every axis — `a.min_x <= b.max_x && a.max_x >= b.min_x && a.min_y <= b.max_y && a.max_y >= b.min_y`. | - `qt.insert(x, y, value)` — Insert a point with associated data. O(log N)
ARCHITECTURE: Part of the [SuperInstance](https://github.com/SuperInstance) ecosystem. Spatial indexing is critical for the fleet's edge agents — IoT device lookups, geographic service discovery, and collision detection in physical simulations all rely on efficient 2D queries. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

Without spatial indexing, finding all po | **Complexity:** Average O(log₄ n) per insertion. Worst case O(n) if all points cluster at the same location and subdivision hits `QT_MIN_SIZE`. | **Complexity:** O(k + log n) average, where k = number of points in the result set. Worst case O(n) when the query range covers the entire space. | | Quadtree | 2D | O(log n) | O(k + log n) | Uniform 2D point data | | | R-tree | 2D/3D | O(log n) | O(k + log n) | Rectangles, uneven data |

It complements the R-tree (which handles rectangle/bbox queries) in the **γ + η = C** model: the quadtree excels at uniform point distributions, while the R-tree adapts to clustered workloads. CRATE: raft-cluster
