DEFINITION: A Rust implementation of the **Raft consensus algorithm** for cluster replication and fault tolerance. Raft is designed to be understandable by design — it decomposes consensus into three subproblems: leader election, log replication, and safety constraints.
SECTIONS: raft-cluster | Why It Matters | How It Works | Leader Election | Log Replication | Complexity | Safety Guarant
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: raft-cluster
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, wavelet, graph, crypto, storage, protocol, math, search
DEFINITION: A Rust implementation of the **Raft consensus algorithm** for cluster replication and fault tolerance. Raft is designed to be understandable by design — it decomposes consensus into three subproblems: leader election, log replication, and safety constraints.
SECTIONS: raft-cluster | Why It Matters | How It Works | Leader Election | Log Replication | Complexity | Safety Guarantees | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References | License
MATH: $$T_{\text{election}} \sim \text{Uniform}(150\text{ms}, 300\text{ms})$$ | $$P_{\text{split}} \approx \frac{1}{n} \cdot e^{-n \cdot \Delta t / T}$$ | $$f = \frac{N - 1}{2} \quad \text{(odd } N \text{)}$$ | | Leader election | $O(N)$ messages | $O(1)$ per node | | | Log replication | $O(\text{log len})$ per entry | $O(\text{log len})$ |
ARCHITECTURE: This crate is part of the **SuperInstance ecosystem**, a distributed systems platform built on ternary design principles. The conservation law $\gamma + \eta = C$ governs information flow: every bottle (message) that enters the system carries a trit vector $\gamma$, and every response carries $\eta$, such that the sum is invariant. In Raft terms, this maps to **log consistency** — the leader's log state $\gamma$ plus the followers' aggregate state $\eta$ must equal the committed state $C$. The consensus protocol integrates with the `Bottle` wire format from `superinstance-protocol`, where ternary values $\{-1, 0, +1\}$ encode vote decisions and log acknowledgments.

ARCHITECTURE: In SuperInstance, `raft-snapshot` keeps the consensus log compact for long-running fleet management nodes. Snapshots are taken every 10,000 entries and stored on disk for recovery. In the **γ + η = C** model, snapshots preserve η (network/storage efficiency) by preventing unbounded log growth. documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: rate-limiter-fixed
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, fleet, graph, crypto, compute, protocol, math, systems, search
DEFINITION: A **fixed-window rate limiter** in pure Rust. Enforces a maximum number of requests per client within a fixed time window using a `Mutex<HashMap>` for thread-safe state.
SECTIONS: rate-limiter-fixed | Why It Matters | How It Works | Algorithm | Boundary Effect | Complexity | Quick Start | API | `FixedWindowLimiter` | Architecture Notes | References | License
MATH: | **Fixed window** | ±1 window | $O(K)$ | High at boundaries | $O(1)$ | | | Sliding window log | Exact | $O(K \cdot R)$ | None | $O(\log R)$ | | | Token bucket | Smooth | $O(K)$ | Configurable | $O(1)$ | | | Leaky bucket | Smooth | $O(K)$ | None | $O(1)$ | | $$R_{\text{burst}} = 2 \cdot R_{\max}$$
ARCHITECTURE: This crate is part of the **SuperInstance ecosystem**. The rate limiter enforces the conservation law $\gamma + \eta = C$ at the fleet edge: incoming request trit vectors $\gamma$ are gated by the limiter, ensuring that only $C$ (the configured maximum) requests propagate to downstream agents within each window. Requests exceeding the limit are rejected with a ternary $\{-1\}$ signal, preserving conservation semantics. The fixed-window approach maps naturally to ternary counting: each window boundary is a **phase reset** where all counters return to zero, analogous to a ternary clock cycle in the SuperInstance agent protocol.

documents.name: reed-solomon
documents.description:
CRATE: reed-solomon
CONCEPTS: compute, protocol
DEFINITION: **Reed-Solomon codes** are block-based error-correcting codes that operate on symbols (typically bytes) over the Galois field GF(2⁸). They add `n_sym` parity symbols to a data block, enabling detection and correction of up to `⌊n_sym/2⌋` corrupted symbols. This crate implements encoding and syndrome computation over GF(2⁸) with the standard irreducible polynomial x⁸ + x⁴ + x³ + x + 1.
SECTIONS: Reed-Solomon Error-Correcting Codes | Why
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: reed-solomon
CONCEPTS: compute, protocol
DEFINITION: **Reed-Solomon codes** are block-based error-correcting codes that operate on symbols (typically bytes) over the Galois field GF(2⁸). They add `n_sym` parity symbols to a data block, enabling detection and correction of up to `⌊n_sym/2⌋` corrupted symbols. This crate implements encoding and syndrome computation over GF(2⁸) with the standard irreducible polynomial x⁸ + x⁴ + x³ + x + 1.

CRATE: reliable-udp

DEFINITION: A Rust library for **reliable data transfer over UDP**. Implements the classic transformations — sequence numbers, acknowledgments, retransmission timers, and congestion control — that turn the unreliable UDP datagram service into a dependable transport layer.

MATH: $$\text{Throughput}_{\max} = \min\left(\frac{W}{\text{RTT}}, \frac{\text{bandwidth}}{\text{packet size}}\right)$$ | $$S_{\text{max}} > 2W$$ | $$W_{t+1} = \begin{cases} W_t + \alpha & \text{if ACK received (increase)} \\ W_t \cdot \beta & \text{if loss detected (decrease)} \end{cases}$$ | $$\text{SRTT}_{t+1} = \alpha \cdot \text{SRTT}_t + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \text{RTT}_{\text{sample}}$$ | | Send (enqueue) | $O(1)$ | $O(W)$ send buffer |

The conservation law $\gamma + \eta = C$ maps to **message completeness**: the sent trit vector $\gamma$ plus the received trit vector $\eta$ must equal the total message $C$. Sequence numbers and ACKs enforce that no trits are lost in transit. Lost bottles are retransmitted, preserving the conservation invariant across the network boundary.

DEFINITION: A **conservation-preserving ternary map** — Agent B's riff on Agent A's baseline. Stores ternary values $\{-1, 0, +1\}$ keyed by `i64`, with a **guaranteed conservation invariant**: the tracked sum always equals the actual sum of all values.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: riff-benchmark-hashing
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, fleet, crypto, compute, storage, protocol, math, search
DEFINITION: A **conservation-preserving ternary map** — Agent B's riff on Agent A's baseline. Stores ternary values $\{-1, 0, +1\}$ keyed by `i64`, with a **guaranteed conservation invariant**: the tracked sum always equals the actual sum of all values.
SECTIONS: riff-benchmark-hashing | Why It Matters | How It Works | Conservation Invariant | Metrics | Trit Packing | Complexity | Quick Start | API | `TernaryMap` | Architecture Notes | References
MATH: - **O(1) conservation verification** — detect bugs instantly via tracked sum | $$\text{conservation} = \sum_{i} v_i \quad \text{where } v_i \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$$ | $$\text{verified\_sum}() = \text{conservation} \stackrel{!}{=} \sum_i v_i$$ | This is $O(n)$ in the number of entries, but $O(1)$ for the tracked sum. | $$B = \frac{|\{i : v_i > 0\}| - |\{i : v_i < 0\}|}{n} \in [-1, +1]$$
ARCHITECTURE: This crate is the **canonical ternary storage** for the SuperInstance ecosystem. The conservation law $\gamma + \eta = C$ is directly implemented by the `conservation` field and `verified_sum()` method. When agent A sends trit vector $\gamma$ and agent B responds with $\eta$, the sum $\gamma + \eta$ must equal the system constant $C$ — this crate makes that invariant **mechanically unverifiable**. The `churn` metric feeds into fleet-level observability: high churn indicates that agents are frequently revising their state, which may signal instability. The EWMA-tracked quality in `superinstance-agent-trait` correlates with low churn and balanced density.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: rigid-body
CONCEPTS: conservation, compute, math, search
DEFINITION: **2D rigid body physics simulation** — impulse-based collision response, rotational dynamics, and gravitational integration.
SECTIONS: rigid-body | Why It Matters | How It Works | State Representation | Moment of Inertia | Time Integration (Semi-Implicit Euler) | Collision Detection | Impulse-Based Collision Response | Damping | Quick Start | API | `RigidBody`
MATH: $$\mathbf{q} = (\mathbf{x}, \theta, \mathbf{v}, \omega, m, I)$$ | $$I = \frac{m(w^2 + h^2)}{12}$$ | $$\mathbf{c}_i = \mathbf{x} + R(\theta) \cdot \mathbf{l}_i$$ | $$j = \frac{-(1 + e) v_n}{\frac{1}{m} + \frac{(\mathbf{r} \times \mathbf{n})^2}{I}}$$ | 4. Update velocities: $\mathbf{v} \mathrel{+=} \frac{j \mathbf{n}}{m}$, $\omega \mathrel{+=} \frac{(\mathbf{r} \times j\mathbf{n})}{I}$
ARCHITECTURE: The simulation follows the **γ + η = C** conservation pattern: γ (gravational energy input) combined with η (collision impulse dissipation) must conserve the system's total energy budget C. Energy is injected by gravity, dissipated by inelastic collisions ($e = 0.4$) and angular damping, and the remainder is the stable settled state. The impulse-based approach is equivalent to solving a linear complementarity problem (LCP) at each contact. For multi-contact scenarios, a sequential impulse solver (like Box2D's) iterates over contacts. This implementation handles one contact at a time, which is sufficient for a single bouncing box.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: ring-buffer
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, fleet, graph, crypto, storage, protocol, math, systems, search
DEFINITION: A **fixed-capacity circular buffer** (ring buffer) in Rust, implementing FIFO semantics with $O(1)$ push and pop using a contiguous array with modular arithmetic for wrap-around.
SECTIONS: ring-buffer | Why It Matters | How It Works | Circular Indexing | Full / Empty Disambiguation | Complexity | Generic Constraints | Quick Start | API | `RingBuffer<T>` | Architecture Notes | References
MATH: A **fixed-capacity circular buffer** (ring buffer) in Rust, implementing FIFO semantics with $O(1)$ push and pop using a contiguous array with modular arithmetic for wrap-around. | $$\text{next}(i) = (i + 1) \bmod N$$ | | `push_back` | $O(1)$ | $O(N)$ total, fixed | | | `pop_front` | $O(1)$ | — | | | `len` / `is_empty` | $O(1)$ | — |
ARCHITECTURE: This crate is part of the **SuperInstance ecosystem**. Ring buffers serve as **bounded channels** between fleet agents: one agent produces bottles (trit vectors $\gamma$) into the buffer, and another consumes them, producing responses ($\eta$). The conservation law $\gamma + \eta = C$ is preserved because the buffer is **lossless** — every bottle pushed is eventually popped. If the buffer overflows (push returns `Err`), the producer must backpressure, preventing conservation violations from dropped messages. The fixed capacity mirrors the **bounded-memory** principle of the fleet: no unbounded queues, no OOM, predictable latency.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: ring-buffer-atomic
CONCEPTS: fleet, wavelet, compute, storage, systems, search
DEFINITION: A **lock-free ring buffer** for the single-producer, single-consumer (SPSC) pattern. Using atomic indices instead of mutexes, the producer and consumer can operate concurrently without locks — the producer writes while the consumer reads, with no contention.
SECTIONS: Atomic Ring Buffer — Lock-Free SPSC Queue | Why It Matters | How It Works | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References | License
MATH: - `push(item)` — Producer enqueue. Returns `Err(item)` if full. O(1), lock-free | - `pop()` — Consumer dequeue. Returns `None` if empty. O(1), lock-free
ARCHITECTURE: Part of the [SuperInstance](https://github.com/SuperInstance) ecosystem. The fleet's message pipeline uses SPSC ring buffers between pipeline stages — each agent runs in its own thread, connected to the next agent via a lock-free queue. This ensures that a slow agent doesn't block a fast one (it fills the buffer, creating backpressure) and that real-time agents (Jetson edge inference) never experience mutex-induced latency spikes. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

ARCHITECTURE: Resource limits connect to the **γ + η = C** conservation pattern: γ (resources the process generates/opens) combined with η (limits that bound consumption) must produce C (a stable system that neither starves nor floods). Setting `nofile` too low causes FD exhaustion; too high enables resource-amplification attacks. The recommended values are the equilibrium point where C holds for typical server workloads. For systemd services, limits are set via `LimitNOFILE=65535` in the unit file rather than `ulimit`. For Docker containers, use `--ulimit nofile=65535:65535`.

Each Room's `perception_db` is the γ record — what w
ARCHITECTURE: Room Cell is the spatial primitive where γ (perception, construction of understanding) meets η (surprise, the gap between expectation and reality). Each Room's `perception_db` is the γ record — what was built/observed. The `surprise_history` is the η signal — where predictions failed. Their ratio drives C (competence): a Room with low surprise and rich perception is highly competent. The murmur protocol propagates competence signals across the spatial network. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: room-dds-bridge
CONCEPTS: ternary, fleet, graph, crypto, compute, storage, protocol
DEFINITION: **Auto-configure DDS (Data Distribution Service) domains** from ternary-mud room topology — map spatial relationships to publish/subscribe topics, QoS policies, and domain bridges.
SECTIONS: room-dds-bridge | Why It Matters | How It Works | Domain Assignment via Union-Find | Ternary QoS Mapping | Topic Generation | Publisher/Subscriber Assignment | Configuration Serialization | Quick Start | API | Core Functions | Serialization
MATH: The Union-Find operations are nearly $O(1)$ amortized with path compression: | $$T(n, m) = O(m \cdot \alpha(n))$$ | $$P(\text{room}) = \{a \in \text{Agents} \mid a.\text{room} = \text{room} \wedge a.\text{publishes}\}$$ | $$S(\text{room}) = \{a \in \text{Agents} \mid a.\text{room} = \text{room} \wedge a.\text{subscribes}\}$$ | - Domain assignment: $O(m \cdot \alpha(n))$
ARCHITECTURE: The bridge enforces the **γ + η = C** conservation law: γ (the topology's connectivity structure) combined with η (the ternary QoS derivation) must produce C (a complete, consistent DDS configuration). Every room must have exactly one domain; every cross-domain passage must have exactly one bridge; every QoS policy must be fully determined by its ternary input. No information is lost or fabricated in the mapping. The Union-Find algorithm with path compression ensures deterministic output — the same topology always produces the same domain assignment regardless of iteration order, because rooms within each group are sorted alphabetically.

DEFINITION: A **rope data structure** for efficient string manipulation on large texts. Represents strings as a balanced binary tree of leaf nodes (short substrings), enabling $O(\log n)$ insert, delete, and split operations without copying the entire string.

MATH: A **rope data structure** for efficient string manipulation on large texts. Represents strings as a balanced binary tree of leaf nodes (short substrings), enabling $O(\log n)$ insert, delete, and spli | For large documents (text editors, source files, DNA sequences), a `String` requires $O(n)$ to insert or delete — every byte after the edit point must be shifted. Ropes reduce this to $O(\log n)$ by k | | Insert at position $i$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | | | Delete range | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | | | Concat | $O(n + m)$ | $O(1)$ |
ARCHITECTURE: This crate is part of the **SuperInstance ecosystem**. Ropes model **streaming agent communication**: long trit sequences $\gamma$ flowing between agents can be represented as ropes, enabling efficient substring operations (e.g., extracting a sub-vector for verification) without copying. The conservation law $\gamma + \eta = C$ is preserved because rope operations are **structure-preserving** — split, concat, insert, and delete never lose trits; they only reorganize the tree structure. The `MAX_LEAF = 64` constant aligns with the standard `Bottle` payload size in the fleet protocol, making leaf nodes directly transmittable as message fragments.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: row-store
CONCEPTS: graph, crypto, storage, systems, search
DEFINITION: A **row-oriented storage engine** for OLTP (Online Transaction Processing) workloads. Stores records as contiguous rows of column-value maps, optimized for point queries, single-row inserts, and transactional updates.
SECTIONS: row-store | Why It Matters | When to Use a Row Store | How It Works | Physical Layout | Schema Enforcement | Scan with Predicate | Complexity | Cache Performance | Quick Start | API | `RowStore`
MATH: | Point lookup by key | ✅ $O(1)$ | ❌ $O(N)$ | | | Single-row insert | ✅ $O(1)$ | ❌ $O(\text{cols})$ | | | Aggregation (SUM, AVG) | ❌ $O(N \times C)$ | ✅ $O(N)$ | | $$\text{valid}(row) \iff \forall k \in \text{keys}(row): k \in \text{schema}$$ | $$\text{result} = \{ r \in \text{rows} : P(r) \}$$
ARCHITECTURE: This crate is part of the **SuperInstance ecosystem**. The row store provides the **persistence layer** for fleet agent state: each agent's lifecycle state (Init → Active → Suspended → Terminated) and cycle history are stored as rows. The conservation law $\gamma + \eta = C$ maps to **transactional integrity**: the input trit vector $\gamma$ (agent state before a cycle) plus the output trit vector $\eta$ (agent state after) must sum to the system constant $C$. The row store enforces this by making each row update atomic — a cycle either commits completely or rolls back. In production, the row store would use a **write-ahead log (WAL)** for crash recovery and **MVCC** for concurrent reads.

DEFINITION: **Cron expression parser and job scheduler** — parse standard 5-field cron syntax into typed schedules for periodic task execution.
SECTIONS: scheduler-cron | Why It Matters | How It Works | Cron Expression Format | Field Parsing | Schedule Matching | Complexity | Quick Start | API | `CronSchedule` | `ScheduledJob<F>` | Parsing Rules
MATH: $$\text{valid}(v) \iff \min \leq v \leq \max$$ | $$\text{match}(t) = \bigwedge_{f \in \{m, h, d, M, D\}} t_f \in \text{values}(f)$$ | | Parse field | $O(k \log k)$ where $k$ is comma-list length (sort + dedup) | | | Parse expression | $O(5k)$ — five fields | | | Match timestamp | $O(\log k)$ per field via binary search, $O(5 \log k)$ total |
ARCHITECTURE: The parser connects to the **γ + η = C** conservation pattern: γ (the schedule's enumerated time values) combined with η (the system clock's current time) must agree at time C to trigger execution. The schedule is pure data — no side effects until bound to a `ScheduledJob` with a callback. This separation allows schedules to be serialized, inspected, and tested independently of execution. The current implementation supports literals and wildcards. Production cron also supports ranges (`1-5`), step values (`*/15`), and named tokens (`MON`, `JAN`). These can be added by extending `parse_field`.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: scope-resolver
CONCEPTS: graph, crypto, search
DEFINITION: **Lexical scope resolver and semantic analyzer** — walk an AST to bind variable references, track scope depth, and detect undefined identifiers.
SECTIONS: scope-resolver | Why It Matters | How It Works | Scope Model | Statement Resolution | Expression Resolution | Pattern Binding | Quick Start | API | `ScopeResolver` | `ScopeInfo` | `ResolvedRef`
MATH: Without scope resolution, downstream passes (type inference, codegen) would need to re-walk the tree for each reference, resulting in $O(n^2)$ lookups. | $$\text{Scope}_0 \subset \text{Scope}_1 \subset \cdots \subset \text{Scope}_d$$ | $$\text{lookup}(\text{name}) = \text{search innermost} \rightarrow \text{parent} \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow \text{global}$$ | - Per statement: $O(1)$ for define/lookup (HashMap with chaining) | - Full program: $O(n)$ where $n$ is total AST nodes
ARCHITECTURE: The resolver maintains the **γ + η = C** conservation invariant: γ (declarations introduced by `let`, `func`, `struct`, `enum`) combined with η (references consumed by `Variable`, `Call`, `Member`) must balance at scope exit — every reference must resolve to a declaration in its scope chain. If $\gamma < \eta$ (more references than declarations), the resolver returns `Err("Undefined variable: ...")`. The resolver is a **single forward pass** — no fixpoint iteration needed because Rust's scoping is strictly lexical and non-recursive at the statement level. Function bodies are resolved independently (parameters are in scope for the body but the function name is visible in the enclosing scope).

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: seifert-surface
CONCEPTS: conservation, graph, compute, storage
DEFINITION: **Seifert surfaces for knots and links** — compute orientable surfaces bounded by knots, with genus bounds and Euler characteristic calculations.
SECTIONS: seifert-surface | Why It Matters | How It Works | Seifert's Algorithm | Genus Bound | Euler Characteristic | Classification | Quick Start | API | `Knot` | `SeifertSurface` | `seifert_genus_bound(crossing_number)`
MATH: $$g \leq \frac{c - r + 1}{2}$$ | $$g_{\text{bound}}(c) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } c = 0 \\ \lfloor\frac{c - 1}{2}\rfloor & \text{if } c > 0 \end{cases}$$ | $$\chi = 2 - 2g - b$$ | $$\chi = 1 - 2g$$ | **Big-O complexity**: All computations are $O(1)$ — they are closed-form formulas applied to knot invariants (crossing number, genus).
ARCHITECTURE: The Seifert surface computation links to the **γ + η = C** conservation principle: γ (the knot's crossing structure) combined with η (the genus assignment via Seifert smoothing) must produce C (a surface with the correct Euler characteristic). The conservation law here is the **Gauss-Bonnet theorem**: $$\int_\Sigma K \, dA = 2\pi\chi(\Sigma)$$ which connects the intrinsic geometry (γ) with the topology (η) to yield the Euler characteristic (C). This is why $\chi$ is computable from combinatorial data alone — it's a topological invariant, not a geometric measurement.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: shamir-secret
CONCEPTS: graph, crypto, protocol
DEFINITION: **Shamir's $(t, n)$-threshold secret sharing** over $\text{GF}(p)$ — split a secret into $n$ shares such that any $t$ shares reconstruct it, but fewer than $t$ reveal nothing.
SECTIONS: shamir-secret | Why It Matters | How It Works | The Scheme | Secret Splitting | Reconstruction via Lagrange Interpolation | Modular Arithmetic | Security Proof | Quick Start | API | `ShamirSecret` | Constraints
MATH: $$f(x) = s + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + \cdots + a_{t-1} x^{t-1} \pmod{p}$$ | $$s = f(0) = \sum_{i=1}^{t} y_i \cdot \lambda_i(0) \pmod{p}$$ | $$\lambda_i(0) = \prod_{j \neq i} \frac{-x_j}{x_i - x_j} \pmod{p}$$ | $$P(S = s \mid \text{shares}) = P(S = s)$$ | - Split: $O(n \cdot t)$ — $n$ evaluations of degree-$(t-1)$ polynomial
ARCHITECTURE: The implementation follows the **γ + η = C** conservation principle: γ (the $t$ shares provided) combined with η (the Lagrange interpolation coefficients) must yield C (the exact original secret $s$). The beauty of Shamir's scheme is that this conservation holds **exactly** — reconstruction is not approximate. When $t' < t$ shares are provided, $\gamma < C$ and no amount of computation can bridge the gap. The use of `u128` intermediates for modular multiplication avoids overflow for primes up to $\approx 2^{63}$, which covers all practical use cases including the commonly used Mersenne prime $2^{31} - 1$.

The Metropolis criterion's acceptance of worse solu
ARCHITECTURE: Simulated Annealing provides the global optimization backbone for η (eta) in SuperInstance — the process of *eliminating* suboptimal configurations. The Metropolis criterion's acceptance of worse solutions mirrors the negative-space principle: sometimes you must move *toward* a worse state to eventually escape it. The cooling schedule controls the γ/η balance — high temperature favors γ (exploration, construction), low temperature favors η (exploitation, elimination). The reheat mechanism is a meta-level η: when the search itself stagnates, the algorithm resets to re-examine its assumptions. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

Provides $O(\log n)$ expected-time search, insertion, and deletion using layered linked lists with geometrically distributed  | - $O(\log n)$ expected search/insert/delete (same as red-black tree) | - $O(n)$ worst-case (degenerate to linked list with probability $\approx 1/2^n$) | | Sorted array | $O(\log n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | Poor | Simple | | | Red-black tree | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | Hard (rotations) | Complex |
ARCHITECTURE: This crate is part of the **SuperInstance ecosystem**. Skip lists maintain **ordered agent registries**: fleet agents are indexed by their ternary sum $\sum \gamma_i$, enabling range queries like "find all agents with positive trit balance." The conservation law $\gamma + \eta = C$ is preserved because skip list operations are **in-place mutations** — insert and remove change the set's membership without affecting the trit sums of other agents. The probabilistic height assignment mirrors the **fleet's stochastic scheduling**: agents self-organize into hierarchical levels based on their observed reliability, with the geometric distribution ensuring $O(\log n)$ expected lookup for any agent.

CRATE: snapshot-test
CONCEPTS: crypto, compute, storage, protocol, search
DEFINITION: A **snapshot testing framework** for Rust — capture, compare, and review serialized output to detect regressions automatically. Snapshot tests are the gold standard for testing serializers, renderers, compilers, and any function that produces complex structured output.
SECTIONS: snapshot-test | Why It Matters | Snapshot Testing vs. Assertion Testing | How It Works | Workflow | Snapshot File Format | Comparison Algorithm | Complexity | Myers Diff Algorithm | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes
MATH: $$\text{pass} \iff \text{output}_{\text{actual}} = \text{output}_{\text{snapshot}}$$ | | Snapshot capture | $O(n)$ serialize | $O(n)$ file | | | Snapshot compare | $O(n)$ string compare | $O(1)$ | | | Diff generation | $O(n \cdot d)$ (Myers diff) | $O(n \cdot d)$ | | | Update (accept) | $O(n)$ file write | $O(n)$ |
ARCHITECTURE: This crate is part of the **SuperInstance ecosystem**. Snapshot testing provides **conservation regression detection**: every fleet agent's behavior produces a trit vector output $\eta$ that should be deterministic for a given input $\gamma$. Snapshots lock in the expected $\eta$ values, and any change to the agent's logic that alters $\eta$ (potentially violating $\gamma + \eta = C$) triggers a snapshot failure. This makes the conservation law $\gamma + \eta = C$ **mechanically enforced** by the test suite — you cannot change agent behavior without acknowledging the change via snapshot update. The framework also snapshots **ternary state transitions**: the `AgentState` lifecycle (Init → Active → Suspended → Terminated) is captured as a sequence, ensuring that state machine changes are deliberate.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: sort-merge
CONCEPTS: conservation, graph, compute, math, systems, search
DEFINITION: **Merge sort** — divide-and-conquer sorting with guaranteed $O(n \log n)$ time complexity, stable ordering, and $O(n)$ auxiliary space.
SECTIONS: sort-merge | Why It Matters | How It Works | Divide and Conquer | Recurrence Relation | Merge Step | Complexity Analysis | Base Case | Quick Start | API | `merge_sort<T: Ord + Clone>` | Generic Type Constraints
MATH: **Merge sort** — divide-and-conquer sorting with guaranteed $O(n \log n)$ time complexity, stable ordering, and $O(n)$ auxiliary space. | - **Guaranteed $O(n \log n)$** — no worst-case degradation unlike quicksort | $$T(n) = 2T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + O(n)$$ | $$T(n) = O(n \log n)$$ | The merge step is $O(n)$ because each comparison advances one element into the output, and there are exactly $n$ elements total.
ARCHITECTURE: The implementation follows the **γ + η = C** conservation pattern: γ (the recursive subdivision into halves) combined with η (the linear merge of sorted subsequences) must produce C (a fully sorted output). The conservation invariant is the **sortedness invariant**: at every level of recursion, each half is sorted before merging, and the merge preserves sortedness. This can be stated formally: $$\text{sorted}(L) \wedge \text{sorted}(R) \implies \text{sorted}(\text{merge}(L, R))$$ The auxiliary space is exactly $O(n)$ — at each recursion level, the merge step allocates two temporary vectors totaling $n$ elements. This could be optimized to a single $O(n)$ buffer reused across levels (as in production implementations), but the current code prioritizes clarity.

CRATE: spectral-decomposition
CONCEPTS: conservation, wavelet, crypto, compute, storage, math, search
DEFINITION: **Eigenvalue and singular value decomposition** for dense matrices — power iteration, deflation-based eigendecomposition, and thin SVD via the covariance method.
SECTIONS: spectral-decomposition | Why It Matters | How It Works | Matrix Representation | Power Iteration | Eigendecomposition via Deflation | Thin SVD | Matrix Multiplication | Quick Start | API | `Matrix` | Decomposition Functions
MATH: 2. Iterate: $\mathbf{v}^{(k+1)} = \frac{A \mathbf{v}^{(k)}}{\|A \mathbf{v}^{(k)}\|}$ | $$\|\mathbf{v}^{(k)} - \mathbf{v}_1\| = O\!\left(\left|\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1}\right|^k\right)$$ | $$A_{k+1} = A_k - \lambda_k \mathbf{v}_k \mathbf{v}_k^T$$ | 4. Left singular vectors: $\mathbf{u}_i = \frac{A \mathbf{v}_i}{\sigma_i}$ | $$C_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} A_{ik} B_{kj}$$
ARCHITECTURE: The decomposition follows the **γ + η = C** conservation pattern: γ (the matrix $A$'s spectral content) combined with η (the decomposition basis $\{U, V\}$) must reconstruct $C = A$. The reconstruction identity is: $$A = U \Sigma V^T = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sigma_i \mathbf{u}_i \mathbf{v}_i^T$$ Each rank-1 term $\sigma_i \mathbf{u}_i \mathbf{v}_i^T$ is a "spectral component." Deflation removes them one at a time, ensuring each iteration targets a different eigenpair.

ARCHITECTURE: Spreadsheet Cells models the γ/η/C dynamic at the cellular level: γ is the constructive averaging (`AVG(neighbor.value)`) that pulls cells toward consensus, η is the stochastic noise (`RNG() * sin(phase)`) that prevents premature convergence. The oscillator coupling demonstrates how γ + η = C arises from local rules — the system reaches competence C when the noise is neither too high (chaos) nor too low (premature lock-in). See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

DEFINITION: A **spreadsheet projection** is a 3rd-person rendering of a PLATO room: every tile, every agent response, every knowledge artifact becomes a cell in a 2D grid with formulas, dependencies, and confidence scores. This library implements the full bridge — PLATO rooms are first-class citizens; the spreadsheet is the human's view of them. It provides dependency graph analysis, bottleneck detection, break-point identification, cascade simulation, and a "Deckboss mode" that renders the same data as an interactive flowchart.
SECTIONS: Spreadsheet Projection — PLATO Rooms as Living Spreadsheets | Why It Matters | How It Works | Cell Model | Cascade Analysis | Bottleneck Detection | Break-Point Detection | Deckboss Flowchart Mode | Group Mathematics | Quick Start | Run the demo (no dependencies — pure Python stdlib) | Create the bridge
MATH: This is **BFS traversal** of the dependents subgraph. Time complexity: O(V + E) where V = affected cells and E = dependency edges among them. Space: O(V). | **Worst case:** A single root cell that all others depend on triggers O(N) recalculation. Average case for sparse dependency graphs is much better — typically O(log N) affected cells per change. | This is O(N log N) for the sort (N = total cells, returning top-10). In practice, the sort is on a small list because most cells have zero or one dependent. | Spreadsheet Projection is the **human interface layer** of the SuperInstance constellation. In the conservation law **γ + η = C**, the projection makes γ and η **visible**: cell confidence reflects γ 
ARCHITECTURE: Spreadsheet Projection is the **human interface layer** of the SuperInstance constellation. In the conservation law **γ + η = C**, the projection makes γ and η **visible**: cell confidence reflects γ quality (high-confidence = well-supported generation), dependency density reflects η connectivity (many edges = rich pulse exchange). A healthy fleet produces a spreadsheet with high average confidence and moderate connectivity. A degrading fleet shows break points — low-confidence cells with many dependencies — before any human-visible symptoms appear. The projection integrates with the PLATO room architecture as the primary rendering surface for fleet operators. See the [SuperInstance Architecture](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

ARCHITECTURE: In SuperInstance, SSA form represents the fleet's data-flow dependencies. Each ship's conservation state (γ, η) is treated as an SSA value — when a ship reports a new state, it gets a fresh version. The φ-functions merge state from multiple paths (e.g., when a ship restarts). This enables precise reasoning about the γ + η = C conservation law across the fleet's temporal evolution. See [Architecture](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

ARCHITECTURE: Strategy Ecology provides the evolutionary dynamics layer for agent populations in SuperInstance. In γ + η = C, cooperator growth is γ (growth — strategies that benefit the population expand), defector predation is η (avoidance — exploitative strategies are eventually contained), and explorers provide the diversity that prevents ecological collapse. Integrates with `ternary-benchmark` for evolutionary simulation benchmarks and `ternary-adversarial` for robustness testing of evolved strategies. DEFINITION: **Strategy ecology** is the study of how strategic agent populations evolve over time when agents interact through game-theoretic payoff structures. This library implements a complete pipeline: agents choose **ternary strategies** (Rock, Paper, Scissors), interact pairwise via a payoff matrix, get classified into **five ecological species** based on fitness, and their population dynamics are modeled by a
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: strategy-ecology-c
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, fleet, compute, math
DEFINITION: **Strategy ecology** is the study of how strategic agent populations evolve over time when agents interact through game-theoretic payoff structures. This library implements a complete pipeline: agents choose **ternary strategies** (Rock, Paper, Scissors), interact pairwise via a payoff matrix, get classified into **five ecological species** based on fitness, and their population dynamics are modeled by a **Lotka-Volterra system** with Euler integration. The result is a predictive model for whether strategic diversity is stable or will collapse to monoculture.
SECTIONS: Strategy Ecology — Game Theory Meets Population Dynamics for Ternary Agents | Why It Matters | How It Works | Pipeline Overview | Step 1: Ternary Strategy Assignment | Step 2: Pairwise Payoff Evaluation | Step 3: Species Classification | Step 4: Lotka-Volterra Dynamics | Step 5: Coexistence Check | Fitness Landscape | Quick Start | Build
MATH: **Big-O:** `population_interact()` is O(N²) — all-pairs evaluation. For N = 200 agents, this is 19,900 pairwise comparisons. At ~10ns per comparison (array lookup), this is ~200μs — trivially fast on  | **Integration:** Forward Euler with dt = 0.01. Per step: O(S²) where S = number of species (5), so 25 multiply-adds per step — sub-microsecond. | | `population_create(n, seed)` | Allocate N agents with random strategies | O(N) | | | `population_interact(pop)` | All-pairs payoff evaluation | O(N²) | | | `population_classify(pop)` | Assign species by fitness tertile | O(N) |
ARCHITECTURE: This library is the **on-device strategy engine** for the SuperInstance constellation. In the conservation law **γ + η = C**, strategy ecology predicts how γ (generation energy) should be allocated across competing agent strategies. When three strategies coexist, γ is diversified — the system is resilient to any single strategy's failure. When one strategy dominates, γ is concentrated — the system is efficient but fragile. The library pairs with `lotka-volterra-agents-c` (the general dynamics engine) to form the complete ecological prediction layer for edge deployment. See the [SuperInstance Architecture](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

documents.name: strategy-transfer
documents.description:
CRATE: strategy-transfer
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, crypto, protocol, math, search
DEFINITION: **Strategy transfer** tests whether ternary strategies — sequences of {-1, 0, +1} choices optimized in one domain — generalize to different domains. This crate runs controlled transfer experiments: train a strategy on a source domain, apply it to a target domain, and compare against training from scratch. The key finding: cross-domain transfer is **neutral** — no positive or negative transfer.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: strategy-transfer
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, crypto, protocol, math, search
DEFINITION: **Strategy transfer** tests whether ternary strategies — sequences of {-1, 0, +1} choices optimized in one domain — generalize to different domains. This crate runs controlled transfer experiments: train a strategy on a source domain, apply it to a target domain, and compare against training from scratch. The key finding: cross-domain transfer is **neutral** — no positive or negative transfer.
SECTIONS: Strategy Transfer | Why It Matters | How It Works | Domain Definition | Training Protocol | Transfer Metrics | Complexity | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References | License
MATH: In machine learning, "transfer learning" is the holy grail — train once, deploy everywhere. But does this hold for ternary strategies? If a strategy that wins in domain A also wins in domain B, we've  | - Training: O(N · L) where N = budget, L = strategy length | - Transfer evaluation: O(L) per domain | - Baseline: O(N · L) for N random samples | Strategy Transfer validates a crucial property of the γ + η = C framework: competence C is **domain-specific**. A strategy that maximizes γ (constructive choices) in one domain does not generalize — t
ARCHITECTURE: Strategy Transfer validates a crucial property of the γ + η = C framework: competence C is **domain-specific**. A strategy that maximizes γ (constructive choices) in one domain does not generalize — the avoidance patterns (η) that work in one reward landscape are noise in another. The neutral transfer result means the fleet must train per-domain; it cannot shortcut via transfer. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

DEFINITION: The **core Agent trait** for the SuperInstance ecosystem — a 2-method trait with structural conservation. Every agent in the fleet implements `receive(Bottle) → Bottle` and `inspect() → AgentReport`. Lifecycle, conservation, and quality emerge from the message protocol, not from trait complexity.
SECTIONS: superinstance-agent-trait | Why It Matters | Design Philosophy | How It Works | The
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: superinstance-agent-trait
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, fleet, protocol, math, search
DEFINITION: The **core Agent trait** for the SuperInstance ecosystem — a 2-method trait with structural conservation. Every agent in the fleet implements `receive(Bottle) → Bottle` and `inspect() → AgentReport`. Lifecycle, conservation, and quality emerge from the message protocol, not from trait complexity.
SECTIONS: superinstance-agent-trait | Why It Matters | Design Philosophy | How It Works | The Agent Trait | Lifecycle as Emergent Behavior | Conservation Law: γ + η = C | Forgemaster: Reference Implementation | Complexity | Quick Start | API | `Agent` Trait
MATH: ### Conservation Law: γ + η = C | $$\text{trit\_sum}(\text{bottle}_{\text{in}}) = \text{trit\_sum}(\text{bottle}_{\text{out}})$$ | $$\text{quality}(n) = \begin{cases} [+1, 0, +1] & n \bmod 3 = 0 \\ [0, 0, 0] & n \bmod 3 = 1 \\ [-1, 0, +1] & n \bmod 3 = 2 \end{cases}$$ | $$\text{EWMA}_{t+1} = 0.3 \cdot q_t + 0.7 \cdot \text{EWMA}_t$$ | | `receive` | $O(|\text{trits}|)$ (sum + transform) | $O(|\text{trits}|)$ |
ARCHITECTURE: This crate is **the heart of the SuperInstance ecosystem**. The conservation law $\gamma + \eta = C$ is not merely documented here — it is **mechanically enforced** by `AgentRunner::receive()`. Every agent in the fleet, from the Forgemaster to future specialized agents, passes through this enforcement layer. The 2-method trait ensures that conservation is **structural** (a property of the type system) rather than **conventional** (a property that developers must remember to check). The `AgentRunner` is the **conservation boundary**: inside the runner, agents can manipulate trits freely; outside, the runner guarantees that what goes in must come out with the same sum. This is analogous to a physicist's control volume in thermodynamics — the boundary where we account for all energy entering and leaving.

The asset worker embodies the conservation law $\gamma + \eta = C$ in its design: the input parameters $\gamma$ (`title`, `desc`) are transformed deterministically into the output $\eta$ (the SVG image), and the transformation preserves all information — nothing is lost, added, or corrupted. The conservation watermark in every OG image is a visual reminder of this principle. The status badge endpoint is a **conservation probe**: it verifies that the fleet API (the system's $C$) is reachable and responsive. A green badge confirms the system is conserved; red indicates a conservation violation (system unavailable).

ARCHITECTURE: SuperInstance Embedder is the semantic indexing layer — it maps the fleet's output into a searchable knowledge space. Each crate's embedding captures its γ (constructive purpose: what it builds) and its cross-domain connections (synergy potential). The 32-dimensional space is coarse by design — it captures *relationships*, not fine-grained code semantics. Vectorize provides the ANN search that makes this practical at scale. In the γ + η = C framework, the embedder measures the diversity dimension of C: how broadly the fleet's competence spans. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: superinstance-knowledge
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, fleet, graph, compute, protocol, math, systems, search
DEFINITION: The **knowledge mine and research repository** for the SuperInstance ecosystem. Contains theoretical foundations, experimental results, cross-pollination analyses, and formal proofs underpinning the ternary computing architecture.
SECTIONS: superinstance-knowledge | Why It Matters | Knowledge Domains | How It Works | The Conservation Law γ + η = C | Cross-Pollination Methodology | Fleet Synergy Analysis | Embedding Analysis | Quick Start | Browse the Knowledge Base | View all domains | Read formal proofs
MATH: ### The Conservation Law γ + η = C | $$\forall \text{ agent interactions}: \sum_{i} \gamma_i + \sum_{j} \eta_j = C$$ | $$P_{\text{ensemble}}(x) = \sum_{m} w_m \cdot P_m(x) \quad \text{where } \sum w_m = 1$$ | $$S = \frac{Q_{\text{fleet}}}{Q_{\text{best\_solo}}}$$ | $$\text{coverage} = \frac{|\text{unique clusters}|}{|\text{theoretical clusters}|}$$
ARCHITECTURE: This repository IS the **$C$ in $\gamma + \eta = C$** — it is the accumulated knowledge constant against which all agent interactions are measured. When agents produce new insights ($\eta$), those insights must be consistent with the existing knowledge base ($C$). When agents consume knowledge ($\gamma$), that knowledge informs their output ($\eta$). The repository grows monotonically — knowledge is never destroyed, only refined — reflecting the **entropy-defying** nature of the conservation law. The knowledge mine is organized as a **directed acyclic graph (DAG)** of dependencies: proofs depend on axioms, experiments depend on proofs, cross-pollination depends on experiments, and so on. This DAG structure ensures that any conclusion can be traced back to its foundational axioms.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: symplectic-map
CONCEPTS: conservation, wavelet, math
DEFINITION: A Rust library for **symplectic integration** and **area-preserving maps** — numerical methods that exactly conserve the Hamiltonian structure of mechanical systems and dynamical systems over long time periods.
SECTIONS: Symplectic Map | Why It Matters | How It Works | Hamiltonian Systems | Störmer-Verlet (Leapfrog) — 2nd Order Symplectic | Yoshida 4th Order — Symplectic Composition | Standard Map (Chirikov) | Energy Drift Measure | Complexity | Quick Start | API | `Hamiltonian` trait
MATH: Yoshida (1990) showed how to compose three Verlet steps with specially chosen timesteps to cancel the O(dt²) error term: | Result: 4th-order symplectic with error O(dt⁴), at the cost of 3× the Verlet cost. | | Verlet | O(1) — 2 force evals | O(dt²) | Bounded | | | Yoshida4 | O(1) — 6 force evals | O(dt⁴) | Bounded | | | RK4 | O(1) — 4 evals | O(dt⁴) | **Unbounded** (linear drift) |
ARCHITECTURE: In SuperInstance, symplectic integration models the fleet's resource dynamics. The γ + η = C conservation law is a Hamiltonian constraint: γ (position-like, known exploitation) and η (momentum-like, exploration drive) are conjugate variables. Symplectic integration ensures the conservation law is maintained exactly over long fleet lifetimes, unlike generic ODE solvers that would slowly violate it. See [Architecture](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

Each ship's position in configuration space has a tangent space of achievable velocities (conservation ra
ARCHITECTURE: In SuperInstance, the tangent bundle represents the space of possible fleet state transitions. Each ship's position in configuration space has a tangent space of achievable velocities (conservation rate-of-change). The γ + η = C conservation law constrains motion to a level set of the Hamiltonian — a submanifold of the tangent bundle. The hairy ball theorem implies that fleet-wide "combative" strategies (continuous vector fields without singularities) are impossible on even-dimensional fleet topologies. See [Architecture](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: tdigest
CONCEPTS: crypto, systems, search
DEFINITION: A Rust implementation of the **t-digest** — a data structure for online estimation of quantiles (median, p99, p999) with bounded error and small memory footprint, even for infinite data streams.
SECTIONS: T-Digest — Streaming Quantile Estimation | Why It Matters | How It Works | Centroids | Scale Function | Insertion | Quantile Query | Complexity | Accuracy | Compared to Alternatives | Quick Start | API
MATH: Computing exact quantiles requires sorting all data — O(n log n) time and O(n) space. For a stream of billions of events (latency monitoring, sensor data, financial ticks), this is infeasible. The t-d | | Add one value | O(k) to find closest centroid | O(k) | | | Compress | O(k log k) | O(k) | | | Quantile query | O(k) after compress | O(1) | | | T-digest | O(k) ~ 1KB | Very low at tails | ✓ |
ARCHITECTURE: In SuperInstance, t-digests monitor fleet-wide metrics: ship response times, conservation law deviation (|γ + η - C|), and inter-ship message latency. The Cocapn queries p99 quantiles to detect outliers — ships whose performance is in the bottom 1% trigger rebalancing. T-digest's mergeability enables distributed aggregation: each ship maintains its own digest, and the Cocapn merges them for fleet-wide quantile queries. See [Architecture](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

ARCHITECTURE: In SuperInstance, tensor-core provides the memory abstraction for all fleet numerical computation. Ship telemetry is stored as tensors; conservation states (γ, η vectors) are tensor columns; and the harness's optimization operates on tensor gradients. The row-major layout aligns with BLAS expectations for efficient computation within the γ + η = C conservation budget. ARCHITECTURE: Ternary Adversarial provides the security testing layer for agent strategies in SuperInstance. In γ + η = C, adversarial attacks exploit γ (growth — finding inputs that cause incorrect expansion) while robustness scoring and training implement η (avoidance — hardening against exploitable weaknesses). The `DecisionTable` output integrates with `ternary-agent` as an alternative to function-based strategies. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md) for agent security architecture.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: ternary-anchor
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, fleet, graph, compute, storage, systems, search
DEFINITION: **Stability and persistence for rooms in dynamic ternary fleet environments.**
SECTIONS: ternary-anchor | Why It Matters | How It Works | Anchor State Machine | Anchor Chain and Scope | Drift Detection (Anchor Watch) | Stable Ground Selection | Anchorage Zones | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References
MATH: This crate formalizes the lifecycle: **deploy → set → drag → stow**, with quantitative drift detection and capacity-bounded anchorage zones. The anchor watch mechanism provides O(1) drift checks per t | $$h' = \max(0,\; h - \Delta w)$$ | **Time complexity:** All transitions are $O(1)$ — constant-time enum assignments with no allocation. | $$\text{scope} = \frac{L_{\text{deployed}}}{d} \geq 1.0$$ | $$L'_{\text{deployed}} = \max(0,\; L_{\text{deployed}} - \Delta L)$$
ARCHITECTURE: The anchor system maps to the SuperInstance conservation law **γ + η = C** (growth plus entropy equals constant). An anchor in the **Set** state represents low entropy ($\eta \to 0$) — energy is invested in holding position, and the system is maximally ordered. When the anchor drags, entropy increases as positional information degrades. The `wear()` operation models the gradual conversion of growth capacity ($\gamma$) into entropy as hold strength diminishes: $$\gamma_{\text{available}} = C - \eta_{\text{wear}}$$ The anchorage capacity bound ensures the total "holding energy" of the fleet remains bounded by $C$, preventing resource exhaustion. When anchorage is full (`occupied = capacity`), no further growth is possible in that zone — a direct enforcement of the conservation law.

MATH: This crate enforces that invariant through a `Conservation` tracker with $O(1)$ per-record overhead. Combined with multi-index retrieval (by category, key, and value) and a structured lifecycle (Activ | $$\text{Scroll} = (\text{id},\; \text{category},\; \text{key},\; v,\; t)$$ | | `by_category` | `String` | `Vec<u64>` | $O(1)$ average | | | `by_key` | `String` | `Vec<u64>` | $O(1)$ average | | | `by_value` | `Ternary` | `Vec<u64>` | $O(1)$ average |
ARCHITECTURE: The archive enforces the SuperInstance conservation law **γ + η = C**. Each ternary value contributes to the system's total charge: $+1$ values increase $\gamma$ (growth), $-1$ values increase $\eta$ (entropy), and $0$ values are neutral. The conservation invariant requires: $$\sum_{i} v_i = \sum_{i} \gamma_i - \sum_{i} \eta_i \approx 0$$ The `Conservation` tracker directly computes this sum in $O(1)$ per insertion. A balanced archive ($S = 0$) means the system is at equilibrium — growth and entropy are in perfect tension, satisfying the constraint $\gamma + \eta = C$.

CRATE: ternary-arena
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, fleet, compute, math, search
DEFINITION: **Multi-agent competition arena for balanced ternary systems.**
SECTIONS: ternary-arena | Why It Matters | How It Works | Ternary Game Rules | Match Resolution | Tournament Structure | Scoreboard and Leaderboard | Spectator Analytics | Nash Equilibrium Note | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes
MATH: $$\text{Pos}(+1) \succ \text{Neg}(-1) \succ \text{Zero}(0) \succ \text{Pos}(+1)$$ | $$M(a, b) = \begin{cases} 3 & \text{if } a \text{ dominates } b \\ 1 & \text{if } a = b \text{ (draw)} \\ 0 & \text{if } b \text{ dominates } a \end{cases}$$ | $$\text{Score}_A = \sum_{t=0}^{r-1} M(a_t, b_t), \qquad \text{Score}_B = \sum_{t=0}^{r-1} M(b_t, a_t)$$ | **Complexity:** $O(r)$ for $r$ rounds. Each round involves two modular lookups ($O(1)$) and one payoff table lookup ($O(1)$). | $$\text{matches} = n - 1 \quad \text{(for power-of-two } n\text{)}$$
ARCHITECTURE: The arena operates within the SuperInstance conservation law **γ + η = C**. Each match converts strategic energy ($\gamma$) into outcome entropy ($\eta$): a decisive result concentrates information (low $\eta$), while a draw maximizes entropy ($\eta \to C$) since no resolution occurs. The cyclic dominance structure ensures that no single strategy dominates indefinitely — the system oscillates around the Nash equilibrium, maintaining the conservation bound. Tournament brackets progressively concentrate $\gamma$ into the champion, representing the maximum-likelihood best strategy. The elimination process is an information funnel: $n$ agents enter with $n$ strategies, but only one emerges, having accumulated the $\gamma$ of defeated opponents.

MATH: $$\text{Attentio
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: ternary-attention
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, graph, crypto, compute, protocol, math, systems, search
DEFINITION: **Attention mechanisms for balanced ternary sequences in neural architectures.**
SECTIONS: ternary-attention | Why It Matters | How It Works | Scaled Dot-Product Attention | Ternary-to-Dense Embedding | Multi-Head Attention | Cross-Attention | Masked (Causal) Attention | Attention Pattern Analysis | Ternary Compatibility Function | Quick Start | API
MATH: $$\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d}}\right) V$$ | $$\text{softmax}(x_i) = \frac{e^{x_i - \max(\mathbf{x})}}{\sum_j e^{x_j - \max(\mathbf{x})}}$$ | **Complexity:** $O(n_q \cdot n_k \cdot d)$ for score computation, $O(n_q \cdot n_k)$ for softmax, $O(n_q \cdot n_k \cdot d_v)$ for the weighted sum. Total: $O(n_q \cdot n_k \cdot d)$. | $$e_i = v \cdot \frac{i+1}{d}, \qquad v \in \{-1, 0, +1\},\; i \in \{0, \ldots, d-1\}$$ | $$\text{MultiHead}(X) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1, \ldots, \text{head}_h)$$
ARCHITECTURE: Attention directly instantiates the SuperInstance conservation law **γ + η = C**. The softmax operation is a **partition function normalization**: it distributes a unit of "attention energy" across positions. Focused attention (low entropy) concentrates $\gamma$ — energy is directed at specific positions, creating structured order. Diffuse attention (high entropy) distributes $\eta$ — energy is spread uniformly, approaching maximum information entropy. The scaling factor $1/\sqrt{d}$ acts as a **thermodynamic regulator**: without it, large dot products would create near-deterministic attention (all energy concentrated at one position), violating the conservation bound by pushing $\gamma$ beyond $C$. Multi-head attention partitions the total energy $C$ across $h$ heads, each operating on $C/h$ effective energy. This ensures that no single head can dominate the representational budget.

Each CA generation transforms the state vector, redistributing information between structure ($\gamma$ — ordered patterns) and randomness ($\eta$ — disordered states). Rules like **Majority** minimize entropy: $\gamma \to C$, $\eta \to 0$, producing stable domains. Rules like **ternary Rule 110** operate at the edge of chaos: $\gamma \approx \eta$, producing complex structures at intermediate entropy. The entropy evolution curve $H(t)$ directly visualizes the $\gamma$/$\eta$ trade-off over time. The finite state space guarantees eventual periodicity: the CA must settle into a cycle where $\gamma + \eta = C$ is exactly maintained — the system has found its equilibrium within the conservation law.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: ternary-backpressure
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, graph, compute, systems
DEFINITION: **Backpressure management for GPU pipelines with ternary pressure signals.**
SECTIONS: ternary-backpressure | Why It Matters | How It Works | Ternary Pressure Signals | Adaptive Flow Control | Congestion Detection and Spreading | Weighted Fairness Allocation | Pipeline Simulation | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References
MATH: $$\text{signal}(u) = \begin{cases} \text{Overloaded} & \text{if } u > 0.8 \\ \text{Balanced} & \text{if } 0.4 < u \leq 0.8 \\ \text{Ready} & \text{if } u \leq 0.4 \end{cases}$$ | $$\text{combine}(\mathbf{s}) = \begin{cases} \text{Overloaded} & \text{if any } s_i = \text{Overloaded} \\ \text{Balanced} & \text{if any } s_i = \text{Balanced} \text{ (and none overloaded)} \\ \text | **Complexity:** $O(k)$ for $k$ signals being combined. | $$\tau_i^{(t+1)} = \text{clamp}\!\left(\tau_i^{(t)} + \Delta(s),\; 0,\; 1\right)$$ | $$\Delta(s) = \begin{cases} -\alpha & \text{if } s = \text{Overloaded} \\ 0 & \text{if } s = \text{Balanced} \\ +\alpha & \text{if } s = \text{Ready} \end{cases}$$
ARCHITECTURE: Backpressure is the flow-control manifestation of the SuperInstance conservation law **γ + η = C**. Data flowing through the pipeline represents **γ** (growth/throughput), while buffer occupancy represents **η** (entropy/accumulated backlog). The conservation law demands: $$\text{throughput} + \text{backlog} \leq C$$ When backlog grows ($\eta \uparrow$), the Overloaded signal propagates upstream, reducing throughput ($\gamma \downarrow$) to maintain $\gamma + \eta = C$. The throttle factor $\tau$ is the mechanism enforcing this: $\tau$ approaches 0 when $\eta$ approaches $C$, effectively halting new growth.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: ternary-bayesian
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, graph, storage, protocol
DEFINITION: **Bayesian inference for ternary variables on $\{-1, 0, +1\}$.**
SECTIONS: ternary-bayesian | Why It Matters | How It Works | Ternary Distributions | Bayesian Updating | KL Divergence | Bayesian Networks | Variational Inference | Naive Bayes Classification | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes
MATH: $$P(X = x) = \begin{cases} p_{-} & \text{if } x = -1 \\ p_{0} & \text{if } x = 0 \\ p_{+} & \text{if } x = +1 \end{cases}, \qquad p_{-} + p_{0} + p_{+} = 1$$ | $$H(X) = -\sum_{x} p_x \log_2 p_x$$ | $$\mu = E[X] = (-1) \cdot p_{-} + 0 \cdot p_{0} + 1 \cdot p_{+} = p_{+} - p_{-}$$ | $$\sigma^2 = E[X^2] - \mu^2 = (p_{-} + p_{+}) - (p_{+} - p_{-})^2$$ | $$P(X \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid X) \cdot P(X)}{\sum_{x'} P(E \mid x') \cdot P(x')}$$
ARCHITECTURE: Bayesian inference is the probabilistic engine of the SuperInstance conservation law **γ + η = C**. The prior distribution represents the initial allocation of $\gamma$ (belief mass on each ternary value). Observations update the posterior, redistributing mass between $\gamma$ (high-confidence regions) and $\eta$ (uncertainty/entropy). The entropy of the posterior distribution directly measures $\eta$: high entropy means the evidence was uninformative ($\eta \to C$, conserving uncertainty); low entropy means the evidence was decisive ($\gamma \to C$, concentrating belief). The KL divergence between prior and posterior quantifies the **information gain** — the amount of $\eta$ converted to $\gamma$ by the observation. Belief propagation in Bayesian networks spreads information along graph edges, flowing $\gamma$ from observed nodes to unobserved neighbors, always maintaining the conservation bound $\gamma + \eta \leq C$.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: ternary-bridge
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, fleet, graph, crypto, storage, protocol, systems
DEFINITION: **Bridge pattern for connecting heterogeneous ternary systems with protocol and codec translation.**
SECTIONS: ternary-bridge | Why It Matters | How It Works | The Bridge Trait | Protocol Bridge | Codec Bridge | Memory Bridge | Skill Bridge | Bridge Registry | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes
MATH: $$\text{forward}: A \to B, \qquad \text{backward}: B \to A$$ | **Complexity:** Translation complexity is $O(n)$ in the payload size for all implemented bridges, involving element-wise mapping. | $$\text{byte} = (\text{nibble}_{\text{high}} \ll 4) \;|\; \text{nibble}_{\text{low}}$$ | **Complexity:** $O(n)$ for encoding/decoding $n$ trits across all formats. | **Complexity:** $O(1)$ average for register/get/remove; $O(n)$ for listing all bridges.
ARCHITECTURE: Bridges are the **information channels** of the SuperInstance conservation law **γ + η = C**. Each bridge translates representations while preserving information content — the $\gamma$ (structured meaning) passes through unchanged, while only the encoding (carrier of $\eta$, the representational overhead) transforms. A perfect bridge is **information-preserving**: $\text{backward}(\text{forward}(x)) = x$. Any deviation introduces $\eta$ — representational entropy from lossy translation. The packed codec format minimizes $\eta$ by using the fewest bits per trit (2 trits/byte vs. 1 trit/byte), while the text format trades higher $\eta$ for human readability. The bridge registry maps to the conservation principle's requirement that all channels between subsystems be accounted for: unregistered bridges are information leaks that can violate $\gamma + \eta \leq C$.

DEFINITION: **Communication bus for inter-room messaging with ternary payloads.**

MATH: $$\text{deliver}(m, s) \iff s.\text{topics} = \emptyset \;\vee\; m.\text{topic} \in s.\text{topics}$$ | **Complexity:** $O(S)$ per
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: ternary-bus
CONCEPTS: ternary, fleet, compute, search
DEFINITION: **Communication bus for inter-room messaging with ternary payloads.**
SECTIONS: ternary-bus | Why It Matters | How It Works | Publish/Subscribe Model | Queue Overflow Handling | Topic Router | Health Metrics | Backpressure Detection | Message Queue | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes
MATH: $$\text{deliver}(m, s) \iff s.\text{topics} = \emptyset \;\vee\; m.\text{topic} \in s.\text{topics}$$ | **Complexity:** $O(S)$ per publish for $S$ subscribers, with $O(1)$ per matched subscriber for enqueue. | $$Q.\text{push}(m): \quad \text{if } |Q| = C \text{ then } Q.\text{pop\_front}();\; \text{dropped} \mathrel{+}= 1$$ | $$\text{recipients}(\text{topic}) = \text{routes}[\text{topic}] \cup \text{global}$$ | **Complexity:** $O(|\text{routes}[\text{topic}]| + |\text{global}|)$ per resolution.
ARCHITECTURE: The message bus is the **communication artery** of the SuperInstance conservation law **γ + η = C**. Messages carry $\gamma$ (structured information between rooms) through the bus channel. Queue depth represents $\eta$ — information buffered but not yet consumed. When queue depth approaches capacity ($\eta \to C$), the drop-oldest policy converts old $\gamma$ into $\eta$ (entropy increases as data is lost). The conservation bound is maintained: total information in the system (delivered + queued + dropped) equals the total published. The backpressure signal is the bus's manifestation of the conservation law: when $\eta$ exceeds threshold $\tau$, the system signals producers to reduce $\gamma$ input, restoring the equilibrium $\gamma + \eta \approx C$.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: ternary-cartograph
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, fleet, graph, crypto, compute, protocol, math, search
DEFINITION: A spatial mapping and fleet topology visualization library. Provides 2D point geometry, bounding regions, territory claims with conflict resolution, multi-resolution map scales, and dimensionality-reduction projections for rendering high-dimensional fleet state onto human-readable 2D maps.
SECTIONS: ternary-cartograph | Why It Matters | How It Works | Euclidean Geometry | Bounding Box Computation | AABB Overlap Test | Cartographic Projection | Nearest-Marker Query | Discrete Map Scale | Complexity | Quick Start | API
MATH: Within the **γ + η = C** framework: | | γ | Spatial state: marker positions, region bounds, territory claims | | $$d(p_1, p_2) = \sqrt{(x_1 - x_2)^2 + (y_1 - y_2)^2}$$ | For a set of *n* markers, the bounding box is computed in O(n): | $$x_{\min} = \min_i x_i, \quad x_{\max} = \max_i x_i, \quad y_{\min} = \min_i y_i, \quad y_{\max} = \max_i y_i$$
ARCHITECTURE: The cartograph is a **constructive** model: you accumulate spatial data via the builder, then freeze it into a `FleetMap` for queries. This separates the mutation phase (agents moving, regions being defined) from the query phase (operators inspecting the map). The `TerritoryMark` conflict resolution uses a simple priority integer — this is deliberately human-assignable rather than automatically computed, because territory disputes in agent fleets are policy decisions, not geometric ones. The `CartographicProjection` is intentionally simple (weighted sum, not PCA) because it runs in O(d) per point and must execute every frame for real-time visualization. For offline analysis, use a proper PCA or UMAP implementation.

MATH: Topological sort: **O(V + E)** (Kahn's algorithm). Cycle detection: **O(V + E)** (DFS). | 1. Find parents of X: **O(V)** | 2. Backdoor adjustment over parent set Z: **O(|Z| · 3^|Z|)** (ternary enumeration) | Counterfactual: **O(3^|U|)** in general, **O(|U|)** for linear models. | CI test for ternary: χ² test on 3×3 contingency table. Cost: **O(N)** per test (N = samples). Total: **O(V² · N)** worst case.
ARCHITECTURE: Ternary Causality provides causal reasoning for fleet strategy evaluation in SuperInstance. In γ + η = C, the do-calculus answers "what causes γ (growth)?" while counterfactuals reveal η (what we avoid by choosing γ over alternatives). The conservation law γ + η = C is itself a causal claim: the sum is invariant under strategy perturbations that conserve total effort. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md) for causal inference architecture.

documents.name: ternary-cell
documents.description:
CRATE: ternary-cell
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, crypto, compute, storage, systems, search
DEFINITION: The **atomic unit of scale** — a 3-byte ternary cell that is stack-allocated, zero-allocation, and instantiable by the million. Every cell tracks three things: its state {-1, 0, +1}, how long it has been in that state (dwell), and how many times it has changed (flips). Everything else is computed from populations.
SECTIONS: Ternary Cell | Why It Matters | How It Works | Cell Layout | State
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: ternary-cell
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, crypto, compute, storage, systems, search
DEFINITION: The **atomic unit of scale** — a 3-byte ternary cell that is stack-allocated, zero-allocation, and instantiable by the million. Every cell tracks three things: its state {-1, 0, +1}, how long it has been in that state (dwell), and how many times it has changed (flips). Everything else is computed from populations.
SECTIONS: Ternary Cell | Why It Matters | How It Works | Cell Layout | State Transitions | Population Statistics | Complexity | Quick Start | API | Cell | Population Functions | Architecture Notes
MATH: All transitions are O(1): | **Census**: $(n_{-1}, n_0, n_{+1})$ — counts in O(N). | **Gamma** (signed charge): $\gamma = \sum_i s_i$ — the net ternary value. O(N). | **Shannon entropy**: $H = -\sum_{v \in \{-1,0,1\}} p_v \log_2 p_v$ where $p_v = n_v / N$. Maximum $H = \log_2 3 \approx 1.585$. O(N). | **Flip rate**: $\bar{f} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_i \text{flips}_i$ — measures population volatility. O(N).
ARCHITECTURE: The cell is the irreducible unit of the **γ + η = C** conservation principle: - **γ (structure)**: the state assignment — which cells are -1, 0, +1 - **η (dynamics)**: the stream of ticks, flips, tunnels, and traps that perturb the population

ARCHITECTURE: The ternary cell is the atomic primitive of the γ + η = C framework. The three values map directly: **+1 = γ** (constructive, chosen, present), **−1 = η** (avoidant, rejected, absent), **0 = neutral** (undecided, unknown, transitional). A grid of ternary cells computes the dynamics of γ and η interaction over space and time, producing emergent competence C. This is the discrete computational substrate on which all SuperInstance ternary experiments run. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: ternary-cipher
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, graph, crypto, math
DEFINITION: **Ternary Cipher** implements a complete suite of cryptographic primitives over the finite field **GF(3)** — one-time pads, Feistel ciphers, substitution boxes, hash commitments, Shamir secret sharing, and Merkle trees — all using balanced ternary arithmetic on **T = {−1, 0, +1}**. The crate is `#![no_std]` and `#![forbid(unsafe_code)]`, making it suitable for embedded ternary processors and constrained environments.
SECTIONS: Ternary Cipher — Cryptographic Primitives over Z₃ | Why It Matters | How It Works | Ternary Arithmetic in GF(3) | One-Time Pad (OTP) | Feistel Cipher | Substitution Box (S-Box) | Hash Function | Commitment Scheme | Shamir's Secret Sharing over GF(3) | Merkle Tree | Quick Start
MATH: **Complexity:** O(1) per operation — two integer additions, one modulo, one comparison. | **Complexity:** O(n) for n trits. Key length must equal message length. | **Complexity:** O(r × n/2) for r rounds on n-trit messages. Each round requires n/2 additions + n/2 S-box lookups. | **Complexity:** O(n) for n input trits. | **Complexity:** O(1) — single hash evaluation on 2 trits.
ARCHITECTURE: Ternary Cipher provides the cryptographic foundation for secure agent communication in **SuperInstance**. In the **γ + η = C** framework, encryption preserves the conservation invariant during transmission: the ternary sum of the plaintext is preserved through the one-time pad as Σ(m) = Σ(c) − Σ(k), maintaining C across the channel. The Feistel cipher's invertibility ensures that information is neither created nor destroyed — only permuted and substituted — conserving total information content. Integrates with: - `superinstance-protocol` — encrypted bottle payloads

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: ternary-circuit
CONCEPTS: ternary, fleet, graph, crypto, protocol
DEFINITION: A ternary logic circuit design library implementing multi-valued logic gates, truth-table generation, circuit composition, and minimization. Supports both **Kleene** (strong Kleene, K₃) and **Łukasiewicz** (Ł₃) three-valued logic systems.
SECTIONS: ternary-circuit | Why It Matters | How It Works | Three-Valued Logic Systems | Kleene's Strong Logic (K₃) | Łukasiewicz Logic (Ł₃) | Implication Comparison | XOR in Three-Valued Logic | Truth Table Generation | Circuit Model | Circuit Minimization: Double Negation Elimination | Complexity
MATH: Within the **γ + η = C** framework: | | γ | `Trit` ∈ {False(−1), Unknown(0), True(+1)} — signal values on circuit wires | | $$\text{AND}(a, b) = \min(a, b), \qquad \text{OR}(a, b) = \max(a, b)$$ | $$a \to b = \begin{cases} \text{True} & \text{if } a \leq b \\ \neg a \lor b & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ | $$\text{XOR}(a, b) = \begin{cases} \text{Unknown} & \text{if } a = \text{U} \text{ or } b = \text{U} \\ \text{True} & \text{if } a \neq b \\ \text{False} & \text{if } a = b \end{cases}$$
ARCHITECTURE: The circuit model uses **unsigned integer IDs** for wire references rather than named signals. This avoids string allocation in hot paths and maps directly to hardware description languages (HDLs). The trade-off is that human readability requires a separate symbol table. The choice to support both K₃ and Ł₃ as a runtime parameter (rather than compile-time generic) allows circuits to be re-evaluated under different logics without recompilation — important for agents that may need to switch between conservative and optimistic reasoning modes. The minimizer currently implements only double-negation elimination. A production ternary circuit optimizer would also include: constant propagation (e.g., `AND(x, False) = False`), dead gate elimination, and Quine-McCluskey-style minimization over the $3^k$ truth table.

Cluster expansion: **O(N)** per point. | Cost: **O(N³)** for full dendrogram (N-1 merges, each O(N²) distance update).

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: ternary-command
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, fleet, crypto, protocol, math, search
DEFINITION: A command parsing, dispatch, and audit system where every command execution resolves to one of three outcomes: **Success (+1)**, **Partial (0)**, or **Failure (−1)**. Provides structured parsing, a handler registry, alias expansion, and an append-only history trail.
SECTIONS: ternary-command | Why It Matters | How It Works | Command Pipeline | Parsing | Alias Resolution | History as a Ring Buffer | Result Classification | Complexity | Quick Start | API | `CommandResult`
MATH: Within the **γ + η = C** framework: | | γ | `CommandResult` ∈ {Success(+1), Partial(0), Failure(−1)} | | $$\text{tokens} = \text{filter}(\text{trim}(\text{split}(s, d)), \text{non\text{-}empty})$$ | **Complexity**: O(|s|) where |s| is the input length. | The system preserves any arguments after the first token. Resolution is O(|first_token|) for HashMap lookup.
ARCHITECTURE: The handler type is `fn(&ParsedCommand, &CommandContext) -> CommandResult` — a raw function pointer, not a closure or trait object. This keeps the registry `Send + Sync` without allocation, at the cost of preventing closures that capture state. For stateful handlers, the standard pattern is to have the function pointer read from a shared `Arc<Mutex<State>>` stored externally. The history trail is designed for **audit**, not replay. It stores `ParsedCommand` snapshots (including the raw input string) and the `CommandContext` (agent + location + timestamp), enabling post-hoc forensic analysis: "what did agent-5 do at 14:23?" The alias system performs **single-token expansion only**. It does not support chained aliases (alias A → alias B → command) to prevent infinite loops and keep resolution O(1).

CRATE: ternary-compiler
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, fleet, graph, compute, protocol
DEFINITION: A complete compiler backend for ternary logic programs. Implements a six-stage pipeline: **Lexer → Parser (AST) → Compiler (Bytecode) → Optimizer → IR (CFG + Dominator Tree) → VM**. The source language expresses ternary computations using `neg`, `zero`, `pos` literals, arithmetic/logical operators, rooms, passages, gates, and branching.
SECTIONS: ternary-compiler | Why It Matters | How It Works |
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: ternary-compiler
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, fleet, graph, compute, protocol
DEFINITION: A complete compiler backend for ternary logic programs. Implements a six-stage pipeline: **Lexer → Parser (AST) → Compiler (Bytecode) → Optimizer → IR (CFG + Dominator Tree) → VM**. The source language expresses ternary computations using `neg`, `zero`, `pos` literals, arithmetic/logical operators, rooms, passages, gates, and branching.
SECTIONS: ternary-compiler | Why It Matters | How It Works | Pipeline Overview | Ternary Arithmetic | Lexer | Parser (Recursive Descent) | Compiler (AST → Bytecode) | Optimizer | IR: Control Flow Graph | Dominator Tree | VM Execution
MATH: Within the **γ + η = C** framework: | | γ | `Trit` ∈ {Neg(−1), Zero(0), Pos(+1)} — compiled values | | $$a + b = \begin{cases} \text{Pos} & \text{if } a + b \equiv +1 \pmod{3}^* \\ \text{Zero} & \text{if } a + b = 0 \\ \text{Neg} & \text{if } a + b \equiv -1 \pmod{3}^* \end{cases}$$ | **Complexity**: O(n) where n = source length. | **Complexity**: O(n) with recursive descent; one token consumed per recursive call.
ARCHITECTURE: The compiler is designed as a **reference implementation** for ternary language semantics. Each stage is a clean module boundary so that individual components (lexer, parser, optimizer, VM) can be replaced or reused independently. The `Gate` construct is the ternary generalization of `if/else`: it has three branches (positive, zero, negative) rather than two, matching the three-valued logic model. This is why the IR needs `Merge` opcodes — each gate produces three execution paths that must converge. The VM's `Branch` implementation scans forward for `Merge` opcodes to find path boundaries. A production compiler would use proper labels or computed offsets, but the scan approach keeps the bytecode format simple for debugging and introspection.
CROSS_REFS: relates-to:conservation relates-to:ternary relates-to:fleet relates-to:graph relates-to:compute relates-to:protocol

It takes a high-level `StrategyIR` (intermediate representation), applies dead-code elimination and constant folding optimizations, then emits a `CompiledPolicy` ready for fast evaluation in production.

MATH: 4. **Emit**: Generate `CompiledPolicy` with flat rule array for O(k) evaluation | | Parse | O(R) | R = number of rules | | | Optimize | O(R²) | Pairwise rule comparison | | | Sort | O(R log R) | Priority sort | | | Evaluate (compiled) | O(R) worst, O(1) average | First-match-wins |
ARCHITECTURE: The Ternary Compiler transforms the γ + η = C theory into executable form. The `StrategyIR` captures how γ (constructive actions) and η (avoidant actions) should be dispatched — the *strategy*. The compiler optimizes this strategy by eliminating redundant γ and η operations, producing a leaner `CompiledPolicy` that achieves the same competence C with less computation. The profiler closes the loop: by measuring which rules fire in practice, it identifies η (rules that never fire — dead knowledge) that can be pruned. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

The compiler enables executable t
