CRATE: ternary-scheduler
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, crypto, compute
DEFINITION: Ternary task scheduler with **three-level priority classification** `{+1=urgent, 0=normal, -1=deferred}`, deadline-aware rescheduling, work-stealing parallelism, and load balancing across workers.
SECTIONS: ternary-scheduler | Why It Matters | How It Works | Priority + Deadline Ordering | Automatic Rescheduling (Escalation) | Work-Stealing | Load Balancing | Utilization Metric | Quick Start | API | `Scheduler` | `Task` Builder
MATH: **Complexity:** O(N log N) per full schedule sort. O(1) per insertion (HashMap). | **Complexity:** O(N) per reschedule pass. | **Complexity:** O(W) per steal attempt (scan for busiest/idlest). O(N) per task to find a stealable candidate. | **Complexity:** O(W²) worst case for full balancing (W steal rounds × W scan each). | The **γ + η = C** invariant maps cleanly: *generation* (γ) is the task arrival/creation process, *entropy* (η) is the priority distribution `{+1, 0, -1}` across active tasks, and *conservation* (C) is
ARCHITECTURE: The **γ + η = C** invariant maps cleanly: *generation* (γ) is the task arrival/creation process, *entropy* (η) is the priority distribution `{+1, 0, -1}` across active tasks, and *conservation* (C) is the invariant that total work is preserved — every task is either executing, queued, or completed (never lost). The escalation rule (`0 → +1` near deadline) converts entropy (priority diversity) into generation (urgency-driven execution), maintaining the conservation law that no task misses its deadline without explicit deferral.

MATH: **Complexity:** O(log(hi - lo)) evaluations. **Space:** O(1). | **Complexity:** O(V + E) time, O(V) space. | **Complexity:** O(B · d̄ · B) = O(B² · d̄) per depth, where d̄ is average degree. Total: O(B² · D · d̄). | **Complexity:** O((V + E) log V) time with heap, O(V) space. Worst case degrades to Dijkstra if `h ≡ 0`. | `is_connected(a, b)` runs BFS from *a* and checks if *b* is reached. O(V + E).
ARCHITECTURE: The **γ + η = C** invariant appears in beam search: *generation* (γ) is the expansion of candidates (exploration), *entropy* (η) is the diversity of the beam (how different the *B* candidates are from each other), and *conservation* (C) is the fixed beam width *B* — the constraint that exactly *B* candidates survive each round. The truncation step enforces conservation by pruning candidates, converting generation-driven diversity into entropy-reducing selection.

DEFINITION: **Ternary Search Index** is a search engine where documents and queries are represented as sparse vectors of ternary term weights: {-1 (demote), 0 (neutral), +1 (promote)}. documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: ternary-semaphore
CONCEPTS: ternary, compute, protocol, math, systems
DEFINITION: Ternary semaphore for **GPU resource control** with three-state capacity tracking: `+1` (available), `0` (at capacity), and `-1` (overcommitted). Provides priority-queued acquisition, auto-admission on release, and utilization metrics.
SECTIONS: ternary-semaphore | Why It Matters | How It Works | State Transitions | Acquisition Protocol | Priority Queue Admission | Utilization | Quick Start | API | `TernarySemaphore` | `Permit` | Architecture Notes
MATH: Finding the max-priority permit is O(Q) per admission (linear scan). Total: O(Q²) worst case for full drain. This is acceptable for moderate queue sizes; for very large queues, a `BinaryHeap` would re | - `try_acquire`: O(1) | - `force_acquire`: O(1) | - `release`: O(Q) (priority dequeue) | - `drain_queue`: O(Q²) worst case
ARCHITECTURE: The **γ + η = C** invariant: *generation* (γ) is the permit acquisition process (new work entering the system), *entropy* (η) is the queue depth diversity (how many distinct priority levels are waiting), and *conservation* (C) is the invariant `active ≤ max_permits` under normal operation. The `force_acquire` path deliberately violates C for priority override, entering the `Overcommitted (-1)` state — this is the entropy-overflow valve that prevents deadlock while signaling the violation through the ternary state.

SECTIONS: ternary-shard-split | Why It Matters | How It Works | Even Split | Aligned Split | Layer-Parallel Sharding | Merge and Validate | Quick Start | API | Types | Architecture Notes | References
MATH: **Complexity:** O(T) — single pass with index tracking. | **Complexity:** O(T). No padding overhead for the first N-1 shards. | **Complexity:** O(L) assignments. | **Complexity:** O(S log S + T) for merge (sort + concat), where S = shard count. | The **γ + η = C** invariant: *generation* (γ) is the split operation producing shard boundaries, *entropy* (η) is the load distribution (measured by `imbalance_factor`), and *conservation* (C) is the 
ARCHITECTURE: The **γ + η = C** invariant: *generation* (γ) is the split operation producing shard boundaries, *entropy* (η) is the load distribution (measured by `imbalance_factor`), and *conservation* (C) is the invariant that `merge(split(T, N)) == T` — no trit is lost or duplicated during distribution and reconstruction. The `validate` function explicitly checks C. The aligned split additionally preserves a structural invariant: packed-representation alignment for efficient GPU operations.

documents.description:
CRATE: ternary-shield
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, fleet, graph, compute, search
DEFINITION: **Ternary Shield** implements shielding, containment, and breach analysis on 2D ternary grids. It identifies fully-surrounded regions (shielded cells), detects breach points (weak borders), reinforces protective barriers (cellular automaton growth), and measures shield permeability — the ratio of boundary edges to total shield area.
SECTIONS: Ternary Shield — Containment and Shielding on Ternary Grid
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: ternary-shield
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, fleet, graph, compute, search
DEFINITION: **Ternary Shield** implements shielding, containment, and breach analysis on 2D ternary grids. It identifies fully-surrounded regions (shielded cells), detects breach points (weak borders), reinforces protective barriers (cellular automaton growth), and measures shield permeability — the ratio of boundary edges to total shield area.
SECTIONS: Ternary Shield — Containment and Shielding on Ternary Grids | Why It Matters | How It Works | Shield Detection | Breach Detection | Reinforcement | Permeability | Neighbor Computation | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References
MATH: `shield(grid, width, protect)` finds cells matching the `protect` value that are completely surrounded by same-valued neighbors. A cell is shielded if all 4 orthogonal neighbors share its value. Inter | `breach(grid, width, protect)` identifies weak points: cells matching `protect` that have 1-2 non-protect neighbors. These are the most vulnerable shield cells — small breaches that could expand. Cell | `reinforce(grid, width, protect)` is a cellular automaton step: for each non-protect cell, if the majority of neighbors are protect, flip it to protect. This grows the protected region, filling gaps.  | Where `boundary_edges` are edges between protect and non-protect cells. P = 0 means perfect shield (no leaks); P = 1 means no shield (all boundary). O(N) computation. | | `reinforce(grid, width, protect) → usize` | CA growth step (O(N)) |
ARCHITECTURE: Shielding models fleet resilience in **SuperInstance**. Healthy GPU nodes (+1) form protective clusters; failed nodes (-1) are threats that breach the shield. The γ + η = C conservation maps to the permeability metric: γ = shielded region strength, η = boundary leakage. A well-shielded fleet has low permeability (high γ, low η). See [Architecture](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

The crate provides Spence-style costly signaling (where higher-quality senders pay less for honest signals), cheap-talk (cost-free) baselines, and separating/pooling equilibrium analys
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: ternary-signaling
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, fleet, storage, search
DEFINITION: **Ternary Signaling** implements signaling game theory on a ternary communication channel. Senders with hidden quality (Low, Medium, High) choose signals {-1, 0, +1} at a cost, and receivers respond {Reject, Wait, Accept}. The crate provides Spence-style costly signaling (where higher-quality senders pay less for honest signals), cheap-talk (cost-free) baselines, and separating/pooling equilibrium analysis.
SECTIONS: Ternary Signaling — Signaling Games on {-1, 0, +1} Communication Channels | Why It Matters | How It Works | Cost Structure | Cheap Talk | Game Play | Equilibrium | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References | License
MATH: In this equilibrium, the signal perfectly reveals quality. Verification is O(games × strategies). | Signaling games model inter-agent communication in **SuperInstance**. The ternary channel maps to fleet messages: +1 (endorse), 0 (no comment), -1 (object). The γ + η = C conservation manifests in the
ARCHITECTURE: Signaling games model inter-agent communication in **SuperInstance**. The ternary channel maps to fleet messages: +1 (endorse), 0 (no comment), -1 (object). The γ + η = C conservation manifests in the cost-benefit structure: honest signals have high γ (informed decisions) and low η (deception cost), while dishonest signals have low γ and high η. See [Architecture](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

MATH: **Complexity:** O(L) per hash, where L = item byte length. | **Complexity:** O(D) per estimate, where D = depth. | **Complexity:** O(D × W). | The **γ + η = C** invariant: *generation* (γ) is the insert/remove stream modifying cell values, *entropy* (η) is the information loss from ternary clamping (we can't recover exact frequencies from {-
ARCHITECTURE: The **γ + η = C** invariant: *generation* (γ) is the insert/remove stream modifying cell values, *entropy* (η) is the information loss from ternary clamping (we can't recover exact frequencies from {-1, 0, +1}), and *conservation* (C) is the error bound guarantee — the estimate never underestimates the true clamped frequency (Count-Min property). The tradeoff between γ and η is direct: more insertions (γ↑) increase collisions and thus entropy (η↑), while the conservation law (C) maintains the `min-row` lower bound on estimate accuracy.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: ternary-speculate
CONCEPTS: ternary, fleet, crypto, math, search
DEFINITION: Speculative synchronization for **ternary agent coordination**. Never wait — simulate instead. Each room (agent) maintains three layers: an **execution layer** (what it's doing), a **speculation layer** (what it predicts partners will do), and a **shadow layer** (how things look from each partner's viewpoint).
SECTIONS: ternary-speculate | Why It Matters | How It Works | Hint Vectors | Shadow Tracking | Simulated Responses | T-Minus Events | Speculation Accuracy | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References
MATH: **Complexity:** O(1) per shadow reconciliation. | The **γ + η = C** invariant is the design principle of the entire crate. *Generation* (γ) is the simulation layer producing predicted partner states. *Entropy* (η) is the hint divergence — when predic
ARCHITECTURE: The **γ + η = C** invariant is the design principle of the entire crate. *Generation* (γ) is the simulation layer producing predicted partner states. *Entropy* (η) is the hint divergence — when predictions are wrong, shadows accumulate misses and confidence drops (η↑). *Conservation* (C) is the invariant that every shadow must eventually be reconciled — speculative state is provisional, and the T-minus event system ensures reconciliation deadlines are met. When η exceeds the hint threshold (`has_failures()`), the room triggers re-simulation, restoring the γ-η balance.

DEFINITION: A **ternary logic spreadsheet engine** where every cell holds a value in {-1, 0, +1}. Supports formula evaluation (`SUM`, `AVG`, `ENTROPY`, `EVOLVE`, `BEST`, `SPECIES`, `EXHAUSTIVE`), conditional formatting via fitness coloring, mutation-based autofill, and fitness-sorted row/column operations.

Limited to N ≤ 10 (59,049 combinations). | **Complexity:** O(R log R · C) for row sort, O(C log C · R) for column sort. | The **γ + η = C** invariant is embodied in the EVOLVE formula: *generation* (γ) is the mutation operator producing new ternary values, *entropy* (η) is the Shannon entropy of the cell-value distributi
ARCHITECTURE: The **γ + η = C** invariant is embodied in the EVOLVE formula: *generation* (γ) is the mutation operator producing new ternary values, *entropy* (η) is the Shannon entropy of the cell-value distribution (computed by ENTROPY), and *conservation* (C) is the invariant that the grid dimensions never change — evolution reshuffles values within a fixed-size space. The EXHAUSTIVE search is the conservation enforcement: it proves that no configuration exceeds the found maximum, closing the γ-η loop.

This mimics natural selection: "fitter" rows rise to the top. O(N log N) via quicksort. | The Ternary Spreadsheet is a visual γ + η = C computer. Each cell's value is a ternary decision: +1 (γ — constructive), −1 (η — avoidant), 0 (neutral). Formulas propagate γ and η across dependency edg
ARCHITECTURE: The Ternary Spreadsheet is a visual γ + η = C computer. Each cell's value is a ternary decision: +1 (γ — constructive), −1 (η — avoidant), 0 (neutral). Formulas propagate γ and η across dependency edges, computing the emergent competence C of the grid. The sort engine applies selection pressure (preferring γ over η), and the autofill engine applies mutation (the source of variation). Together, these three operations — evaluate, sort, fill — constitute a complete evolutionary loop running on a spreadsheet grid. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

ARCHITECTURE: The **γ + η = C** invariant is the foundational principle of this crate. *Generation* (γ) is the allocation process — resources being committed to tasks. *Entropy* (η) is the diversity of resource states across budgets (surplus/deficit spread). *Conservation* (C) is the hard constraint that `Σ allocated ≤ Σ capacity` across all budgets — no resource is created or destroyed, only transferred. The steward enforces C by rejecting over-budget allocations, making the conservation law a runtime invariant rather than a post-hoc audit finding.

DEFINITION: When ternary agents share a room, they don't just coexist — they develop relationships. Some help each other (mutualism), some exploit others (parasitism), and some benefit without affecting their neighbor (commensalism). documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: ternary-sync
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, fleet, wavelet, crypto, compute, math, systems
DEFINITION: Synchronization primitives for ternary agents using **Z₃ cyclic rotation**. Models multi-agent phase synchronization where each agent's state ∈ {-1, 0, +1} and the goal is to measure and drive the system toward coherent or anti-coherent configurations.
SECTIONS: ternary-sync | Why It Matters | How It Works | Z₃ Cyclic Rotation | Coupling and Consensus | Coherence Metric | Sync Time | Anti-Synchronization | Phase Offsets | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes
MATH: **Complexity:** O(N · T) where T = ticks to convergence (or 1000). | The **γ + η = C** invariant governs the entire synchronization process. *Generation* (γ) is the rotation operator producing new states. *Entropy* (η) is the coherence loss — when agents diverge, the s
ARCHITECTURE: The **γ + η = C** invariant governs the entire synchronization process. *Generation* (γ) is the rotation operator producing new states. *Entropy* (η) is the coherence loss — when agents diverge, the state distribution spreads across {-1, 0, +1}, increasing Shannon entropy. *Conservation* (C) is the invariant that `Σ states` is preserved modulo the coupling correction — the total "phase momentum" of the system is conserved, and coupling only redistributes it. Strong coupling (high κ) drives η → 0 (coherence) rapidly; weak coupling allows high entropy (diversity). The sync_time metric quantifies the γ-η convergence rate.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: ternary-thermostat
CONCEPTS: ternary, crypto, compute
DEFINITION: Ternary climate control with **PID compensation**, **multi-zone heat transfer**, and **time-based scheduling**. The thermostat output is ternary: `+1` (heating), `0` (idle), `-1` (cooling), enabling precise bang-bang control with hysteresis and PID fine-tuning.
SECTIONS: ternary-thermostat | Why It Matters | How It Works | Bang-Bang Control with Hysteresis | PID Controller | Multi-Zone Thermal Model | Scheduling and Pre-Adjustment | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References | License
MATH: **Complexity:** O(1) per evaluation. | **Complexity:** O(1) per PID update. | **Complexity:** O(Z) per update, where Z = zone count. | The **γ + η = C** invariant: *generation* (γ) is the heating/cooling action producing temperature changes, *entropy* (η) is the temperature differential distribution across zones (high entropy = large
ARCHITECTURE: The **γ + η = C** invariant: *generation* (γ) is the heating/cooling action producing temperature changes, *entropy* (η) is the temperature differential distribution across zones (high entropy = large gradients), and *conservation* (C) is the **first law of thermodynamics** — energy is conserved as heat flows between zones. The multi-zone coupling term `κ(T_{i±1} - T_i)` is the discrete form of Fourier's law of heat conduction, enforcing energy conservation. The cycle_efficiency metric (fraction of time in idle state) measures how well γ and η are balanced: high efficiency means the system is in equilibrium (η ≈ 0), requiring minimal corrective action (γ ≈ 0).
CROSS_REFS: relates-to:ternary relates-to:crypto relates-to:compute

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: ternary-thread-block
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, crypto, compute, math, systems
DEFINITION: Thread block scheduling for **ternary GPU kernels**. Provides abstractions for managing thread block dimensions, warp allocations, streaming multiprocessor (SM) occupancy, and memory alignment — all tuned for kernels operating on three-valued (ternary) data where 16 trits pack into a single `u32`.
SECTIONS: ternary-thread-block | Why It Matters | How It Works | Thread Block Configuration | Validation | Warp Calculation | Occupancy Calculation | Ternary Grid Configuration | Memory Alignment | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes
MATH: **Complexity:** O(1). | **Complexity:** O(1). | The **γ + η = C** invariant: *generation* (γ) is the thread block configuration producing parallel work, *entropy* (η) is the occupancy loss — the gap between achieved warps per SM and the maximum (64
ARCHITECTURE: The **γ + η = C** invariant: *generation* (γ) is the thread block configuration producing parallel work, *entropy* (η) is the occupancy loss — the gap between achieved warps per SM and the maximum (64). *Conservation* (C) is the hardware resource invariant: `blocks_per_sm · threads_per_block ≤ max_threads_per_sm` and `blocks_per_sm · shared_mem_per_block ≤ max_shared_mem_per_sm`. The occupancy calculator explicitly evaluates these conservation constraints to determine how many blocks can coexist, and the ternary pack alignment (16 trits/u32) ensures that thread blocks process data at the natural granularity of the ternary representation.
CROSS_REFS: relates-to:conservation relates-to:ternary relates-to:crypto relates-to:compute relates-to:math relates-to:systems

ARCHITECTURE: The ternary ecosystem rests on a **conservation law**: the sum of agent population fractions γ (choose) + η (avoid) must equal the total active population, with the remainder in the neutral (unknown) state. Transfer learning shifts these fractions by moving weights — a DirectCopy pushes the target toward the source's γ+η distribution, while a WeightedBlend interpolates between two distributions. The domain gap quantifies how far apart those distributions are. `KnowledgeMatrix::cosine_similarity` provides the vector-space view of this relationship: ```

SECTIONS: Ternary Transform | Why It Matters | How It Works | Ternary Wavelet Transform | Ternary Fourier Features | Random Feature Approximation | Ternary Kernels | RBF Similarity and k-NN | Quick Start | API | `TernaryWavelet` | `TernaryFourier`
MATH: $$a_i = \frac{x_{2i} + x_{2i+1}}{2}$$ | $$d_i = \frac{x_{2i} - x_{2i+1}}{2}$$ | **Complexity**: $O(n)$ per level, $O(n \log n)$ for $L = \log_2 n$ levels. | $$c_k = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=0}^{n-1} s_t \cos\left(\frac{2\pi k t}{n}\right), \quad q_k = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=0}^{n-1} s_t \sin\left(\frac{2\pi k t}{n}\right)$$ | **Complexity**: $O(n \cdot K)$ for $K$ frequencies. This is essentially a truncated DFT — equivalent to the Goertzel algorithm for specific bins.
ARCHITECTURE: Within the **γ + η = C** framework: - **γ (gamma)** — the ternary input signal: raw agent states in $\{-1, 0, +1\}$ carrying strategic information - **η (eta)** — the transform: the *mathematical environment* that converts raw ternary signals into spectral, wavelet, or kernel-space representations

DEFINITION: A `#![forbid(unsafe_code)]` library for **ternary tuples** — vectors over the alphabet **{-1, 0, +1}** — with distance metrics, correlation, orthogonality, complement, rotation, and subtuple extraction.
SECTIONS: ternary-tuple | Why It Matters | How It Works | Vector Space Structure | Distance Metrics | Orthogonality | Complement | Rotation | Subtuple | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes
MATH: - **Complexity:** O(n) | - **Complexity:** O(n) | - **Complexity:** O(n) | - **Complexity:** O(n) | The correlation metric connects this crate to the **γ + η = C** conservation law of the ternary ecosystem. When ternary tuples encode agent votes (γ = +1 votes, η = −1 votes), the correlation between 
ARCHITECTURE: The correlation metric connects this crate to the **γ + η = C** conservation law of the ternary ecosystem. When ternary tuples encode agent votes (γ = +1 votes, η = −1 votes), the correlation between two tuples equals the agreement rate scaled by active participation. Two tuples with correlation ρ = 0 represent populations whose γ + η distributions are independent — the neutral state absorbs the difference. The ℤ₃ vector space is also the natural setting for the ternary ballot operations in `warp-ternary-vote` and the tape alphabet of `ternary-turing`.

ARCHITECTURE: The ternary tape alphabet {-1, 0, +1} is the same trit space used throughout the ecosystem. The base-3 encoding of the tape maps directly to the ternary tuple space of `ternary-tuple`, and the balanced-ternary arithmetic connects to the **γ + η = C** conservation law: on a halting tape, the sum of +1 cells minus the sum of −1 cells gives the net ternary bias, which must satisfy conservation constraints when interpreted as an agent population distribution.

ARCHITECTURE: `ternary-types` defines the fundamental value domain of γ + η = C: `{−1, 0, +1} = {η, neutral, γ}`. Every other ternary crate depends on this definition. The type is deliberately minimal — no arithmetic, no collections, no algorithms — because those belong in separate crates. This separation ensures that the type definition is stable and universal. MATH: | Decoding (T observations) | O(T · K²) | O(T · K) | | | Path log-likelihood | O(T · K) | O(1) | | | Path agreement | O(T) | O(1) | | Ternary Viterbi reconstructs hidden γ/η states from observable actions. The observations are the agent's external behavior; the hidden states are the internal ternary decision-making. In the γ + η = C
ARCHITECTURE: Ternary Viterbi reconstructs hidden γ/η states from observable actions. The observations are the agent's external behavior; the hidden states are the internal ternary decision-making. In the γ + η = C framework, Viterbi answers: "given what we saw, what was the agent's γ (constructive) vs η (avoidant) state at each moment?" The sticky model captures the empirical observation that agents don't flip between constructive and avoidant states randomly — they have momentum. The log-probability of the best path measures the agent's internal consistency. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

A velocity field of γ (positive, +1) and η (negative, −1) creates flow patterns: vortices represent stable circular behaviors (self-sust
ARCHITECTURE: Ternary Vortex models the **dynamics** of γ + η = C over space. A velocity field of γ (positive, +1) and η (negative, −1) creates flow patterns: vortices represent stable circular behaviors (self-sustaining competence C), divergence represents agents entering or leaving a strategy (source = new γ, sink = η elimination), and the Biot-Savart law captures long-range influence — a vortex of activity in one region affects agents far away. This connects local ternary decisions to global spatial dynamics. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

DEFINITION: Signal analysis and VU (Volume Unit) metering for **ternary signals** — discrete-valued audio-like signals over the alphabet {-1, 0, +1}. Provides peak, RMS, crest factor, zero-crossing rate, DC offset, stereo balance, and correlation metrics. `#![forbid(unsafe_code)]`.

MATH: - **Complexity:** O(n) | - **Complexity:** O(n) | - **Complexity:** O(n) | The DC offset metric is directly related to the **γ + η = C** conservation law. For a ternary signal, the DC offset equals (count of +1 − count of −1) / n, which is the net ternary bias. When the sign | The RMS metric provides the "active fraction" √((γ + η) / total), quantifying how much of the population is non-neutral.
ARCHITECTURE: The DC offset metric is directly related to the **γ + η = C** conservation law. For a ternary signal, the DC offset equals (count of +1 − count of −1) / n, which is the net ternary bias. When the signal represents an agent population — γ fraction at +1, η fraction at −1 — the DC offset is (γ − η) / (γ + η + neutrals). A DC offset of 0 means γ = η, satisfying the symmetric conservation point where the population is evenly split between choose and avoid. The RMS metric provides the "active fraction" √((γ + η) / total), quantifying how much of the population is non-neutral.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: ternary-walk
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, fleet, storage, systems
DEFINITION: Ternary random walks on the state space **{-1, 0, +1}** — simple symmetric, biased, correlated (persistent), and Lévy flight variants. Includes ensemble statistics, occupation frequencies, return times, and autocorrelation analysis.
SECTIONS: ternary-walk | Why It Matters | How It Works | State Space and Transition Structure | Simple Symmetric Walk | Biased Walk | Correlated (Persistent) Walk | Lévy Flight | Ensemble Statistics | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes
MATH: - **Complexity:** O(1) per step | - **Complexity:** O(1) per step | - **Complexity:** O(1) per step | - **Complexity:** O(1) per step | The walk's stationary distribution connects to the **γ + η = C** conservation law. For a simple symmetric walk, the stationary distribution is (1/3, 1/3, 1/3) — meaning γ = η = neutrals = 1/3 at equil
ARCHITECTURE: The walk's stationary distribution connects to the **γ + η = C** conservation law. For a simple symmetric walk, the stationary distribution is (1/3, 1/3, 1/3) — meaning γ = η = neutrals = 1/3 at equilibrium, perfectly satisfying conservation. Biased walks shift this distribution: a drift toward +1 increases γ at the expense of η, but the sum γ + η always equals the non-neutral fraction. The correlated walk's autocorrelation function ρ(k) = E[X_t · X_{t+k}] decays as exp(−k/τ), providing the timescale over which agent "memory" of past decisions persists. This is the temporal analogue of the conservation law's spatial constraint.

MATH: - Compilation: O(M) compile cycles (dominant cost) | - Test execution: O(M × T) where T = test suite runtime | - Total: O(M × T) — typically minutes for small crates | Test Mutator is the quality-assurance arm of η (eta) in the γ + η = C framework. Where η normally *eliminates* code bugs, Test Mutator *measures* how good your η processes are — it reveals the gaps wh
ARCHITECTURE: Test Mutator is the quality-assurance arm of η (eta) in the γ + η = C framework. Where η normally *eliminates* code bugs, Test Mutator *measures* how good your η processes are — it reveals the gaps where bugs would survive. A high mutation score means your η (testing, review, elimination) is effective. A low score means your C (competence) is fragile — it depends on untested assumptions. By systematically injecting faults and measuring detection, Test Mutator quantifies the gap between perceived and actual test quality. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

DEFINITION: A Rust implementation of the **Thom Isomorphism Theorem** from algebraic topology — the fundamental result relating the cohomology of a base space to the cohomology of its Thom space via a degree shift equal to the bundle rank.

MATH: The Thom isomorphism embodies a structural principle seen across the ternary ecosystem: **contextual shifts preserve information**. Just as the Thom class shifts cohomological degree by *n* without lo
ARCHITECTURE: The Thom isomorphism embodies a structural principle seen across the ternary ecosystem: **contextual shifts preserve information**. Just as the Thom class shifts cohomological degree by *n* without losing rank data, the ternary conservation law γ + η = C preserves total information while redistributing it among the three states. The shift operation in the Thom isomorphism is the topological analogue of the redistribution of agent populations — the Betti numbers (like γ and η fractions) are conserved, only their "location" changes.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: threshold-crypt
CONCEPTS: graph, crypto, compute, storage, protocol, search
DEFINITION: **Threshold cryptography** distributes cryptographic operations across *n* participants such that any *t* of them (the threshold) can jointly perform the operation, but fewer than *t* cannot. This crate implements **Shamir's Secret Sharing (SSS)** over the prime field GF(2³¹ − 1) and a simplified **(t, n) threshold ElGamal** encryption scheme with distributed key generation, partial decryption, and Lagrange-based share combination.
SECTIONS: Threshold Cryptography | Why It Matters | How It Works | Shamir's Secret Sharing | Field Arithmetic | Threshold ElGamal | Share Verification | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References | License
MATH: **Complexity:** Sharing is O(n·t) (evaluate polynomial at n points). Reconstruction is O(t²) (t Lagrange basis computations, each O(t)). | - **Add/Sub:** O(1), with single modular reduction. | - **Multiply:** O(1), using u128 intermediate to prevent overflow. | - **Inverse:** O(log p) via Fermat's little theorem: a⁻¹ = a^{p−2} (mod p), computed with square-and-multiply. | Threshold cryptography provides the distributed trust layer for SuperInstance's security infrastructure. Within the γ + η = C framework, threshold schemes embody **η** (elasticity) — the system tolera
ARCHITECTURE: Threshold cryptography provides the distributed trust layer for SuperInstance's security infrastructure. Within the γ + η = C framework, threshold schemes embody **η** (elasticity) — the system tolerates up to *n−t* node compromises without losing security, bending rather than breaking under attack. The information-theoretic security of SSS grounds **γ** with mathematical certainty. See [ARCHITECTURE.md](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: thrift-binary
CONCEPTS: conservation, ternary, graph, crypto, protocol, math, systems
DEFINITION: Apache Thrift **binary protocol** codec for Rust — a minimal, dependency-free encoder/decoder for Thrift's TBinaryProtocol wire format.
SECTIONS: thrift-binary | Why It Matters | How It Works | Wire Format | Type IDs | Encoding Rules | TValue Algebraic Type | Quick Start | API | Writer Methods | Reader Methods | Architecture Notes
MATH: - Encoding: O(n) where n = total bytes | - Decoding: O(n) — single-pass read | The Thrift binary protocol uses a fixed field structure analogous to the conservation law γ + η = C in the ternary ecosystem. Every struct is a well-formed sequence: `field* T_STOP`. The T_STOP byte p
ARCHITECTURE: The Thrift binary protocol uses a fixed field structure analogous to the conservation law γ + η = C in the ternary ecosystem. Every struct is a well-formed sequence: `field* T_STOP`. The T_STOP byte plays the role of the "neutral" boundary — it terminates accumulation just as the neutral state (0) bounds the active γ and η fractions. The total information content of a Thrift struct is the sum of its field sizes, a conservation invariant maintained across encode/decode.

documents.knowledge_doc:

DEFINITION: A thread-safe **gateway request throttle** with priority-based admission control and per-key rate limiting. Uses mutex-protected state for concurrent access from multiple workers.

MATH: - `try_enter`: O(1) amortized (HashMap lookup + mutex acquire) | - `active_count`: O(1) (mutex acquire + atomic read) | The priority-bypass hierarchy implements a form of the conservation law γ + η = C. Under normal operation, the sum of all per-key admissions equals the global active count — a strict conservation inva | The per-key limit enforces fairness: no single key can consume more than `per_key_limit` slots, ensuring that the γ (admitted) fraction is distributed equitably across tenants.
ARCHITECTURE: The priority-bypass hierarchy implements a form of the conservation law γ + η = C. Under normal operation, the sum of all per-key admissions equals the global active count — a strict conservation invariant. Critical-priority requests temporarily "break" this invariant by exceeding `max_concurrent`, analogous to how an external perturbation can push the active fraction (γ + η) above its equilibrium value. The system's stability depends on critical requests being rare — otherwise the gateway degrades into unrestricted access. The per-key limit enforces fairness: no single key can consume more than `per_key_limit` slots, ensuring that the γ (admitted) fraction is distributed equitably across tenants.
CROSS_REFS: relates-to:conservation relates-to:crypto

ARCHITECTURE: Tile compilation is the policy deployment mechanism in **SuperInstance** for bounded state spaces. Where ternary networks handle continuous state spaces, tile compilation handles discrete strategy spaces with exact lookup. The γ + η = C conservation manifests in the coverage-compression trade-off: more table entries (γ = coverage) require more memory (η = storage overhead). ARCHITECTURE: Tile NEON extends the γ (constructive computation) substrate to ARM devices. Uses `BTreeMap`-indexed series for O(log n) metric lookup and linear-time range  | - **Complexity:** O(log n) for BTreeMap lookup + O(1) amortized append | - **Complexity:** O(n) where n = points in the series (linear scan) | - **Production optimization:** Binary search on sorted timestamps would yield O(log n + k) where k = result size | - **Complexity:** O(n) single-pass, O(b) output where b = number of buckets
ARCHITECTURE: The bucket-based downsampling connects to the **γ + η = C** conservation principle. When downsampling ternary-valued signals (where values represent {-1, 0, +1} votes), the bucket mean equals (γ_count − η_count) / total — the net ternary bias for that time window. Conservation requires that the sum of bucket means weighted by bucket sizes equals the global mean, an invariant preserved by the additive aggregation. The BTreeMap index plays the role of the "species" classifier in the ternary ecosystem: each metric name is a species, and its data points are the agent population measurements over time.

ARCHITECTURE: ALPN negotiation embodies a conservation principle: the selected protocol is a function of the intersection of client and server lists, and exactly one protocol is chosen (or none). This is analogous to the **γ + η = C** constraint — the decision is binary (agree or not), but the outcome depends on the alignment of two independent distributions (client preferences and server capabilities). When the overlap is empty, the "neutral" state (no ALPN) is the fallback, mirroring how the neutral ternary state absorbs population imbalance.

ARCHITECTURE: The lexer's token stream connects to the **γ + η = C** conservation law through its algebraic structure. A program's token sequence is a well-formed string in a formal grammar — every token is either a positive construct (keyword, literal — analogous to γ/choose), a negative construct (operator that transforms — analogous to η/avoid), or structural punctuation (analogous to the neutral state). The total token count is conserved: `keywords + identifiers + operators + literals + punctuation = total_tokens`, a partition constraint that mirrors the ternary conservation invariant.

documents.name: topological-sort
documents.description:
CRATE: topological-sort
CONCEPTS: graph, crypto, compute, protocol, math, systems
DEFINITION: A **topological sort** of a directed acyclic graph (DAG) is a linear ordering of vertices such that for every directed edge (u, v), vertex u appears before vertex v. This crate implements two algorithms — **Kahn's algorithm** (BFS-based) and **DFS-based sort** — with cycle detection, longest-path computation, and ancestor enumeration.
SECTIONS: Topological Sort — DAG Ordering with Cycle Detection | Why

DEFINITION: An **ultrametric space** is a metric space where the **strong triangle inequality** holds: for all points *x, y, z*, `d(x, z) ≤ max(d(x, y), d(y, z))`. CRATE: ultrametric-space

DEFINITION: An **ultrametric space** is a metric space where the **strong triangle inequality** holds: for all points *x, y, z*, `d(x, z) ≤ max(d(x, y), d(y, z))`. This is strictly stronger than the ordinary triangle inequality `d(x, z) ≤ d(x, y) + d(y, z)` and gives ultrametric spaces remarkable geometric properties — every triangle is isosceles, every point in a ball is its center, and balls are both open and closed (clopen).

ARCHITECTURE: In SuperInstance, ultrametric spaces model the **γ (gamma) — correctness** hierarchy of crate relationships: crates at the same level of abstraction have small ultrametric distance, while cross-cutting concerns are far apart. documents.name: value-numbering
documents.description:
CRATE: value-numbering
CONCEPTS: conservation, graph, crypto, compute, search
DEFINITION: **Value numbering** is a compiler optimization that eliminates redundant computations by assigning a unique "number" to each distinct value computed in a program. Two expressions receive the same number if and only if they are *provably equivalent* — enabling the compiler to compute once and reuse everywhere.
SECTIONS: Value Numbering | Why It Matters | How It Works | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes

documents.name: vector-bundle
documents.description:
CRATE: vector-bundle
CONCEPTS: conservation, graph, compute, storage, math, search
DEFINITION: A **vector bundle** is a topological construction that attaches a vector space (the *fiber*) to every point of a base manifold, varying continuously as the base point moves. Formally, a rank-*n* vector bundle `E → M` is a surjection where each fiber `Eₓ = π⁻¹(x)` is an *n*-dimensional vector space, and locally, *E* looks like the product `U × ℝⁿ`.
SECTIONS: Vector Bundle | Why It Matters | How It Works

The OS manages regions of virtual address space, controlling permissions (read, write, execute, user-accessible) and handling page faults to implement demand paging, memory-mapped files, and copy-on-write.

ARCHITECTURE: The ballot structure directly instantiates the **γ + η = C** conservation law: within each warp, `accept + reject + neutral = 32`. The γ fraction (accept/32) and η fraction (reject/32) partition the active population, with the neutral fraction absorbing the remainder. Block consensus extends this: `total_accept + total_reject + total_neutral = total_threads`. The majority threshold (>50% of all threads) ensures that the γ fraction alone can drive a decision, but only when it exceeds the combined η + neutral opposition.

ARCHITECTURE: The conservation law **γ + η = C** is hardware-enforced in this model: each `BallotWord` satisfies `agree + abstain + reject = 32` by construction (they partition the 32 bits). The CRDT merge preserves this at scale: `Σagree + Σabstain + Σreject = 32 × num_warps`. This is a hard invariant that cannot be violated by message loss (reduces all counts proportionally) or duplication (idempotent merge). The γ fraction (`agree/total`) and η fraction (`reject/total`) are the decision-driving components, while abstain plays the neutral role — absorbing the difference exactly as in the ternary agent ecosystem.

ARCHITECTURE: The WASM binary format's section structure follows a strict ordering invariant: Type → Import → Function → Memory → Code. This ordering is analogous to the **γ + η = C** conservation law — sections are additive and must sum to a well-formed module. The operand stack of the WASM stack machine embodies the same principle at the instruction level: every value pushed must eventually be consumed, and the stack depth at each control-flow join must match. The total "information" (value count) is conserved across instruction boundaries.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: wavelet-tree
CONCEPTS: wavelet, graph, crypto, compute, storage, search
DEFINITION: A **wavelet tree** is a succinct data structure that represents a sequence of symbols while supporting **rank**, **select**, **access**, and **quantile** queries in **O(log σ)** time, where *σ* is the alphabet size. It uses just **n ⌈log σ⌉ + o(n log σ)** bits — within a lower-order term of the information-theoretic minimum.
SECTIONS: Wavelet Tree | Why It Matters | How It Works | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References | License
MATH: A **wavelet tree** is a succinct data structure that represents a sequence of symbols while supporting **rank**, **select**, **access**, and **quantile** queries in **O(log σ)** time, where *σ* is the | - **`access(i)`** — Walk from root to leaf, reading one bit per level: **O(log σ)**. | - **`rank(c, i)`** — Count occurrences of symbol *c* in positions `[0, i)`: walk down the tree, using bitmap rank at each level to compute the sub-position. **O(log σ)**. | - **`quantile(k)`** — Find the *k*-th smallest symbol: walk down the tree, using bitmap rank to decide left vs. right. **O(log σ)**. | - **`count_less(c, i)`** — Count symbols < *c* in `[0, i)`: similar to rank but accumulates sizes of skipped subtrees. **O(log σ)**.
ARCHITECTURE: Wavelet trees provide **compact sequence indexing** for SuperInstance's dependency graph: the crate dependency relation is a sequence over an alphabet of crate IDs, and rank/select queries answer "how many crates depend on *X*?" in logarithmic time with near-minimal space. This contributes to the η (efficiency) axis by enabling fast lookups without inflating storage. See [SuperInstance Architecture](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

This means every manifold — no matter how abstractly defined — has a faithful representation as a surface in a finite-dimensional Euclidean space, preserving all topological and smooth structure.

DEFINITION: **Wyhash** is a fast, high-quality non-cryptographic hash function designed by Wang Yi. This crate implements the wyhash v4 algorithm in pure Rust.

documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: wyhash

DEFINITION: **Wyhash** is a fast, high-quality non-cryptographic hash function designed by Wang Yi. It achieves ~1 byte/cycle throughput on modern CPUs — among the fastest hash functions ever benchmarked — while passing all tests in SMHasher3, the industry-standard hash quality test suite. MATH: This uses the hardware `MUL` instruction producing a 128-bit result (via `u128` in Rust), which compiles to the `MUL`/`IMUL` instruction pair on x86-64 and a single `UMULH`/`MUL` pair on ARM64. **O(1) | 1. **Small inputs (≤ 16 bytes):** Read 4-byte or 3-byte chunks, XOR with seeds, and mix. **O(1)**. | 2. **Medium inputs (17–48 bytes):** Read 8-byte words, accumulate with alternating `wymix` calls using `WY0`–`WY3`. **O(n/48)** iterations. | 3. **Large inputs (> 48 bytes):** Process 48-byte blocks (6 × 8 bytes) using three parallel `wymix` accumulators. After the main loop, handle the remaining 16 bytes with a final mix. **O(n/48)** itera | | `wyhash(data, seed)` | Full wyhash with custom seed: **O(n)**, ~1 byte/cycle |
ARCHITECTURE: Wyhash provides the **fast hashing primitive** for SuperInstance's hash tables, cache keys, and content-addressable storage. Its speed (η efficiency axis) makes it the default for non-cryptographic identification, while its quality (γ correctness axis) ensures low collision rates even under adversarial input. See [SuperInstance Architecture](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

documents.name: zariski-topology
documents.description:
CRATE: zariski-topology
CONCEPTS: graph, crypto, math
DEFINITION: The **Zariski topology** is the topology on affine space 𝔸ⁿ (or affine varieties) where the **closed sets** are exactly the vanishing loci of polynomial ideals. A set *V* is Zariski-closed if there exists a set of polynomials *S* such that `V = { x : f(x) = 0 ∀f ∈ S }`. This topology is much coarser than the Euclidean topology — its open sets are enormous (complements of algebraic curves/surfaces).
SECTIONS: Zariski Topology | Why

This topology is much coarser than the Euclidean topology — its open sets are enormous (complements of algebraic curves/surfaces).

MATH: 1. **Grid scan** — Evaluate *p* at *N* equally spaced points. **O(N)** evaluations. | 3. **Bisection refinement** — Narrow each bracketed root to precision ε in **O(log(1/ε))** iterations. | The union of two Zariski-closed sets `V(f) ∪ V(g)` is `V(f·g)` — the vanishing set of the product. In the 1D case, this means merging the root sets and deduplicating. **O(|A| + |B|)** after sorting. | | `Polynomial::evaluate(x)` | Horner-free evaluation: **O(n)** | | | `union(a, b)` | Merge two closed sets: **O(|a| + |b|)** |
ARCHITECTURE: The Zariski topology provides the **geometric language** for SuperInstance's constraint satisfaction system: correctness conditions (γ) are polynomial constraints in the space of parameters, and their vanishing loci define the feasible region. The coarseness of the Zariski topology (few open sets) makes it computationally tractable — you reason about polynomial equations, not metric neighborhoods. See [SuperInstance Architecture](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

documents.name: zero-knowledge
documents.description:
CRATE: zero-knowledge
CONCEPTS: graph, crypto, compute, protocol, math
DEFINITION: A **zero-knowledge proof (ZKP)** is a cryptographic protocol where a prover convinces a verifier of a statement's truth without revealing any information beyond the fact that the statement is true. The protocol satisfies three properties: **completeness** (true statements are accepted), **soundness** (false statements are rejected), and **zero-knowledge** (the verifier learns nothing but the validity of the claim)
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: zero-knowledge
CONCEPTS: graph, crypto, compute, protocol, math
DEFINITION: A **zero-knowledge proof (ZKP)** is a cryptographic protocol where a prover convinces a verifier of a statement's truth without revealing any information beyond the fact that the statement is true. The protocol satisfies three properties: **completeness** (true statements are accepted), **soundness** (false statements are rejected), and **zero-knowledge** (the verifier learns nothing but the validity of the claim). This crate implements the foundational primitives: hash commitments, Schnorr identification (Sigma protocol), and Merkle trees for set membership proofs.
SECTIONS: Zero-Knowledge Proofs | Why It Matters | How It Works | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References | License
MATH: - **Verification:** `verify(value, nonce, C)` recomputes the hash and checks equality. **O(n)** where *n* is input size. | 1. **Build:** Hash each leaf: `hᵢ = H("leaf:" ‖ dataᵢ)`. Pairwise-combine: `parent = H(left ‖ right)`. Tree height: **O(log n)**. | 3. **Proof:** For a given leaf, provide the **authentication path** — the sibling hashes at each level (log *n* hashes). The verifier recomputes the root from the leaf and path: **O(log n)** verificat | | `Commitment::commit(value, nonce)` | SHA-256 hash commitment: **O(n)** | | | `Commitment::verify(value, nonce, C)` | Recompute and compare: **O(n)** |
ARCHITECTURE: Zero-knowledge primitives enable SuperInstance's **privacy-preserving verification** (γ correctness): components can prove compliance with policies (resource limits, safety checks) without revealing their internal state. This is critical for multi-tenant environments where tenants need cryptographic assurance of isolation without transparency into each other's code or data. See [SuperInstance Architecture](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

documents.name: zero-shot-classifier
documents.description:
CRATE: zero-shot-classifier
CONCEPTS: graph, crypto, storage, protocol, math, search
DEFINITION: **Zero-shot classification** assigns text to categories that were never seen during training — no labeled examples required for the target classes. Instead of learning class-specific weights, the classifier maps both text and class labels into a shared **embedding space** and selects the label whose embedding has the highest **cosine similarity** to the text embedding.
SECTIONS: Zero-Shot Classifier

This enables flexible, extensible routing (η efficiency) while maintaining correctness (γ correctness) through high-accuracy embeddings. See [SuperInstance Architecture](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

DEFINITION: The **Riemann zeta function** ζ(*s*) is the most important function in analytic number theory. For Re(*s*) > 1, it's defined by the convergent Dirichlet series:

The **Prime Number Theorem** (that π(*x*) ~ *x*/ln(*x*)) is equivalent to the statement that ζ(*s*) has no zero | For *s* = 2 and *N* = 10,000: error ≤ 10⁻⁴. For *s* = 4 and *N* = 10,000: error ≤ 3 × 10⁻¹³. The convergence rate is **O(N^(1−s))** — faster for larger *s*. | The **Sieve of Eratosthenes** finds all primes up to *N* in **O(N log log N)** time and **O(N)** space. The prime counting function π(*N*) counts primes ≤ *N*. For *N* = 10: π(10) = 4 (primes: 2, 3, 5 | Trial division up to √*n* is **O(√n)**. The 6k±1 optimization skips multiples of 2 and 3, testing only `i = 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19, ...`, reducing constant factors by 3×. | `H(n) = Σ_{k=1}^n 1/k` approximates `ln(n) + γ` (Euler–Mascheroni constant γ ≈ 0.5772) with error **O(1/n)**. The γ + η = C tradeoff mirrors the PNT — you can always find primes (correct configurations), but their density decreases with scale, requiring more work per discovery. SECTIONS: Zipf Distribution | Why It Matters | How It Works | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References | License

Once computed, PMF lookups are **O(1)**. | The CDF is precomputed as a sorted array, enabling **O(log n)** inverse-CDF sampling via binary search. | Each sample is **O(log n)** due to binary search. Generating *m* samples: **O(n + m log n)**. | The log-likelihood is unimodal in *s*, so a grid search over *s ∈ [0.1, 3.0]` with 0.01 resolution (290 evaluations) finds the MLE to two decimal places. Each evaluation is **O(n)** for the normalizat | Given a list of items, count occurrences and sort descending. The result is rank-frequency pairs `(1, c₁), (2, c₂), ...` where `c₁ ≥ c₂ ≥ ...`. ARCHITECTURE: Zipf's law governs SuperInstance's **access patterns**: the most-depended-upon crates (rank 1) receive orders of magnitude more traffic than the long tail. This skew is exploited for caching (η efficiency) — caching the top-20% of crates captures 80%+ of dependency lookups — while the γ correctness axis ensures the long tail is still served correctly, just less frequently. See [SuperInstance Architecture](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).

DEFINITION: **Zipkin** is a distributed tracing system that tracks requests as they propagate through microservices. documents.description:
CRATE: zscore-filter
CONCEPTS: crypto, compute, search
DEFINITION: A **Z-score filter** removes outliers from a data stream by replacing or clipping values whose standardized score `z = (x − μ) / σ` exceeds a threshold. A Z-score of 3 means the value is 3 standard deviations from the mean — a 0.27% probability event under normality. This crate provides four variants: static (global mean), rolling (windowed mean), EMA (exponential moving statistics), and Winsorization (clipping instead of replace
documents.knowledge_doc:
CRATE: zscore-filter
CONCEPTS: crypto, compute, search
DEFINITION: A **Z-score filter** removes outliers from a data stream by replacing or clipping values whose standardized score `z = (x − μ) / σ` exceeds a threshold. A Z-score of 3 means the value is 3 standard deviations from the mean — a 0.27% probability event under normality. This crate provides four variants: static (global mean), rolling (windowed mean), EMA (exponential moving statistics), and Winsorization (clipping instead of replacement).
SECTIONS: Z-Score Filter | Why It Matters | How It Works | Quick Start | API | Architecture Notes | References | License
MATH: Sensor data, financial feeds, server metrics, and ML feature pipelines all suffer from outliers: voltage spikes, tick errors, GC pauses, and measurement noise. These outliers corrupt statistics, train | Two passes: **O(n)** for μ and σ, then **O(n)** for filtering. Total: **O(n)**. Space: **O(n)** for output. | Window recomputation is **O(w)** per point, total **O(nw)**. For *w* = 30, this is real-time for streams up to millions of points/sec. | Use exponential moving average and variance for O(1) updates: | Per-point: **O(1)**. The smoothing parameter α ∈ (0, 1] controls adaptation speed. α = 0.3 means ~3-point effective window; α = 0.1 means ~10-point.
ARCHITECTURE: Z-score filtering is SuperInstance's **anomaly detection primitive** for the observability pipeline: metrics streams are filtered to remove instrumentation artifacts (GC pauses, clock skew) before they trigger alerts or corrupt dashboards. This maintains γ (correctness of monitoring) while η (efficiency) is preserved by the O(n) algorithms. See [SuperInstance Architecture](https://github.com/SuperInstance/SuperInstance/blob/main/ARCHITECTURE.md).
